> - $N_A = 10^{16}\ cm^{-3}$
> - $t_{ox} = 10\ nm = 10^{-6}\ cm$
> - $V_{TN0} = 0.7\ V$ (extracted at $V_{SB} = 0$)
> - $\phi_F = 0.35\ V$
> - $V_{SB} = 1.5\ V$
> - $W/L = 10$, $\mu_n C_{ox} = 50\ \mu A/V^2$
> - $V_{GS} = 1.5\ V$
>
> Find: (a) The new threshold voltage $V_{TN}$.
> (b) The saturation drain current $I_D$ at the new threshold.

**Step 1: Calculate the Body Effect Coefficient $\gamma$**

First, compute $C_{ox}$:
$$C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14}}{10^{-6}} = 3.45 \times 10^{-8}\ F/cm^2$$

Then:
$$\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14} \times 10^{16}}}{3.45 \times 10^{-8}}$$

Numerator inside the square root:
$$2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.036 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{16} = 3.314 \times 10^{-15}$$

$$\sqrt{3.314 \times 10^{-15}} = 5.757 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$\gamma = \frac{5.757 \times 10^{-8}}{3.45 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 1.67\ V^{1/2}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Body Effect Equation**

$$V_{TN} = 0.7 + 1.67 \times \left(\sqrt{0.35 + 1.5} - \sqrt{0.35}\right)$$

$$\sqrt{1.85} \approx 1.360, \quad \sqrt{0.35} \approx 0.592$$

$$V_{TN} = 0.7 + 1.67 \times (1.360 - 0.592) = 0.7 + 1.67 \times 0.768$$

$$V_{TN} = 0.7 + 1.283 \approx \mathbf{1.98\ V}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Drain Current**

The overdrive voltage:
$$V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TN} = 1.5 - 1.98 = -0.48\ V$$

Since $V_{OV} < 0$, the transistor is **OFF** (sub-threshold). This illustrates the critical impact of body bias — at $V_{SB} = 1.5$ V, the device threshold has been pushed above the available gate voltage.

If we instead had $V_{GS} = 2.5$ V:
$$V_{OV} = 2.5 - 1.98 = 0.52\ V$$

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 10 \times (0.52)^2 = 250 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.2704$$

$$I_D = 67.6\ \mu A$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: How I-V Characteristics Shift with $V_{SB}$

| **$V_{SB}$ (V)** | **$V_{TN}$ (V)** | **$V_{GS} - V_{TN}$ (Overdrive)** | **$I_D$ (Saturation)** | **Curve Behavior** |
|------------------|------------------|------------------------------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| 0.0 | 0.70 | 0.80 | Maximum | Curves shifted **leftmost** |
| 0.5 | 1.02 | 0.48 | Reduced | Curves shifted **right** |
| 1.0 | 1.27 | 0.23 | Heavily reduced | Curves shifted **further right** |
| 1.5 | 1.48 | 0.02 | Near zero | Curves nearly **flat** (off-state) |
| 2.0 | 1.66 | -0.16 | Zero (off) | **Transistor fully off** |

> [!NOTE]
> This table is the **expected output** of the KTU lab experiment. The student must record $I_D$ values for at least **three to four** values of $V_{SB}$ and plot two graphs:
> 1. $I_D$ vs $V_{GS}$ for different $V_{SB}$ (transfer characteristics showing rightward shift).
> 2. $V_{TN}$ vs $V_{SB}$ (a curve fitting $\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}$ to extract $\gamma$).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Experimental Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Power Supply VDD] -->|Provides 0-5V| B[Drain Terminal of NMOS]
    C[Variable DC Source VGG] -->|Provides 0-5V Step| D[Gate Terminal of NMOS]
    E[Variable DC Source VBB] -->|Provides 0 to -5V| F[Substrate Body Terminal of NMOS]
    G[Ground 0V] --> H[Source Terminal of NMOS]
    B --> I[Drain Current Meter Ammeter]
    I --> J[Drain Node Voltage]
    D --> K[Gate Node Voltage]
    F --> L[Body Substrate Node]
    H --> M[Source Reference 0V]
    J --> N[Data Acquisition: Record ID, VGS, VSB]
    K --> N
    L --> N
    N --> O[Plot Transfer Characteristics ID vs VGS]
    N --> P[Extract VTN from X-intercept of sqrt ID vs VGS]
    N --> Q[Plot VTN vs VSB Curve to find Gamma]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Cross-Section of NMOS Showing Body Effect

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NMOS_STRUCTURE["NMOS Transistor Cross-Section View"]
        direction TB
        GATE["GATE METAL / POLY"]
        OXIDE["SiO2 GATE OXIDE"]
        CHANNEL["INVERSION LAYER N-type Channel"]
        NPLUS1["n+ Source"]
        NPLUS2["n+ Drain"]
        DEPLET["DEPLETION REGION Widens with VSB increase"]
        PSUB["p-type Substrate BODY Terminal"]
        SBJUNCTION["p-n Junction Reverse Biased by VSB"]
        GATE --- OXIDE
        OXIDE --- CHANNEL
        CHANNEL --- NPLUS1
        CHANNEL --- NPLUS2
        CHANNEL --- DEPLET
        DEPLET --- SBJUNCTION
        SBJUNCTION --- PSUB
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Graph: I-V Curve Family Shift Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph I_SHIFT["Effect of Increasing VSB on I-V Curves"]
        direction LR
        AXIS["VGS Axis Horizontal, ID Axis Vertical"]
        CURVE1["Curve 1: VSB = 0V, VTN = 0.7V, Leftmost"]
        CURVE2["Curve 2: VSB = 0.5V, VTN = 1.02V, Shifted Right"]
        CURVE3["Curve 3: VSB = 1.0V, VTN = 1.27V, Shifted Further"]
        CURVE4["Curve 4: VSB = 1.5V, VTN = 1.48V, Nearly Flat"]
        AXIS --> CURVE1
        AXIS --> CURVE2
        AXIS --> CURVE3
        AXIS --> CURVE4
    end
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Flowchart: Experimental Procedure Steps

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Connect Circuit as per Schematic] --> S2[Step 2: Set VSB = 0V and VDS = 0.1V Linear Region]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Sweep VGS from 0 to 3V in 0.1V steps]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Record ID for each VGS]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Plot sqrt ID vs VGS Linear Fit]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Extract VTN0 from X-intercept]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Repeat for VSB = 0.5, 1.0, 1.5V]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Extract VTN for each VSB]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Plot VTN vs VSB Curve]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Fit to VTN = VTN0 + gamma times sqrt 2phiF + VSB - sqrt 2phiF]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11: Extract Body Effect Coefficient Gamma]
    S11 --> S12[Step 12: Set VGS = 2V and Sweep VDS from 0 to 5V]
    S12 --> S13[Step 13: Record ID vs VDS Output Characteristics for each VSB]
    S13 --> S14[Step 14: Compare Saturation Currents for Different VSB]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define the Body Effect in a MOSFET. What is the significance of the Body Effect Coefficient $\gamma$?**

**Model Answer:**
The Body Effect (or Substrate Bias Effect) refers to the dependence of the threshold voltage $V_{TN}$ of a MOSFET on the voltage $V_{SB}$ applied between the source and the body (substrate) terminal. When $V_{SB} > 0$ (NMOS reverse-biased), the depletion region at the channel-substrate junction widens, requiring a higher gate voltage to form the inversion layer.

The **Body Effect Coefficient $\gamma$** is defined as $\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A}}{C_{ox}}$, and it quantifies the sensitivity of $V_{TN}$ to changes in $V_{SB}$. A larger $\gamma$ implies a stronger body effect. It is measured in $V^{1/2}$ and depends on substrate doping $N_A$ and oxide thickness $t_{ox}$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Why does the threshold voltage of a MOSFET increase when a positive $V_{SB}$ is applied to an NMOS transistor?**

**Model Answer:**
When a positive voltage is applied to the body of an NMOS transistor with respect to the source ($V_{SB} > 0$), the p-n junction between the p-type substrate and the n+ source becomes **reverse-biased**. This causes the depletion region at this junction to widen. The wider depletion region contains more ionized acceptor charges, which must be balanced by additional charge on the gate electrode. Consequently, a higher gate-to-source voltage is required to achieve inversion, leading to an **increase in threshold voltage** $V_{TN}$.

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) – Internal Choice

**Q3. [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the complete expression for the threshold voltage of an NMOS transistor including the body effect. Clearly define every term in your final equation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An NMOS transistor has $V_{TN0} = 0.6\ V$, $\gamma = 0.5\ V^{1/2}$, and $\phi_F = 0.35\ V$. Calculate the new threshold voltage when $V_{SB} = 1.65\ V$. Also find the saturation drain current if $V_{GS} = 2.0\ V$, $W/L = 20$, and $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100\ \mu A/V^2$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The threshold voltage of an NMOS transistor is derived by equating the gate charge to the semiconductor charge at the onset of strong inversion.

1. **Surface Potential at Threshold:** $\phi_S = 2\phi_F$ where $\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$ is the Fermi potential.

2. **Depletion Width:** Solving Poisson's equation in the depletion approximation,
$$W_D = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_{si}(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}{qN_A}}$$

3. **Depletion Charge per Unit Area:**
$$Q_B = -qN_A W_D = -\sqrt{2q\varepsilon_{si}N_A(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}$$

4. **Charge Balance at the Gate:**
$$V_{GS} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{|Q_B|}{C_{ox}}$$

5. **Final Expression:**
$$V_{TN} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2q\varepsilon_{si}N_A(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}$$

Splitting into zero-bias and bias-dependent parts:
$$\boxed{V_{TN} = V_{TN0} + \gamma\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)}$$

where:
- $V_{TN0} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{4q\varepsilon_{si}N_A\phi_F}}{C_{ox}}$ is the threshold voltage with $V_{SB} = 0$.
- $\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2q\varepsilon_{si}N_A}}{C_{ox}}$ is the body effect coefficient.
- $\phi_F$ is the Fermi potential of the p-type substrate.
- $V_{SB}$ is the source-to-body voltage.

**Model Solution (b):**

Given: $V_{TN0} = 0.6\ V$, $\gamma = 0.5\ V^{1/2}$, $\phi_F = 0.35\ V$, $V_{SB} = 1.65\ V$.

$$V_{TN} = 0.6 + 0.5 \times \left(\sqrt{0.35 + 1.65} - \sqrt{0.35}\right)$$

$$\sqrt{2.0} = 1.4142, \quad \sqrt{0.35} = 0.5916$$

$$V_{TN} = 0.6 + 0.5 \times (1.4142 - 0.5916) = 0.6 + 0.5 \times 0.8226$$

$$V_{TN} = 0.6 + 0.4113 = \mathbf{1.011\ V}$$

**Saturation Drain Current:**

The overdrive voltage is:
$$V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TN} = 2.0 - 1.011 = 0.989\ V$$

The saturation drain current is:
$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{OV})^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 20 \times (0.989)^2$$

$$I_D = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.978 = \mathbf{978\ \mu A}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With the help of a neat cross-sectional diagram, explain the physical origin of the body effect in an NMOS transistor. Why does it not affect PMOS transistors in standard bulk CMOS processes in the same way? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An experimental circuit is set up with an NMOS transistor ($V_{TN0} = 0.8\ V$, $\gamma = 0.4\ V^{1/2}$, $\phi_F = 0.4\ V$). The body is biased at $V_{SB} = 2.0\ V$ and the gate is driven such that $I_D = 100\ \mu A$ in saturation with $W/L = 10$, $\mu_n C_{ox} = 50\ \mu A/V^2$. Calculate the required $V_{GS}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

In an NMOS transistor, the channel is formed at the surface of the p-type substrate. A positive gate voltage attracts electrons to form an n-type inversion layer. The body terminal is connected to the p-type substrate, and the source is an n+ region forming a p-n junction with the substrate.

When a voltage $V_{SB} > 0$ is applied between the source and body, this p-n junction becomes **reverse-biased**. The depletion region on the p-side widens. Since the depletion region must be supported by charge on the gate, the gate must now do **additional work** to support both the depletion charge **and** form the inversion layer. This translates to a higher threshold voltage.

In a standard **bulk CMOS process**, PMOS transistors are fabricated inside an **n-well** that is tied to $V_{DD}$. For a PMOS transistor, the source is at the highest potential, and the body is at $V_{DD}$. Therefore, the source-to-body junction is essentially **at zero bias** ($V_{BS} \approx 0$ or slightly positive, but the junction is forward-biased if anything). Hence, the body effect for PMOS in standard configurations is negligible. (In contrast, if the PMOS source is not at $V_{DD}$, then $V_{BS} > 0$ and the body effect is present, but with a **negative** threshold shift since $\gamma$ for PMOS is defined as negative.)

**Model Solution (b):**

Step 1: Compute the new threshold voltage.

$$V_{TN} = V_{TN0} + \gamma\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$$

$$V_{TN} = 0.8 + 0.4\left(\sqrt{0.8 + 2.0} - \sqrt{0.8}\right)$$

$$\sqrt{2.8} \approx 1.6733, \quad \sqrt{0.8} \approx 0.8944$$

$$V_{TN} = 0.8 + 0.4 \times (1.6733 - 0.8944) = 0.8 + 0.4 \times 0.7789$$

$$V_{TN} = 0.8 + 0.3116 = \mathbf{1.1116\ V}$$

Step 2: Use the saturation current equation to solve for $V_{GS}$.

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS} - V_{TN})^2$$

$$100 \times 10^{-6} = \frac{1}{2} \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 10 \times (V_{GS} - 1.1116)^2$$

$$100 \times 10^{-6} = 250 \times 10^{-6} \times (V_{GS} - 1.1116)^2$$

$$(V_{GS} - 1.1116)^2 = \frac{100}{250} = 0.4$$

$$V_{GS} - 1.1116 = \sqrt{0.4} = 0.6325$$

$$V_{GS} = 1.1116 + 0.6325 = \mathbf{1.744\ V}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign of $V_{SB}$:** Students frequently forget that for **NMOS**, $V_{SB} \geq 0$ (reverse bias) is the convention. Using a negative $V_{SB}$ would imply forward biasing the body-source junction, which is **forbidden** in normal operation.
> 2. **Square Root Calculation Errors:** Always carry at least **4 decimal places** in the square root step. Rounding to 2 decimal places early leads to a 5–10% error in the final $V_{TN}$, which the examiner will deduct.
> 3. **Unit Mismatch in $\gamma$:** The body effect coefficient $\gamma$ has units of $V^{1/2}$, **not** V. Students who write $\gamma = 0.5\ V$ will lose marks.
> 4. **Substituting into the wrong equation:** $V_{TN}$ in the I-V equation is the **effective** (body-biased) threshold, not $V_{TN0}$. This is the single most common error.
> 5. **Confusion between $V_T$ (thermal voltage) and $V_{TN}$ (threshold voltage):** These are entirely different quantities. $V_T \approx 26$ mV at room temperature; $V_{TN}$ is typically 0.3 V to 1.5 V.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **Body Effect** describes the dependence of $V_{TN}$ on $V_{SB}$. It is a **second-order** effect that becomes important in modern analog and digital circuits where the source is not tied to the body.
- The **canonical body effect equation** is $V_{TN} = V_{TN0} + \gamma\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$.
- The **body effect coefficient** $\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2q\varepsilon_{si}N_A}}{C_{ox}}$ — increases with substrate doping and decreases with thinner gate oxide.
- A larger $V_{SB}$ produces a **rightward shift** of the transfer characteristics in the $I_D$–$V_{GS}$ plot, indicating a higher effective threshold.
- The **Fermi potential** $\phi_F = V_T \ln(N_A/n_i)$ is typically **0.3 V to 0.4 V** for typical substrate doping.
- The **depletion width** $W_D$ is proportional to $\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}$ — this is the physical origin of the square root in the body effect equation.
- The **drain current** in saturation is $I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS} - V_{TN})^2$ — always use the **body-biased** $V_{TN}$, not $V_{TN0}$.
- In the lab experiment, the **two key graphs** to plot are: (1) $\sqrt{I_D}$ vs $V_{GS}$ for linear extrapolation of $V_{TN}$, and (2) $V_{TN}$ vs $V_{SB}$ to extract $\gamma$.
- The body effect **cannot be ignored** in source followers, cascode amplifiers, and any circuit where the source voltage differs from the body voltage.
- For **PMOS** in standard bulk CMOS, the body is tied to $V_{DD}$ and the source is at or below $V_{DD}$, so $V_{BS} \approx 0$ and the body effect is typically negligible.
- **SPICE simulation tip:** The threshold voltage shift due to body effect is automatically captured in **Level 1, 2, and 3 SPICE models** through the parameters `GAMMA`, `PHI`, and `VTO`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design a CS Amplifier with Resistive Load and Triode Load. Perform Transient and AC Analyses, Plot the Frequency Response, and Obtain the Bandwidth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVL607 - ANALOG IC DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design a CS Amplifier with Resistive Load and Triode Load. Perform Transient and AC Analyses, Plot the Frequency Response, and Obtain the Bandwidth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Common-Source (CS) Amplifier** — A single-stage MOSFET amplifier in which the input signal is applied at the **gate** of a transistor operating in the **saturation region**, the output is taken at the **drain**, and the **source** is tied to a small-signal ground (AC ground). It is the MOSFET counterpart of the BJT Common-Emitter amplifier and is the workhorse building block of analog integrated circuits.

In this experiment we study **two variants of the CS stage** distinguished by the type of load connected at the drain node:

| Load Type | Description | Operating Region of Load |
|---|---|---|
| **Resistive Load** | A passive poly-resistor $R_D$ tied between $V_{DD}$ and the drain | Always linear (ohmic) |
| **Triode Load (Active Load)** | An NMOS transistor with $V_{GS,load} > V_{TH}$ but $V_{DS,load} < V_{GS,load} - V_{TH}$ | Operates in the **triode (linear) region**, behaving as a small-signal resistance $R_{on}$ |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water-Pipe Analogy**
> - The **transistor (M1)** is like a *faucet* that you control with the gate handle. The harder you twist (larger $V_{GS}$), the more water (current) flows.
> - The **resistive load** is a *fixed narrow pipe* — it always resists flow by the same amount, no matter what.
> - The **triode load** is a *smart adjustable pipe* — it keeps itself wide-open enough to let most water through, but its effective narrowness depends on the pressure at its own end.
> - The **gain** is how much a small twist of the faucet changes the water pressure at the output — the wider the output pipe (high $R_D$ or low $R_{on}$), the smaller the change.
> - The **bandwidth** is how fast you can wiggle the faucet handle before the output stops keeping up — limited by the capacitance of the output bucket.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Threshold voltage (typical 180 nm CMOS):** $V_{THn} \approx \mathbf{0.5\ V}$
- **Process transconductance parameter (NMOS, 180 nm):** $k_n' = \mu_n C_{ox} \approx \mathbf{170\ \mu A/V^2}$
- **Supply rail:** $V_{DD} = \mathbf{1.8\ V}$ (for 180 nm technology)
- **Channel-length modulation coefficient:** $\lambda \approx \mathbf{0.1\ V^{-1}}$
- **Unit gain target for lab exercise:** $\vert A_v \vert \ge \mathbf{5\ V/V}$ ($\approx 14\ \text{dB}$)
- **Unit-gain bandwidth target:** $f_T \ge \mathbf{100\ MHz}$

## 1.4 Topology at a Glance

The CS stage can be redrawn for both load options as a *black box* with three terminals — **Gate (input)**, **Drain (output)**, and **Source (AC ground)** — but the internal load is what changes the DC bias point, the small-signal gain, and the high-frequency response.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The design of any CS amplifier proceeds in **three layers**: (i) DC bias, (ii) small-signal AC analysis, and (iii) frequency/time-domain simulation. We treat each layer separately because the three responses are **superposition-orthogonal** in a linear circuit.

### 2.1.1 DC Bias Layer (Operating Point)

The transistor **M1** must be biased in **saturation** so that the small-signal model (which assumes a constant $g_m$ and $r_o$) is valid.

**Step 1** — Choose overdrive voltage $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ (typical $0.15 - 0.3\ \text{V}$ for low-power design).

**Step 2** — Choose DC drain current $I_D$ (typical $50 - 200\ \mu\text{A}$ for sub-mA analog blocks).

**Step 3** — Compute aspect ratio:

$$\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 = \frac{2 I_D}{k_n' \, V_{OV}^2}$$

**Step 4** — For **resistive load**, choose $R_D$ so that the DC drain voltage $V_{OUT,DC} = V_{DD} - I_D R_D$ sits roughly at the midpoint of the available swing (i.e., $V_{OUT,DC} \approx V_{DD}/2$) to maximize symmetric output swing.

**Step 5** — For **triode load**, choose the load transistor's $V_{GS,load}$ such that the resulting $R_{on}$ is large enough to give the desired gain yet small enough to keep M1 in saturation.

### 2.1.2 Small-Signal AC Layer

The MOSFET is replaced by its hybrid-π small-signal model: a voltage-controlled current source $g_m v_{gs}$ in parallel with an output resistance $r_o$. The load element is replaced by its incremental resistance. KCL at the output node yields the gain.

### 2.1.3 Frequency / Transient Layer

The parasitic capacitances $C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$, and $C_{db}$ of M1 (and the load device) introduce poles. The **dominant pole** is almost always at the output node because of the Miller-multiplied $C_{gd}$ that the input node *sees*. The AC simulation in the EDA tool solves the linearized nodal equations in the phasor (frequency) domain, automatically computing the -3 dB bandwidth.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table collects **every equation** a student is expected to reproduce in the KTU end-semester viva or written exam for this experiment. The vertical pipe symbol is replaced by `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Drain current (saturation) | $I_D = \tfrac{1}{2} k_n' (W/L) \, V_{OV}^2$ | $V_{DS} \ge V_{OV}$; in Amperes |
| 2 | Drain current (triode load) | $I_D = k_n' (W/L)_{L} \left[ (V_{GS,L}-V_{TH})V_{DS,L} - \tfrac{V_{DS,L}^2}{2} \right]$ | $V_{DS,L} < V_{OV,L}$ |
| 3 | Transconductance | $g_m = k_n' (W/L) V_{OV} = \sqrt{2 k_n' (W/L) I_D} = \tfrac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}}$ | S (Siemens) |
| 4 | Output resistance of M1 | $r_{o1} = \tfrac{1}{\lambda I_D}$ | Ω |
| 5 | Triode resistance | $R_{on} = \tfrac{1}{k_n'(W/L)_L (V_{GS,L}-V_{TH}-V_{DS,L})}$ | Ω |
| 6 | Voltage gain, **resistive load** | $A_v = -g_{m1} \,(R_D \,\vert\vert\, r_{o1})$ | V/V |
| 7 | Voltage gain, **triode load** | $A_v = -g_{m1} \,(R_{on} \,\vert\vert\, r_{o1} \,\vert\vert\, r_{o2})$ | V/V |
| 8 | Dominant (output) pole | $p_1 = \tfrac{1}{R_{out} \, C_{out}}$, with $C_{out} = C_{gd1} + C_{db1} + C_L$ | rad/s |
| 9 | -3 dB bandwidth | $f_{-3\text{dB}} = \tfrac{p_1}{2\pi}$ | Hz |
| 10 | Unity-gain frequency | $f_T \approx g_{m1} / (2\pi C_{gd1})$ | Hz |
| 11 | Power dissipation | $P_{diss} = V_{DD} \cdot I_D$ | W |
| 12 | DC drain voltage (resistive) | $V_{OUT,DC} = V_{DD} - I_D R_D$ | V |

> [!NOTE]
> The negative sign in the gain expression denotes the **180° phase inversion** between input and output — a hallmark of CS topology and a frequent viva question.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The CS amplifier is the **fundamental gain stage** in:

- **Op-amp input/driver stages** (folded-cascode, telescopic, two-stage Miller op-amps)
- **RF low-noise amplifiers (LNAs)** in transceivers
- **Discrete-time sample-and-hold amplifiers** in ADC front-ends
- **Active-pixel sensors (image sensors)**
- **High-input-impedance buffers** in sensor read-out ASICs

The choice between a **resistive** and a **triode load** directly trades off:

- **Gain vs. silicon area** — triode load is *transistor-only* (no poly resistor), saving die area
- **DC drop vs. headroom** — resistive load consumes a large IR drop; triode load can be biased to consume less headroom
- **Bandwidth vs. gain** — the smaller $R_{on}$ of a triode load typically yields **lower gain** but a **wider bandwidth** (because the dominant pole $p_1 \propto 1/R_{out} C_{out}$ is pushed to a higher frequency)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Design Specifications (Worked Numerical Example)

To make the derivations concrete, we fix a typical KTU lab specification (180 nm CMOS, $V_{DD} = 1.8\ \text{V}$):

$$I_D = 100\ \mu\text{A}, \quad V_{OV} = 0.2\ \text{V}, \quad V_{THn} = 0.5\ \text{V}, \quad k_n' = 170\ \mu\text{A/V}^2, \quad \lambda = 0.1\ \text{V}^{-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> These same numbers appear in the **Part B** model solution of §5, so the student can cross-check every result.

## 3.2 Derivation A — DC Bias for Resistive Load

**Step A1** — Compute aspect ratio of M1:

$$\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 = \frac{2 I_D}{k_n' V_{OV}^2} = \frac{2 \times 100\ \mu\text{A}}{170\ \mu\text{A/V}^2 \times (0.2\ \text{V})^2} = \frac{200}{6.8} \approx 29.4$$

$$\Longrightarrow \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 \approx 30 \quad (\text{round to nearest integer})$$

**Step A2** — Choose $R_D$ so that $V_{OUT,DC} = V_{DD}/2$ for maximum swing:

$$R_D = \frac{V_{DD} - V_{OUT,DC}}{I_D} = \frac{1.8 - 0.9}{100\ \mu\text{A}} = \frac{0.9}{100\ \mu} = 9\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step A3** — Verify M1 is in saturation. $V_{DS,1} = V_{OUT,DC} - 0 = 0.9\ \text{V}$ and $V_{OV} = 0.2\ \text{V}$. Since $0.9\ \text{V} \gg 0.2\ \text{V}$, M1 is **deep in saturation** ✓.

**Step A4** — Output resistance of M1:

$$r_{o1} = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.1 \times 100\ \mu\text{A}} = 100\ \text{k}\Omega$$

## 3.3 Derivation B — Small-Signal Gain for Resistive Load

**Step B1** — Small-signal transconductance:

$$g_{m1} = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}} = \frac{2 \times 100\ \mu\text{A}}{0.2\ \text{V}} = 1\ \text{mA/V} = 1\ \text{mS}$$

**Step B2** — Apply KCL at the output (drain) node of the small-signal equivalent circuit:

$$-g_{m1} v_{gs} = \frac{v_{out}}{R_D} + \frac{v_{out}}{r_{o1}}$$

**Step B3** — Solve for the gain $A_v = v_{out}/v_{in}$ (where $v_{in} = v_{gs}$ for a directly-coupled CS stage):

$$A_v = -g_{m1} \left( R_D \,\vert\vert\, r_{o1} \right) = -\frac{g_{m1} R_D r_{o1}}{R_D + r_{o1}}$$

**Step B4** — Substitute numerical values:

$$A_v = -1\ \text{mS} \times (9\ \text{k}\Omega \,\vert\vert\, 100\ \text{k}\Omega) = -1\ \text{mS} \times 8.257\ \text{k}\Omega \approx -8.26\ \text{V/V}$$

$$A_v \text{ (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(8.26) \approx 18.3\ \text{dB}$$

## 3.4 Derivation C — DC Bias for Triode Load

**Step C1** — Use a separate NMOS load M2 biased by $V_{BIAS,L} = 1.2\ \text{V}$ at its gate. The source of M2 is tied to $V_{DD}$, drain to the output node (which is the drain of M1). The DC drain voltage of M2 is therefore $V_{OUT,DC}$ and its $V_{DS,2} = V_{DD} - V_{OUT,DC}$.

**Step C2** — For mid-rail output ($V_{OUT,DC} = 0.9\ \text{V}$):

$$V_{DS,2} = 1.8 - 0.9 = 0.9\ \text{V}, \quad V_{GS,2} - V_{TH} = 1.2 - 0.5 = 0.7\ \text{V}$$

Since $V_{DS,2} = 0.9\ \text{V} > 0.7\ \text{V}$, M2 is **not** in triode — it is in saturation. To force triode operation, we need $V_{DS,2} < V_{GS,2} - V_{TH}$, i.e., $V_{BIAS,L}$ must be **lower** or output must sit **higher**.

**Step C3** — Re-target: pick $V_{BIAS,L} = 1.0\ \text{V}$, then $V_{GS,2} - V_{TH} = 0.5\ \text{V}$. Choose $(W/L)_2 = 5$ and solve for the bias current.

KCL at the output in DC:

$$\tfrac{1}{2} k_n' \left(\tfrac{W}{L}\right)_1 V_{OV,1}^2 = k_n' \left(\tfrac{W}{L}\right)_2 \left[ (V_{GS,2}-V_{TH}) V_{DS,2} - \tfrac{V_{DS,2}^2}{2} \right]$$

With $V_{OV,1} = 0.2\ \text{V}$, $(W/L)_1 = 30$:

$$I_D = 100\ \mu\text{A} = 170\ \mu \times 5 \times \left[ 0.5 \cdot V_{DS,2} - \tfrac{V_{DS,2}^2}{2} \right]$$

$$\frac{100}{850} = 0.5 V_{DS,2} - 0.5 V_{DS,2}^2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad V_{DS,2}^2 - V_{DS,2} + 0.235 = 0$$

$$V_{DS,2} = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 0.941}}{2} = \frac{1 \pm 0.243}{2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad V_{DS,2} \approx 0.378\ \text{V (valid root)}$$

Since $0.378\ \text{V} < 0.5\ \text{V}$, M2 is indeed in triode ✓. The DC output is:

$$V_{OUT,DC} = V_{DD} - V_{DS,2} = 1.8 - 0.378 = 1.422\ \text{V}$$

## 3.5 Derivation D — Small-Signal Gain for Triode Load

**Step D1** — Triode resistance of M2:

$$R_{on} = \frac{1}{k_n' (W/L)_2 (V_{GS,2} - V_{TH} - V_{DS,2})} = \frac{1}{170\ \mu \times 5 \times (0.5 - 0.378)} = \frac{1}{170\ \mu \times 5 \times 0.122}$$

$$R_{on} = \frac{1}{103.7\ \mu\text{S}} \approx 9.64\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step D2** — Output resistance of M2 (in triode, $r_{o2}$ still exists but is large):

$$r_{o2} = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.1 \times 100\ \mu} = 100\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step D3** — Total small-signal load at the output node:

$$R_{out} = R_{on} \,\vert\vert\, r_{o1} \,\vert\vert\, r_{o2} = 9.64\ \text{k} \,\vert\vert\, 100\ \text{k} \,\vert\vert\, 100\ \text{k}$$

$$\frac{1}{R_{out}} = \frac{1}{9.64\text{k}} + \frac{1}{100\text{k}} + \frac{1}{100\text{k}} = 0.1037\ \text{mS} + 0.01\ \text{mS} + 0.01\ \text{mS} = 0.1237\ \text{mS}$$

$$R_{out} \approx 8.08\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step D4** — Voltage gain:

$$A_v = -g_{m1} R_{out} = -1\ \text{mS} \times 8.08\ \text{k}\Omega = -8.08\ \text{V/V} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 18.1\ \text{dB}$$

## 3.6 Derivation E — Frequency Response and Bandwidth

The parasitic capacitances at the output node, assuming $C_{gd1} = C_{db1} = 20\ \text{fF}$ and load capacitance $C_L = 50\ \text{fF}$:

$$C_{out} = C_{gd1} + C_{db1} + C_L = 20 + 20 + 50 = 90\ \text{fF}$$

The **dominant pole frequency**:

$$f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{out} C_{out}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 8.08\ \text{k}\Omega \times 90\ \text{fF}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 7.27 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$f_p = \frac{1}{4.57 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 219\ \text{MHz}$$

For a **single-pole** system, $f_{-3\text{dB}} = f_p$. The AC simulation in the EDA tool will directly report this -3 dB point from the Bode plot.

> [!TIP]
> **Why AC analysis gives the bandwidth automatically:** the simulator linearises the circuit at the DC operating point, computes the small-signal transfer function $H(s) = V_{out}(s)/V_{in}(s)$ as a rational function of $s = j\omega$, and evaluates $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ at each frequency. The bandwidth is the frequency at which $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ falls to $1/\sqrt{2}$ of its low-frequency value — i.e., **-3 dB**.

## 3.7 Transient Analysis — Expected Waveform

For transient analysis, apply a sinusoidal input of small amplitude (e.g., 10 mV peak, 1 MHz) at the gate:

- The **drain output** should be a sinusoid of amplitude $10\ \text{mV} \times \vert A_v \vert \approx 80\ \text{mV}$ peak
- The output should be **180° out of phase** with the input
- A square-wave input (1 MHz, 50% duty, 10 mV peak) should yield a correspondingly amplified, inverted, slightly rounded square wave at the drain (corner rounding caused by the dominant pole)

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Comparison

| Performance Metric | Resistive Load | Triode Load (this design) |
|---|---|---|
| DC output voltage | $0.90\ \text{V}$ | $1.42\ \text{V}$ |
| Small-signal gain $\vert A_v \vert$ | $8.26\ \text{V/V}\ (18.3\ \text{dB})$ | $8.08\ \text{V/V}\ (18.1\ \text{dB})$ |
| Dominant pole $f_{-3\text{dB}}$ | $233\ \text{MHz}$ (with $R_{out} = 8.26\ \text{k}\Omega$) | $219\ \text{MHz}$ (with $R_{out} = 8.08\ \text{k}\Omega$) |
| Silicon area | **Large** (poly resistor dominates) | **Smaller** (transistor only) |
| Linearity | High (linear $R_D$) | Moderate ($R_{on}$ is signal-dependent) |
| PSRR | Better (resistor is a passive) | Worse (active load amplifies supply noise) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Resistive Load CS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Vin AC source] --> capA[Cin coupling cap]
    capA --> gateA[Gate of M1 NMOS]
    gateA --> biasA[DC bias VGS]
    gateA --> drainA[Drain of M1]
    drainA --> rdA[Resistor RD]
    rdA --> vddA[VDD supply]
    drainA --> coutA[Cout coupling cap]
    coutA --> routA[Vout to next stage]
    srcB[Source of M1] --> gndA[Ground]

    classDef trans fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7,color:#0b5ed7;
    classDef pass fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#0f5132;
    classDef supply fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#664d03,color:#664d03;
    class M1 trans;
    class RD pass;
    class VDD,gndA supply;
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Triode Load CS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inB[Vin input] --> ginB[Gate of M1 driver]
    ginB --> drnB[Drain of M1 shared output node]
    drnB --> m2blk[M2 NMOS load]
    m2blk --> vddB[VDD supply at source of M2]
    ginB --> biaB[VBiasL applied to gate of M2]
    drnB --> outB[Vout to next stage]
    srcM1[Source of M1] --> gndB[Ground]

    m2blk -. "Triode region VDS less than VOV" .- noteB[Ron = 1 divided by kn prime times W by L times VGS minus VTH minus VDS]

    classDef driver fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7,color:#0b5ed7;
    classDef load fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029,color:#842029;
    classDef ground fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#41464b,color:#41464b;
    class M1 driver;
    class M2 load;
    class gndB ground;
```

## 4.3 Signal-Flow Diagram — DC → AC → Transient → AC Sweep

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start EDA session Cadence Virtuoso] --> libA[Create library and cell view schematic]
    libA --> symA[Place NMOS M1 and load element RD or M2]
    symA --> biasA[Apply VDD and DC bias voltages]
    biasA --> opA[DC operating point analysis]
    opA --> checkA{Is M1 in saturation? VDS greater than VOV}
    checkA -- No --> adjustA[Re-size W by L of M1 or change RD]
    adjustA --> biasA
    checkA -- Yes --> tranA[Transient analysis apply 1 MHz sine input]
    tranA --> wfA[Plot input vs output waveform confirm 180 degree phase inversion]
    wfA --> acA[AC analysis sweep 1 Hz to 10 GHz with 1 mV AC magnitude]
    acA --> bdA[Plot magnitude in dB vs frequency Bode plot]
    bdA --> f3A[Find -3 dB frequency f-3dB using marker]
    f3A --> repA[Tabulate DC op point gain bandwidth and compare both topologies]
    repA --> endA[End of experiment]

    classDef step fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#0f5132;
    classDef decision fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#664d03,color:#664d03;
    classDef finalstep fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7,color:#0b5ed7;
    class opA,tranA,acA,bdA,repA step;
    class checkA decision;
    class endA finalstep;
```

## 4.4 Small-Signal Hybrid-π Model (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    gsigA[Vin] --> gsigA1[Gate node]
    gsigA1 --> capgsA[Cgs to source]
    gsigA1 --> capgdA[Cgd to drain]
    gsigA1 --> gmvA[Voltage controlled current source gm times vgs]
    gmvA --> drnodeA[Drain output node]
    drnodeA --> roA[ro1 output resistance of M1]
    drnodeA --> rloadA[RD or Ron]
    drnodeA --> voutA[Vout]
    capgdA --> drnodeA
    roA --> srcsigA[Source AC ground]
    rloadA --> srcsigA

    classDef srcctrl fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7,color:#0b5ed7;
    classDef res fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029,color:#842029;
    classDef cap fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#0f5132;
    class gmvA srcctrl;
    class roA,rloadA res;
    class capgsA,capgdA cap;
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagrams.** Each block corresponds to a real element on the schematic canvas. The **M1 transistor** is replaced (in AC) by $g_m v_{gs}$ and $r_{o1}$; the **load** is replaced by its incremental resistance ($R_D$ or $R_{on}$). The **parasitic capacitors** $C_{gs}$ and $C_{gd}$ model the high-frequency roll-off that limits the bandwidth.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
**Define the small-signal transconductance $g_m$ of a MOSFET in saturation. Why is it called "small-signal"?**

**Model Answer:**
$g_m$ is defined as the partial derivative of the drain current with respect to the gate-to-source voltage, evaluated at the DC operating point:

$$g_m = \left.\frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}}\right|_{V_{DS}=\text{const}} = k_n' \frac{W}{L} V_{OV} = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}}$$

It is called "small-signal" because the formula is valid **only for infinitesimal perturbations** $v_{gs}$ around the DC bias point $V_{GS,DC}$. For large signals, the square-law $I_D$–$V_{GS}$ relation is no longer linear, and $g_m$ itself becomes a function of the signal amplitude.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
**State any two differences between the triode and saturation regions of operation of an NMOS transistor.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Triode Region | Saturation Region |
|---|---|---|
| Condition | $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ **and** $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ | $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ **and** $V_{DS} \ge V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ |
| $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ behaviour | $I_D$ increases **linearly** with $V_{DS}$ | $I_D$ is (almost) **constant** with $V_{DS}$ |
| Use as load | Active resistor $R_{on}$ | Current source (active load) |
| Channel | Continuous, pinched at drain end | Pinched off, creating a depletion region |

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently **omit the verification that M1 is in saturation** — full marks for the DC design are only awarded if this check is explicitly shown.
> - Many candidates confuse the **triode resistance formula** with the saturation $r_o$ — they are **not the same** ($R_{on}$ depends on $V_{GS}$ and $V_{DS}$, whereas $r_o$ depends on $I_D$ and $\lambda$).
> - In AC analysis, students forget to **convert the gain to dB** — KTU examiners expect both V/V and dB forms.
> - The bandwidth is the **-3 dB frequency** of the **magnitude** plot, not the -3 dB point on a phase plot. Read the marker on the Bode magnitude curve, not the phase curve.
> - Always mention **Miller multiplication** of $C_{gd}$ when explaining the dominant pole — at least one mark is reserved for this phrase.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** *(CS Amplifier with Resistive Load)*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** For an NMOS common-source amplifier with a resistive load $R_D$, derive expressions for **(i)** the small-signal voltage gain $A_v$ and **(ii)** the -3 dB bandwidth $f_{-3\text{dB}}$. Assume the input is directly coupled (no coupling capacitor) and the MOSFET has a small-signal output resistance $r_o$.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a CS amplifier with a resistive load in 180 nm CMOS technology for the following specifications: $V_{DD} = 1.8\ \text{V}$, $I_D = 100\ \mu\text{A}$, $V_{OV} = 0.2\ \text{V}$, $k_n' = 170\ \mu\text{A/V}^2$, $\lambda = 0.1\ \text{V}^{-1}$, $C_{gd} = C_{db} = 20\ \text{fF}$, $C_L = 50\ \text{fF}$. Determine $(W/L)_1$, $R_D$, $g_{m1}$, $r_{o1}$, $A_v$ (in V/V and dB), and the -3 dB bandwidth.  **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation**

**Step 1.** The small-signal equivalent circuit consists of a VCCS $g_m v_{gs}$ in parallel with $r_o$ between drain and source, with $R_D$ from drain to $V_{DD}$ (AC ground). $C_{gd}$ and $C_{db}$ shunt the output node to ground.

**Step 2.** KCL at the drain (output) node:

$$-g_m v_{gs} = \frac{v_{out}}{R_D} + \frac{v_{out}}{r_o} + v_{out} \cdot s C_{out}$$

where $C_{out} = C_{gd} + C_{db} + C_L$.

**Step 3.** Transfer function:

$$A_v(s) = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_m (R_D \,\vert\vert\, r_o) \cdot \frac{1}{1 + s R_{out} C_{out}}$$

with $R_{out} = R_D \,\vert\vert\, r_o$.

**Step 4.** Low-frequency gain: $A_{v0} = -g_m (R_D \,\vert\vert\, r_o)$.

**Step 5.** Dominant pole: $p_1 = -1/(R_{out} C_{out})$. Hence:

$$f_{-3\text{dB}} = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_{out} C_{out}}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Design**

Using the formulas derived above and the specifications:

**Step 1.** $(W/L)_1 = \dfrac{2 I_D}{k_n' V_{OV}^2} = \dfrac{2 \times 100\ \mu}{170\ \mu \times 0.04} \approx 29.4 \Rightarrow \mathbf{30}$

**Step 2.** $R_D = (V_{DD} - V_{DD}/2)/I_D = 0.9/100\ \mu = \mathbf{9\ k\Omega}$

**Step 3.** $g_{m1} = 2 I_D / V_{OV} = 200\ \mu / 0.2 = \mathbf{1\ mS}$

**Step 4.** $r_{o1} = 1/(\lambda I_D) = 1/(0.1 \times 100\ \mu) = \mathbf{100\ k\Omega}$

**Step 5.** $R_{out} = 9\text{k} \,\vert\vert\, 100\text{k} = 8.257\ \text{k}\Omega$

**Step 6.** $A_v = -g_{m1} R_{out} = -1\ \text{m} \times 8.257\ \text{k} = \mathbf{-8.26\ V/V} = \mathbf{18.3\ dB}$

**Step 7.** $C_{out} = 20 + 20 + 50 = 90\ \text{fF}$

**Step 8.** $f_{-3\text{dB}} = 1/(2\pi \times 8.257\text{k} \times 90\text{f}) = \mathbf{214\ MHz}$

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(CS Amplifier with Triode Load)*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Module 2 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** An NMOS common-source amplifier uses an NMOS transistor M2 in the **triode region** as the drain load. Derive the small-signal voltage gain $A_v$ in terms of $g_{m1}$, the triode resistance $R_{on}$ of M2, and the output resistances $r_{o1}$, $r_{o2}$ of M1 and M2.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the resistive-load and triode-load CS amplifiers in terms of **(i)** silicon area, **(ii)** DC output voltage headroom, **(iii)** voltage gain, **(iv)** bandwidth, and **(v)** power-supply rejection ratio (PSRR). Use a comparison table.  **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Derivation**

**Step 1.** Replace M2 (triode) by its small-signal triode resistance $R_{on}$ (a passive resistor). Replace M1 by its hybrid-π model. The output node sees three parallel resistances: $R_{on}$, $r_{o1}$, and $r_{o2}$.

**Step 2.** Triode resistance:

$$R_{on} = \frac{1}{k_n' (W/L)_2 \left[ (V_{GS,2} - V_{TH}) - V_{DS,2} \right]}$$

**Step 3.** KCL at the output (drain of M1 = drain of M2):

$$-g_{m1} v_{gs} = v_{out} \left( \frac{1}{R_{on}} + \frac{1}{r_{o1}} + \frac{1}{r_{o2}} \right)$$

**Step 4.** Solve:

$$A_v = -g_{m1} \left( R_{on} \,\vert\vert\, r_{o1} \,\vert\vert\, r_{o2} \right)$$

**Part (b) — Comparison Table**

| # | Parameter | Resistive Load | Triode Load | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| i | Silicon area | Large (poly resistor) | Small (transistor only) | **Triode** |
| ii | DC headroom (for $V_{DD}/2$ bias) | $V_{OUT,DC} = V_{DD} - I_D R_D$ — easy to set | $V_{OUT,DC}$ depends on $V_{BIAS,L}$ — sensitive | **Resistive** |
| iii | Voltage gain | $g_m (R_D \,\vert\vert\, r_{o1})$ — typically 10–20 dB | $g_m (R_{on} \,\vert\vert\, r_{o1} \,\vert\vert\, r_{o2})$ — usually **lower** because $R_{on}$ is small | **Resistive** |
| iv | Bandwidth | Limited by $R_D C_{out}$ — moderate | **Higher** because smaller $R_{on}$ pushes $f_{-3\text{dB}}$ up | **Triode** |
| v | PSRR (supply noise to output) | Excellent (passive element rejects supply noise) | Poor (active load amplifies $V_{DD}$ ripple) | **Resistive** |

**Conclusion.** The choice of load is a **trade-off**: resistive loads are preferred for high-gain, high-PSRR, low-noise applications; triode loads are preferred for area-efficient, high-bandwidth stages (e.g., buffers driving large capacitive loads).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2 Experiment 2**

- [ ] The CS amplifier is a **phase-inverting** voltage amplifier with input at the **gate** and output at the **drain**.
- [ ] **DC design rule:** M1 must be in **saturation** ($V_{DS} \ge V_{OV}$); verify explicitly after choosing $R_D$ or triode bias.
- [ ] **Low-frequency gain:** $\vert A_v \vert = g_{m1} \cdot R_{out}$ where $R_{out}$ is the parallel combination of the load and the output resistances of all transistors.
- [ ] **Gain formula — resistive load:** $A_v = -g_{m1} (R_D \,\vert\vert\, r_{o1})$.
- [ ] **Gain formula — triode load:** $A_v = -g_{m1} (R_{on} \,\vert\vert\, r_{o1} \,\vert\vert\, r_{o2})$.
- [ ] **Triode resistance:** $R_{on} = 1/[k_n' (W/L)_2 (V_{GS,2} - V_{TH} - V_{DS,2})]$ — note the **$V_{DS,2}$** dependence (signal-dependent!).
- [ ] **Dominant pole:** at the output node, $p_1 = 1/(R_{out} C_{out})$; bandwidth $f_{-3\text{dB}} = p_1 / (2\pi)$.
- [ ] **Miller effect** multiplies $C_{gd}$ by $(1 + g_m R_D)$ at the input side — important when cascading stages.
- [ ] **AC analysis** in the EDA tool yields a **Bode magnitude plot**; the -3 dB point on this plot **is** the bandwidth.
- [ ] **Transient analysis** uses a time-domain input (sine, square, pulse); output is **180° out of phase** and **amplified** by $\vert A_v \vert$.
- [ ] **Resistive load** = high gain, high PSRR, large area. **Triode load** = lower gain, low PSRR, smaller area, higher bandwidth.
- [ ] **180 nm CMOS standard:** $V_{DD} = 1.8\ \text{V}$, $V_{THn} \approx 0.5\ \text{V}$, $k_n' \approx 170\ \mu\text{A/V}^2$, $\lambda \approx 0.1\ \text{V}^{-1}$.
- [ ] **Viva favourite:** "Why is the output phase inverted?" — because increasing $V_{GS}$ increases $I_D$, which **drops** more voltage across the load, **lowering** $V_{OUT}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design a CS Amplifier with Diode-Connected Load and Constant Current Source Load. Perform Transient and AC Analysis, Plot the Frequency Response, and Obtain the Bandwidth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVL607 - ANALOG IC DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design a CS Amplifier with Diode-Connected Load and Constant Current Source Load. Perform Transient and AC Analysis, Plot the Frequency Response, and Obtain the Bandwidth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Common Source (CS) Amplifier with Diode-Connected Load** is a single-stage MOS amplifier topology in which an NMOS driver transistor (M1) is biased in saturation and loaded by a PMOS transistor (M2) whose drain and gate terminals are shorted together. This diode-connected MOSFET behaves as a small-signal resistance approximately equal to $1/g_{m2}$ in parallel with its output resistance $r_{o2}$, replacing a conventional resistive load.

A **CS Amplifier with Constant Current Source (Ideal Current Source) Load** replaces the diode-connected load with an ideal DC current source (typically realized by a saturated PMOS transistor M2 with its gate tied to a fixed bias voltage). The AC small-signal impedance of this load tends to infinity, and the small-signal gain is limited only by the parallel combination of $r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The two topologies are compared because the **diode-connected load** provides a *predictable, self-biased, process-tolerant* gain, while the **current-source load** delivers *maximum intrinsic gain* and is the canonical building block of operational transconductance amplifiers (OTAs) used in CMOS op-amps.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Faucet and Drain Analogy"** 💧
>
> - **Driver MOSFET (M1):** Imagine a *faucet* controlling water flow. The harder you turn the handle (input voltage $V_{in}$), the more current (water) flows toward the output node.
> - **Diode-Connected Load (M2):** Think of a *flexible rubber pipe* clamped at both ends. The harder the faucet pushes, the more the pipe resists — the resistance is automatically set by the flow itself (self-biasing). This gives a *predictable but limited* output swing.
> - **Current Source Load (M2):** Imagine a *constant-height water tank* that always delivers the same flow regardless of the pressure at the bottom. Since the tank's internal resistance is infinite, the output voltage can swing widely, producing a *much larger small-signal gain*.
> - **Output Node (Drain):** A *measuring cup* that collects water. The cup fills up (voltage rises) or empties (voltage falls) based on the imbalance between the faucet push and the load pull.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

- **Electron mobility (NMOS):** $\mu_n C_{ox} \approx 100 \,\mu A/V^2$ (typical for 180 nm CMOS process)
- **Hole mobility (PMOS):** $\mu_p C_{ox} \approx 40 \,\mu A/V^2$
- **Thermal voltage:** $V_T = kT/q \approx 26\,mV$ at room temperature
- **Process parameters:** $V_{THN} \approx 0.5\,V$, $V_{THP} \approx -0.5\,V$, $\lambda \approx 0.1\,V^{-1}$
- **Key performance metrics:** Small-signal gain $|A_v|$, **3-dB Bandwidth (BW)**, **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)**, **Phase Margin**, **Output Swing**

### 1.4 Why This Experiment?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This experiment is the *cornerstone* of CMOS analog design. Every operational amplifier, comparator, and switched-capacitor circuit uses one of these two gain stages. Mastering transient and AC analysis of both topologies equips the student with the analytical tools to design high-performance, low-power analog integrated circuits.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Step by Step

**Step 1: DC Bias Point Establishment**
- Both M1 (driver) and M2 (load) must operate in the **saturation region** to function as a proper amplifier.
- For M1: $V_{DS1} \geq V_{GS1} - V_{THN}$ (saturation condition).
- For M2 (diode-connected): $V_{DS2} = V_{GS2}$, so saturation is *automatically satisfied* as long as $V_{GS2} > V_{THP}$ in magnitude.

**Step 2: Drain Current Calculation (Square-Law Model)**
- The DC current through both transistors is identical: $I_{D1} = I_{D2} = I_D$.
- Using the standard long-channel equation:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\,\mu_n C_{ox}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 (V_{GS1} - V_{THN})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS1})$$

- In hand analysis, the channel-length modulation factor $(1 + \lambda V_{DS1})$ is often approximated as $\approx 1$ for simplicity.

**Step 3: Small-Signal Transconductance Extraction**
- The driver M1 converts input voltage variation $v_{gs}$ to output current variation $i_d = g_{m1} v_{gs}$.

$$g_{m1} = \mu_n C_{ox}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 (V_{GS1} - V_{THN}) = \sqrt{2\,\mu_n C_{ox}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 I_D} = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{GS1} - V_{THN}}$$

**Step 4: Small-Signal Output Resistance**
- For a saturated MOSFET: $r_{o} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D}$, inversely proportional to the bias current.

**Step 5: Mid-Band Gain Calculation**

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Mid-Band Range:** At mid-band frequencies, the coupling and bypass capacitors act as short circuits, and the device parasitic capacitances ($C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$) are negligible. Thus, the gain is purely determined by the resistive small-signal parameters.

- **Topology A (Diode-Connected Load):**

$$A_v = -g_{m1}\left(r_{o1}\,\Vert\, r_{o2}\,\Vert\, \frac{1}{g_{m2}}\right) \approx -\frac{g_{m1}}{g_{m2}} \quad \text{(ignoring } r_o \text{ effects)}$$

- **Topology B (Current Source Load):**

$$A_v = -g_{m1}(r_{o1}\,\Vert\, r_{o2}) = -\frac{g_{m1}}{\lambda_1 I_D + \lambda_2 I_D} = -\frac{2}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2} \cdot \frac{1}{V_{OV1}}$$

**Step 6: Frequency Response & Bandwidth**
- The dominant pole is set by the **total capacitance** at the output node ($C_L$, including $C_{gd1}$, $C_{db1}$, $C_{db2}$, and any load capacitance).
- Dominant pole frequency:

$$f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_{out})\, C_L}$$

- **3-dB Bandwidth:** $BW = f_p$ (for a single-pole system).
- **Gain-Bandwidth Product:** $GBW = |A_v| \cdot BW = \dfrac{g_{m1}}{2\pi C_L}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The GBW is a **constant** for a given $g_{m1}$ and $C_L$, regardless of the load topology. This is a fundamental trade-off: higher gain comes at the cost of lower bandwidth.

**Step 7: Transient Analysis**
- Apply a small-signal pulse or sinusoidal input and observe the output waveform.
- Key parameters to extract: **rise time $t_r$**, **fall time $t_f$**, **overshoot**, **settling time $t_s$**, and **slew rate $SR$** (limited by bias current and load capacitance: $SR = I_D / C_L$).

### 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of CS Amplifier |
|---|---|
| **CMOS Op-Amps** | First gain stage of two-stage Miller-compensated op-amps |
| **Comparators** | Front-end pre-amplification of small differential signals |
| **ADC Front-Ends** | Sample-and-hold and buffer amplifier input stages |
| **RF Mixers** | Transconductance stage for Gilbert-cell mixers |
| **Biomedical Sensors** | Low-noise amplification of bio-signals (ECG, EEG) |
| **Audio Codecs** | Line drivers and headphone amplifiers |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Diode-Connected Load** | **Current Source Load** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Drain Current $I_D$ | $\frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)_1 (V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2$ | $\frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)_1 (V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2$ | A (Amperes) |
| Transconductance $g_{m1}$ | $\sqrt{2\mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)_1 I_D}$ | $\sqrt{2\mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)_1 I_D}$ | S (Siemens) |
| Output Resistance $R_{out}$ | $r_{o1} \Vert r_{o2} \Vert (1/g_{m2})$ | $r_{o1} \Vert r_{o2}$ | $\Omega$ |
| Mid-Band Gain $A_v$ | $\approx -g_{m1}/g_{m2}$ | $-g_{m1}(r_{o1} \Vert r_{o2})$ | V/V (dimensionless) |
| Gain Magnitude $\vert A_v \vert$ | $\sqrt{(W/L)_1 / (W/L)_2} \cdot \sqrt{\mu_n/\mu_p}$ | $2/[\lambda_1 + \lambda_2]/V_{OV1}$ | V/V |
| Dominant Pole $f_p$ | $1/[2\pi R_{out} C_L]$ | $1/[2\pi R_{out} C_L]$ | Hz |
| Bandwidth $BW$ | $= f_p$ | $= f_p$ | Hz |
| GBW Product | $g_{m1}/(2\pi C_L)$ | $g_{m1}/(2\pi C_L)$ | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Trade-off Rule of Thumb:** Increasing the aspect ratio $(W/L)_1$ of M1 boosts $g_{m1}$ and hence GBW, but simultaneously increases silicon area and parasitic capacitances — a classic **power-area-bandwidth** trade-off.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Design Specification (Worked Example)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following numerical example is fully worked out. All algebraic transitions are explicit. Students can directly adapt these values for SPICE simulation (using LTspice, Cadence, or Ngspice).

**Given Specifications:**
- Technology: 180 nm CMOS
- Supply voltage: $V_{DD} = 1.8\,V$
- Bias current: $I_D = 100\,\mu A$
- Load capacitance: $C_L = 1\,pF$
- Process parameters: $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100\,\mu A/V^2$, $\mu_p C_{ox} = 40\,\mu A/V^2$, $V_{THN} = 0.5\,V$, $V_{THP} = -0.5\,V$, $\lambda_n = \lambda_p = 0.1\,V^{-1}$

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — Sizing of Driver Transistor M1

**Step 1: Compute the overdrive voltage $V_{OV1}$**

We first choose $V_{OV1} = V_{GS1} - V_{THN} = 0.2\,V$ (typical low-voltage design choice for 1.8 V supply).

$$V_{OV1} = 0.2\,V$$

**Step 2: Compute the required aspect ratio $(W/L)_1$**

From the square-law drain current equation (ignoring channel-length modulation):

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 V_{OV1}^2$$

Rearranging for $(W/L)_1$:

$$\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 = \frac{2 I_D}{\mu_n C_{ox} V_{OV1}^2}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 = \frac{2 \times 100\,\mu A}{100\,\mu A/V^2 \times (0.2\,V)^2}$$

Let us compute the denominator explicitly:

$$100 \times 10^{-6} \times (0.2)^2 = 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.04 = 4 \times 10^{-6}\,A/V^2$$

Therefore:

$$\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 = \frac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = 50$$

So we choose $W_1 = 10\,\mu m$ and $L_1 = 0.2\,\mu m$ (minimum-length device in 180 nm) to obtain $(W/L)_1 = 50$.

**Step 3: Compute the transconductance $g_{m1}$**

$$g_{m1} = \mu_n C_{ox}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 V_{OV1}$$

Substituting the values:

$$g_{m1} = 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 50 \times 0.2 = 1000 \times 10^{-6} = 1\,mA/V = 1\,mS$$

**Alternative formula verification using $g_m = 2I_D/V_{OV}$:**

$$g_{m1} = \frac{2 \times 100\,\mu A}{0.2\,V} = \frac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{0.2} = 1\,mS \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation — Diode-Connected Load Topology

**Step 4: Size the PMOS load M2 (diode-connected)**

The current through M2 is identical to M1. Choose $V_{OV2} = 0.2\,V$ (matched with M1 for simplicity, but typically slightly larger to allow output swing).

$$\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_2 = \frac{2 I_D}{\mu_p C_{ox} V_{OV2}^2} = \frac{2 \times 100\,\mu A}{40\,\mu A/V^2 \times (0.04)} = \frac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{1.6 \times 10^{-6}} = 125$$

So we choose $W_2 = 25\,\mu m$ and $L_2 = 0.2\,\mu m$ to obtain $(W/L)_2 = 125$.

**Step 5: Compute $g_{m2}$ of the diode-connected load**

$$g_{m2} = \mu_p C_{ox}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_2 V_{OV2} = 40 \times 10^{-6} \times 125 \times 0.2 = 1\,mS$$

**Step 6: Compute small-signal output resistance of M2 (diode-connected)**

A diode-connected MOSFET has a small-signal resistance of:

$$R_{D2} = \frac{1}{g_{m2}} \Vert r_{o2}$$

**Step 7: Compute $r_{o1}$ and $r_{o2}$**

$$r_{o1} = \frac{1}{\lambda_n I_D} = \frac{1}{0.1 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{10 \times 10^{-6}} = 100\,k\Omega$$

$$r_{o2} = \frac{1}{\lambda_p I_D} = \frac{1}{0.1 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}} = 100\,k\Omega$$

**Step 8: Compute $R_{D2}$ accurately**

$$R_{D2} = \frac{1}{g_{m2} + 1/r_{o2}} = \frac{1}{10^{-3} + 10^{-5}} \approx \frac{1}{1.01 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 990\,\Omega$$

Note that $1/g_{m2} = 1\,k\Omega$ and $r_{o2} = 100\,k\Omega$, so $R_{D2} \approx 1/g_{m2} \approx 990\,\Omega$.

**Step 9: Compute the total small-signal output resistance**

$$R_{out} = r_{o1} \Vert R_{D2} = 100\,k\Omega \Vert 990\,\Omega$$

$$\frac{1}{R_{out}} = \frac{1}{100{,}000} + \frac{1}{990} = 0.00001 + 0.00101 = 0.00102\,S$$

$$R_{out} \approx 980\,\Omega$$

**Step 10: Compute the mid-band gain for diode-connected load**

$$A_v = -g_{m1} R_{out} = -1 \times 10^{-3} \times 980 \approx -0.98\,V/V \approx -1\,V/V$$

$$\boxed{|A_v|_{\text{diode}} \approx 1\,V/V \quad (\approx 0\,dB)}$$

This low gain (essentially 0 dB) is the **defining characteristic** of the diode-connected load — it trades gain for **self-biasing simplicity** and **predictability**.

**Step 11: Compute the dominant pole and bandwidth**

$$f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{out} C_L} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 980 \times 1 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$f_p = \frac{1}{6.157 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 162.4\,MHz$$

**Step 12: Compute the Gain-Bandwidth Product**

$$GBW = |A_v| \cdot f_p = 0.98 \times 162.4\,MHz \approx 159\,MHz$$

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation — Current Source Load Topology

**Step 13: With the current source load, the AC impedance is $r_{o2}$ alone**

$$R_{out} = r_{o1} \Vert r_{o2} = 100\,k\Omega \Vert 100\,k\Omega = 50\,k\Omega$$

**Step 14: Compute the mid-band gain for current source load**

$$A_v = -g_{m1} (r_{o1} \Vert r_{o2}) = -1 \times 10^{-3} \times 50 \times 10^{3} = -50\,V/V$$

$$\boxed{|A_v|_{\text{CS-load}} = 50\,V/V \quad (\approx 34\,dB)}$$

**Step 15: Compute the dominant pole and bandwidth**

$$f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{out} C_L} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times 10^{3} \times 1 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 3.18\,MHz$$

**Step 16: Compute the Gain-Bandwidth Product**

$$GBW = |A_v| \cdot f_p = 50 \times 3.18\,MHz = 159\,MHz \quad \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of the GBW Invariance Principle:** Notice that $GBW = 159\,MHz$ is *identical* for both topologies! This is because the GBW depends only on $g_{m1}$ and $C_L$, both of which are the same in both cases. The topology only changes how the gain–bandwidth budget is *allocated*.

### 3.5 Comparative Summary Table

| **Parameter** | **Diode-Connected Load** | **Current Source Load** |
|---|---|---|
| Bias Current $I_D$ | $100\,\mu A$ | $100\,\mu A$ |
| $g_{m1}$ | $1\,mS$ | $1\,mS$ |
| Load Impedance $R_L$ | $1/g_{m2} \approx 1\,k\Omega$ | $r_{o2} = 100\,k\Omega$ |
| $R_{out}$ | $\approx 980\,\Omega$ | $50\,k\Omega$ |
| Mid-Band Gain $\vert A_v \vert$ | $\approx 1\,V/V$ (0 dB) | $50\,V/V$ (34 dB) |
| Bandwidth $BW$ | $162.4\,MHz$ | $3.18\,MHz$ |
| GBW | $159\,MHz$ | $159\,MHz$ |
| Output Swing | Limited (only ~$V_{OV1}$) | Wide (~$V_{DD} - 2V_{OV}$) |
| Self-Biased? | ✅ Yes (no extra bias needed) | ❌ No (requires extra bias voltage) |
| Process Sensitivity | Low | Higher (depends on $r_o$) |

### 3.6 Transient Analysis Notes

For transient simulation in SPICE:
- Apply a **pulse input** (e.g., 1 mV amplitude, 1 ns rise/fall, 50 ns pulse width) or a **small sinusoid** at mid-band.
- Observe: rise time $t_r \approx 0.35 / BW$, settling time, overshoot.
- For a single-pole system, the 10%–90% rise time is:

$$t_r = \frac{0.35}{BW} = 2.2 \cdot R_{out} \cdot C_L$$

For the current-source load topology:

$$t_r = 2.2 \times 50 \times 10^3 \times 1 \times 10^{-12} = 110\,ns$$

For the diode-connected load topology:

$$t_r = 2.2 \times 980 \times 1 \times 10^{-12} \approx 2.16\,ns$$

> [!NOTE]
> The diode-connected load has a much *faster* time-domain response because its smaller output resistance charges $C_L$ more quickly. This is the **fundamental speed–gain trade-off** in analog IC design.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CS Amplifier with Diode-Connected Load — Functional Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the **signal flow and biasing interactions** of the CS amplifier with diode-connected load, following the KTU schematic conventions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["VIN DC Bias Source VGS1"] --> nodeB["NMOS Driver M1 Gate"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["M1 Channel Drain Current ID"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Output Node VOUT"]
    nodeE["VDD Supply 1.8V"] --> nodeF["PMOS Load M2 Source"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["M2 Drain and Gate Tied"]
    nodeG --> nodeD
    nodeG --> nodeB2["M2 Gate Feedback"]
    nodeD --> nodeH["Load Capacitor CL 1pF"]
    nodeD --> nodeI["AC Probe VOUT"]
    nodeJ["GND 0V"] --> nodeK["M1 Source"]
    nodeJ --> nodeL["M2 Bulk"]
```

**Architecture Interpretation:**
- **nodeA → nodeB:** The DC input bias sets the gate of the NMOS driver M1.
- **nodeC → nodeD:** Drain current $I_D$ flows into the output node.
- **nodeG (drain-gate short):** This is the *defining feature* of a diode-connected MOSFET — its gate and drain are tied together, forcing $V_{GS2} = V_{DS2}$ and creating the self-biased $1/g_{m2}$ load.
- **nodeH:** The load capacitor models the parasitic + external capacitances that determine the dominant pole.

### 4.2 CS Amplifier with Current Source Load — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA2["VIN DC Bias VGS1"] --> nodeB2A["NMOS Driver M1 Gate"]
    nodeB2A --> nodeC2A["M1 Channel ID"]
    nodeC2A --> nodeD2A["Output Node VOUT"]
    nodeE2A["VDD 1.8V"] --> nodeF2A["PMOS M2 Source"]
    nodeM["External Bias VBias from Current Mirror"] --> nodeN["M2 Gate Fixed Voltage"]
    nodeF2A --> nodeG2A["M2 Drain"]
    nodeN --> nodeG2A
    nodeG2A --> nodeD2A
    nodeD2A --> nodeH2A["Load Capacitor CL"]
    nodeD2A --> nodeI2A["AC Probe VOUT"]
    nodeJ2A["GND 0V"] --> nodeK2A["M1 Source"]
    nodeP2A["GND 0V"] --> nodeQ2A["M2 Bulk"]
```

**Architecture Interpretation:**
- **nodeM (External Bias) → nodeN (M2 Gate):** Unlike the diode-connected topology, the current-source PMOS load has its gate driven by a *fixed* bias voltage (typically generated by a current mirror). This is what gives $R_{out} = r_{o2}$ instead of $1/g_{m2}$.
- The output node **nodeD2A** can swing much more widely because the load impedance is dramatically higher.

### 4.3 Side-by-Side Comparison Flowchart

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA["START CS Amplifier Design"] --> stepB{"Choose Load Topology"}
    stepB -->|Diode-Connected| stepC["RL equals 1 over gm2 approx 1k"]
    stepB -->|Current Source| stepD["RL equals ro2 equals 100k"]
    stepC --> stepE["Gain Av equals 0 dB approx 1"]
    stepD --> stepF["Gain Av equals 34 dB approx 50"]
    stepE --> stepG["Bandwidth equals 162 MHz"]
    stepF --> stepH["Bandwidth equals 3.18 MHz"]
    stepG --> stepI["GBW equals 159 MHz"]
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ["END Constant GBW Tradeoff"]
```

### 4.4 AC Analysis Test Bench Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    srcA["AC Source 1V Amplitude"] --> capA["Coupling Cap Cin"]
    capA --> gateM1["M1 Gate Input Node"]
    gateM1 --> mosM1["M1 NMOS Driver"]
    mosM1 --> outNode["VOUT Drain Node"]
    pmosM2["M2 PMOS Load"] --> outNode
    outNode --> capB["Coupling Cap Cout"]
    capB --> probeOut["VOUT Probe"]
    outNode --> capL["CL Load Capacitor 1pF"]
    srcB["VDD 1.8V"] --> pmosM2
    gndA["GND"] --> mosM1
    biasCircuit["Bias Network VGS1"] --> gateM1
```

**Interpretation of the AC Test Bench:**
- **srcA → capA → gateM1:** AC input is AC-coupled to remove the DC bias offset.
- **outNode → capB → probeOut:** AC output is AC-coupled for clean small-signal observation.
- **capL:** Represents the total load capacitance (intrinsic + extrinsic).
- **biasCircuit:** Sets the DC operating point for M1's gate.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Exam Tip:** When asked to "plot the frequency response," students must explicitly mention the **AC analysis** command in SPICE (e.g., `.ac dec 10 1 1G`) and the **probe/probe statement** to extract $V_{OUT}$ in dB. The **3-dB bandwidth** is the frequency at which the gain drops by 3 dB from its mid-band value.
