## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Compare the small-signal voltage gain of a CS amplifier with a diode-connected load versus a current-source load. Which one provides higher gain and why?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The small-signal voltage gain of a CS amplifier with a **diode-connected load** is:

$$A_v \approx -\frac{g_{m1}}{g_{m2}} = -\sqrt{\frac{\mu_n(W/L)_1}{\mu_p(W/L)_2}}$$

For the **current-source load**, the gain is:

$$A_v = -g_{m1}(r_{o1} \Vert r_{o2})$$

The current-source load provides **significantly higher gain** (typically 30–60 dB) because the load impedance is $r_{o2} \approx 100\,k\Omega$, which is two orders of magnitude larger than the diode-connected resistance $1/g_{m2} \approx 1\,k\Omega$. **(3 marks — 1 mark for each formula, 1 mark for comparison conclusion)**

---

> **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Define the 3-dB bandwidth of an amplifier. How is it determined from the frequency response plot?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **3-dB bandwidth (BW)** of an amplifier is defined as the range of frequencies over which the magnitude of the voltage gain $|A_v(f)|$ remains within **3 dB** of its maximum mid-band value. Mathematically, BW is the frequency $f_H$ at which:

$$|A_v(f_H)| = \frac{|A_{v,\text{mid}}|}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 \cdot |A_{v,\text{mid}}|$$

It is determined from the Bode magnitude plot by **locating the frequency at which the gain curve drops by 3 dB** from the flat mid-band region. This corresponds to the **dominant pole** $f_p$ in a single-pole amplifier. **(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for mathematical criterion, 1 mark for graphical interpretation)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **(a)** Design a CS amplifier with a **diode-connected PMOS load** to achieve a mid-band gain of at least 5 V/V. Assume 180 nm CMOS technology with $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100\,\mu A/V^2$, $\mu_p C_{ox} = 40\,\mu A/V^2$, $V_{THN} = 0.5\,V$, $V_{THP} = -0.5\,V$, $\lambda = 0.1\,V^{-1}$, and $V_{DD} = 1.8\,V$. Determine $(W/L)_1$, $(W/L)_2$, $I_D$, and $g_{m1}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the amplifier in (a), if a load capacitor $C_L = 2\,pF$ is connected at the output, calculate the **dominant pole frequency**, the **3-dB bandwidth**, and the **gain-bandwidth product**. Sketch the expected Bode magnitude plot. **(7 marks)**

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Design for $|A_v| \geq 5\,V/V$ (7 marks)**

The gain of a CS amplifier with diode-connected PMOS load is:

$$|A_v| = \frac{g_{m1}}{g_{m2}} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_1}{\mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_2}}$$

**Step 1:** Choose $(W/L)_2 = 10$ (PMOS load). Then:

$$5 = \sqrt{\frac{100 \times (W/L)_1}{40 \times 10}} = \sqrt{\frac{(W/L)_1}{4}} \implies 25 = \frac{(W/L)_1}{4} \implies (W/L)_1 = 100$$

**Step 2:** Choose $V_{OV1} = V_{OV2} = 0.2\,V$ for matched overdrive.

**Step 3:** Calculate $I_D$:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_1 V_{OV1}^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times (0.2)^2 = 200\,\mu A$$

**Step 4:** Calculate $g_{m1}$:

$$g_{m1} = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV1}} = \frac{2 \times 200 \times 10^{-6}}{0.2} = 2\,mS$$

**Design Summary:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| $(W/L)_1$ | 100 |
| $(W/L)_2$ | 10 |
| $I_D$ | $200\,\mu A$ |
| $g_{m1}$ | $2\,mS$ |
| $V_{OV1}$ | $0.2\,V$ |
| $V_{GS1}$ | $0.7\,V$ |

**(7 marks — 2 for sizing equations, 2 for numerical computation, 2 for $g_m$ calculation, 1 for final summary)**

---

**Part (b) — Frequency Response (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Compute small-signal output resistance.

$$R_{out} = r_{o1} \Vert r_{o2} \Vert \frac{1}{g_{m2}}$$

With $r_o = 1/(\lambda I_D) = 1/(0.1 \times 200 \times 10^{-6}) = 50\,k\Omega$.

$$g_{m2} = \mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_2 V_{OV2} = 40 \times 10^{-6} \times 10 \times 0.2 = 80\,\mu S \implies \frac{1}{g_{m2}} = 12.5\,k\Omega$$

$$R_{out} = 50\,k\Omega \Vert 50\,k\Omega \Vert 12.5\,k\Omega \approx 8.33\,k\Omega \text{ (approximately)}$$

More precisely:

$$\frac{1}{R_{out}} = \frac{1}{50{,}000} + \frac{1}{50{,}000} + \frac{1}{12{,}500} = 2 \times 10^{-5} + 8 \times 10^{-5} = 10^{-4}\,S$$

$$R_{out} = 10\,k\Omega$$

**Step 2:** Dominant pole frequency.

$$f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{out} C_L} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 2 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{1.2566 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 7.96\,MHz$$

**Step 3:** 3-dB Bandwidth.

$$BW = f_p \approx 7.96\,MHz$$

**Step 4:** Gain-Bandwidth Product.

$$GBW = |A_v| \cdot BW = 5 \times 7.96\,MHz = 39.8\,MHz$$

**Step 5:** Bode magnitude plot description.

- Mid-band gain: $20 \log_{10}(5) \approx 13.98\,dB$
- Flat region from DC to $\sim 8\,MHz$
- Roll-off at $-20\,dB/decade$ beyond the pole
- At $f = 8\,MHz$, the gain is $13.98 - 3 = 10.98\,dB$

**(7 marks — 2 for $R_{out}$ calculation, 2 for pole/BW calculation, 2 for GBW, 1 for plot description)**

---

> **Question B (Alternative Choice).** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **(a)** Design a CS amplifier with a **PMOS current-source load** for a mid-band gain of 50 V/V using the same 180 nm process parameters. Compute the bias current, transconductance, and output resistance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how to perform **transient and AC analysis** in SPICE for this circuit. Describe the **frequency response plot** and calculate the **bandwidth** if $C_L = 500\,fF$. Compare the bandwidth with that of a diode-connected load amplifier having the same $g_{m1}$ and $C_L$. **(7 marks)**

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Current Source Load Design (7 marks)**

The gain is:

$$|A_v| = g_{m1} (r_{o1} \Vert r_{o2}) = \frac{g_{m1}}{\lambda_1 I_D + \lambda_2 I_D} = \frac{2}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2} \cdot \frac{1}{V_{OV1}}$$

Substituting $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = 0.1\,V^{-1}$:

$$50 = \frac{2}{0.2} \cdot \frac{1}{V_{OV1}} = \frac{10}{V_{OV1}} \implies V_{OV1} = 0.2\,V$$

**Step 1:** Choose $V_{OV1} = 0.2\,V$ and $(W/L)_1 = 50$.

**Step 2:** Bias current:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 50 \times 0.04 = 100\,\mu A$$

**Step 3:** Transconductance:

$$g_{m1} = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV1}} = \frac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{0.2} = 1\,mS$$

**Step 4:** Output resistance:

$$r_{o1} = r_{o2} = \frac{1}{0.1 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}} = 100\,k\Omega$$

$$R_{out} = r_{o1} \Vert r_{o2} = 50\,k\Omega$$

**Verification:** $|A_v| = 10^{-3} \times 50 \times 10^3 = 50\,V/V$ ✓

**(7 marks — 2 for design equation, 2 for $V_{OV}/I_D$ calculation, 2 for $g_m/r_o$, 1 for verification)**

---

**Part (b) — SPICE Analysis & Bandwidth Comparison (7 marks)**

**SPICE Netlist Sketch:**

```
M1 VOUT VIN 0 0 NMOS W=10u L=0.2u
M2 VOUT VBIAS VDD VDD PMOS W=20u L=0.2u
VBIAS VBIAS 0 DC 0.9V
VDD VDD 0 DC 1.8V
CL VOUT 0 500fF
VIN VIN 0 SIN(0.7 1m 1Meg)
.OP
.TRAN 1n 5u
.AC DEC 100 1 10G
.PROBE
.END
```

**Transient Analysis:**
- Use `.TRAN` directive with a small-signal pulse or sine input.
- Observe the output waveform: phase-inverted, amplified sinusoid.
- Measure rise time $t_r \approx 2.2 R_{out} C_L$.

**AC Analysis:**
- Use `.AC DEC 100 1 10G` for decade sweep from 1 Hz to 10 GHz.
- Plot $|V_{out}/V_{in}|$ in dB.
- The 3-dB bandwidth is the frequency at which the gain drops by 3 dB from the mid-band value.

**Step 1: Calculate dominant pole.**

$$f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times 10^3 \times 500 \times 10^{-15}} = \frac{1}{1.5708 \times 10^{-10}} = 6.37\,MHz$$

**Step 2: Calculate 3-dB bandwidth.**

$$BW = 6.37\,MHz$$

**Step 3: Compare with diode-connected load.**

For the same $g_{m1} = 1\,mS$ and $C_L = 500\,fF$, the diode-connected load has $R_{out} \approx 1/g_{m2} \approx 1\,k\Omega$ (assuming matched sizing), so:

$$BW_{\text{diode}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1 \times 10^3 \times 500 \times 10^{-15}} = 318\,MHz$$

**Comparison Summary:**

| Topology | Gain | Bandwidth | GBW |
|---|---|---|---|
| Current Source Load | $50\,V/V$ | $6.37\,MHz$ | $318\,MHz$ |
| Diode-Connected Load | $\approx 1\,V/V$ | $318\,MHz$ | $318\,MHz$ |

**Conclusion:** The current-source load provides **higher gain** but **lower bandwidth** for the same $g_{m1}$ and $C_L$, while the **GBW remains constant**, confirming the fundamental gain–bandwidth trade-off in single-stage amplifiers.

**(7 marks — 2 for SPICE netlist/transient/AC explanation, 2 for pole/BW calculation, 2 for comparison table, 1 for concluding trade-off statement)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting channel-length modulation:** Many students compute $g_m$ but ignore $\lambda$, leading to an incorrect infinite bandwidth estimate. Always include $r_o$ in the analysis.
> 2. **Confusing the load type:** The diode-connected load is *self-biased* ($V_{GS2} = V_{DS2}$), but the current-source load requires an *external gate bias* from a current mirror. Mixing up these biasing schemes is a frequent error.
> 3. **Sign convention for gain:** The CS amplifier is an **inverting** stage — output is $180°$ out of phase with input. Students often write $A_v = +g_m R_L$, losing 1 mark.
> 4. **SPICE syntax errors:** The `.AC` directive requires units (Hz), and `.TRAN` requires time. A missing unit is a common mistake.
> 5. **GBW misinterpretation:** GBW is a *figure of merit* — students often confuse it with the unity-gain frequency. The 3-dB bandwidth is **NOT** the same as the unity-gain crossover unless the gain is 1.
> 6. **Neglecting $C_{gd}$ (Miller effect):** At high frequencies, the gate-to-drain capacitance of M1 appears as a larger input capacitance due to the Miller effect. For more accurate high-frequency analysis, this must be considered.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **CS Amplifier Topology:** NMOS driver M1 + load at the drain. Output is taken from M1's drain.
- [x] **Diode-Connected Load:** $V_{GS2} = V_{DS2}$; small-signal resistance $\approx 1/g_{m2}$.
- [x] **Current Source Load:** Externally biased PMOS; AC impedance $= r_{o2}$.
- [x] **Mid-Band Gain:** $A_v = -g_{m1} R_{out}$ where $R_{out}$ depends on the load type.
- [x] **Diode-Connected Gain:** $A_v \approx -g_{m1}/g_{m2}$ (typically low, $\sim 0$ dB).
- [x] **Current-Source Gain:** $A_v = -g_{m1}(r_{o1} \Vert r_{o2})$ (high, typically 20–40 dB).
- [x] **Drain Current Equation:** $I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu C_{ox}(W/L)(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2$.
- [x] **Transconductance:** $g_m = \sqrt{2\mu C_{ox}(W/L) I_D} = 2I_D/V_{OV}$.
- [x] **Output Resistance:** $r_o = 1/(\lambda I_D)$.
- [x] **Dominant Pole:** $f_p = 1/(2\pi R_{out} C_L)$.
- [x] **3-dB Bandwidth:** $BW = f_p$ for a single-pole system.
- [x] **Gain-Bandwidth Product:** $GBW = g_{m1}/(2\pi C_L)$ — a *constant* for given $g_{m1}$ and $C_L$.
- [x] **SPICE Transient Analysis:** `.TRAN` directive with pulse/sinusoidal input.
- [x] **SPICE AC Analysis:** `.AC DEC` directive for Bode plot generation.
- [x] **3-dB Point on Bode Plot:** Frequency at which $|A_v|$ drops by 3 dB from mid-band.
- [x] **Rise Time:** $t_r = 2.2 R_{out} C_L \approx 0.35/BW$.
- [x] **Phase Inversion:** CS amplifier inverts the signal (180° phase shift).
- [x] **Trade-off:** Higher gain ↔ Lower bandwidth (constant GBW).
- [x] **Diode Load:** Self-biased, predictable, low gain, high bandwidth.
- [x] **Current Source Load:** Requires external bias, high gain, low bandwidth, used in op-amp first stages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** *"Know your $g_m$, know your $r_o$, know your $C_L$ — and the rest is just algebra."* Master the formulas, sketch the small-signal model, and you will ace both the design and analysis questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design a Common Drain Amplifier and Perform AC Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEVL607 - ANALOG IC DESIGN LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Design a Common Drain Amplifier and Perform AC Analysis # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition A **Common Drain (CD) Amplifier**, more popularly known as the **Source Follower**, is a single-stage MOSFET amplifier configuration in which the input signal is applied at the **gate**, the output is taken from the **source**, and the **drain is tied directly to the supply $V_{DD}$** (which behaves as an AC ground for small-signal operation). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** The Common Drain amplifier is a three-terminal MOSFET configuration that provides a non-inverting voltage gain of magnitude slightly less than unity ($\vert A_v \vert < 1$), an extremely **high input impedance** ($R_{in} \to \infty$), and a **low output impedance** ($R_{out} \approx 1/g_m$). It is therefore predominantly used as a **voltage buffer** or **impedance transformer** between a high-impedance source and a low-impedance load. The naming convention "common drain" follows the same logic as in BJT circuits: the terminal that is **common to both input and output ports** (here, the drain) is grounded for AC signals. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure Regulator" Imagine a hydraulic pressure regulator connected to a fluctuating main supply line: - The **input pressure** $V_{in}$ (applied at the gate) is the *desired reference pressure*. - The **output pressure** at the nozzle (the source) automatically *follows* the input, always staying just a small fixed drop (called $V_{GS}$) below it. - No matter how much water you draw from the nozzle (the load), the regulator adjusts a valve (the MOSFET channel) internally to keep the output pressure nearly constant. This is **exactly** how the source follower behaves electrically: the source voltage *chases* the gate voltage, lagging it by a small $V_{GS}$ offset. If the gate rises by 1 V, the source rises by almost 1 V (just slightly less). That is why the voltage gain is *near unity*. ## 1.3 Key Physical & Electrical Highlights > [!NOTE] > **Core characteristics of the CD Amplifier:** > - **Phase Relationship:** Non-inverting ($0^\circ$ phase shift between input and output). > - **Voltage Gain:** $A_v \approx +1$ (slightly less than 1, typically **0.8 to 0.95**). > - **Input Resistance:** $R_{in} \approx R_1 \Vert R_2$ (set by the gate bias network, typically **hundreds of $k\Omega$ to $M\Omega$**). > - **Output Resistance:** $R_{out} \approx 1/g_m$ (very low, typically **tens to hundreds of $\Omega$**). > - **Current Gain:** Very high (because the gate draws no DC current). > - **Power Gain:** Moderate to high (product of voltage and current gain). ## 1.4 Why This Circuit Matters in KTU Labs The CD stage is the **fundamental building block** of analog ICs: - It is used as a **level shifter** (output DC level is shifted down by $V_{GS}$). - It drives low-impedance loads such as **speakers, transmission lines, or off-chip loads**. - It is the **output stage of multi-stage op-amps** (e.g., the classic 741 uses emitter followers as output buffers). - It is the basic cell for **adaptive biasing and source-degenerated current mirrors** in advanced CMOS design. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Circuit Topology & DC Biasing The canonical CD amplifier uses a **voltage-divider biased NMOS** with the drain tied to $V_{DD}$ and the source connected to ground through a source resistor $R_S$. A coupling capacitor $C_{in}$ blocks DC at the input, and $C_{out}$ blocks DC at the output. For DC operating point calculation: - **Gate voltage** (set by the bias divider, assuming negligible gate current): $$V_G = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$ - **Source voltage** (from KVL on the gate-source loop): $$V_S = V_G - V_{GS}$$ - **Drain current** (Ohm's law on the source resistor): $$I_D = \frac{V_S}{R_S} = \frac{V_G - V_{GS}}{R_S}$$ - **Drain-to-Source voltage** (must be checked for **saturation region**): $$V_{DS} = V_{DD} - V_S > V_{GS} - V_{TH} = V_{OV}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Saturation Check Simplification:** For a CD stage, the condition $V_{DS} \ge V_{OV}$ reduces to $V_G - V_S \le V_{DD} - V_{OV}$, which is almost always satisfied if $V_S$ is small compared to $V_{DD}$. This is one of the reasons the CD configuration is so easy to bias. ## 2.2 Small-Signal AC Operation When a small AC signal $v_i$ is superimposed on the DC gate bias: - The gate-to-source voltage becomes: $v_{gs} = v_i - v_o$ (since the source is *not* grounded for AC). - The drain current increment is: $i_d = g_m \, v_{gs}$. - The current leaving the source node splits into $R_S$ and the load $R_L$: $i_d = v_o / R_S' $, where $R_S' = R_S \Vert R_L$. - Solving for the small-signal output voltage yields the **gain formula**. ## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Result | Step | Physical Reason | |------|-----------------| | Gate draws no current | MOSFET gate is insulated by $SiO_2$ → $R_{in} \to \infty$ intrinsically | | Output appears at source | Source voltage rises with gate, "chasing" it → gain near 1 | | $A_v < 1$ strictly | A fraction of $v_i$ is dropped across $v_{gs}$ which is needed to drive $i_d$ | | $R_{out}$ is low | Negative feedback action of source resistor forces $V_S$ to follow $V_G$ tightly | | High bandwidth | Miller effect is *absent* (because gain $\approx 1$, no $C_{gd}$ amplification) | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — it is the single most-tested content in KTU ESE for this experiment.** | Parameter | Formula | Typical Units | Remarks | |-----------|---------|---------------|---------| | Transconductance (square-law) | $g_m = \sqrt{2 \, \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} I_D}$ | A/V or S | Uses process parameter $k_n' = \mu_n C_{ox}$ | | Transconductance (alt form) | $g_m = \dfrac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}}$ | A/V | Most useful when $V_{OV}$ is known | | Transconductance (alt form) | $g_m = k_n \dfrac{W}{L} V_{OV}$ | A/V | When $W/L$ is given directly | | Overdrive voltage | $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ | V | Key design choice | | Small-signal Voltage Gain | $A_v = \dfrac{g_m \, R_S'}{1 + g_m \, R_S'}$ | V/V (unitless) | $R_S' = R_S \Vert R_L$ | | Gain (with body effect) | $A_v = \dfrac{g_m \, R_S'}{1 + (g_m + g_{mb}) R_S'}$ | V/V | $g_{mb} = \chi \, g_m$, $\chi \approx 0.1$–$0.3$ | | Input Resistance | $R_{in} = R_1 \Vert R_2$ | $\Omega$ | Limited by bias network | | Output Resistance | $R_{out} = \dfrac{1}{g_m} \Vert R_S \Vert r_o \approx \dfrac{1}{g_m}$ | $\Omega$ | Dominated by $1/g_m$ | | Output Resistance (with body) | $R_{out} = \dfrac{1}{g_m + g_{mb}} \Vert R_S$ | $\Omega$ | When source is tied to substrate | | Bandwidth (-3dB) | $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_{sig} \Vert R_{in}) C_{in,tot}}$ | Hz | $C_{in,tot} = C_{gs} + (1-A_v) C_{gd} \approx C_{gs}$ | | Maximum Voltage Swing | $v_{o,min} = V_{OV}$, $v_{o,max} = V_{DD} - V_{OV}$ | V | Distortion limits | > **Engineering Utility Note:** The CD stage is the **universal buffer** in analog design. When cascading a high-impedance sensor (like a piezoelectric transducer or a sample-and-hold) to a low-impedance load (like a $50\,\Omega$ transmission line), inserting a source follower between them **isolates the source from the load**, preventing loading effects and signal attenuation. This is the same principle behind the use of **op-amp voltage followers (unity-gain buffers)**, but achieved with a single transistor. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 DC Operating Point Derivation We start with the standard CD bias circuit and use KVL on the gate-source loop. ### Step 1 — Gate Voltage from the Divider Because the MOSFET gate draws **zero DC current**, no voltage drop occurs across $R_1$ and $R_2$ beyond that caused by the divider itself. Therefore, $V_G$ is a simple resistive divider result: $$ \begin{aligned} V_G &= V_{DD} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Source Voltage from KVL KVL from gate to source through the $V_{GS}$ drop: $$ \begin{aligned} V_G - V_{GS} - V_S &= 0 \\ \Rightarrow V_S &= V_G - V_{GS} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Drain Current from Ohm's Law The current through $R_S$ (which equals $I_D$ in the channel) is: $$ \begin{aligned} I_D &= \frac{V_S}{R_S} = \frac{V_G - V_{GS}}{R_S} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Self-Consistent Solution with the Square-Law Equation For an NMOS in saturation, $I_D$ also obeys: $$ \begin{aligned} I_D &= \frac{1}{2} \, k_n' \, \frac{W}{L} \, (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 = \frac{1}{2} k_n' \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}^2 \end{aligned} $$ Equating the two expressions for $I_D$ gives a single non-linear equation in $V_{GS}$, which can be solved by iteration or by assuming $V_{OV}$ directly (as done in the KTU design approach below). ## 3.2 AC Small-Signal Voltage Gain — Full Derivation ### Step 1 — Build the Small-Signal Equivalent Replacing the MOSFET with its hybrid-$\pi$ model (with $r_o$ temporarily ignored for clarity), the gate is fed by $v_i$, the drain is grounded (AC-wise), and the source is the output node. ### Step 2 — Identify $v_{gs}$ Correctly Since the source is *floating* for AC (it carries the output), $v_{gs}$ is the **difference** between gate and source: $$ \begin{aligned} v_{gs} &= v_i - v_o \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Write KCL at the Source Node The current from the dependent source $g_m v_{gs}$ flows from drain to source and must equal the current leaving through $R_S'$ (the parallel combination of $R_S$ and the AC load $R_L$): $$ \begin{aligned} g_m \, v_{gs} &= \frac{v_o}{R_S'} \\ g_m (v_i - v_o) &= \frac{v_o}{R_S'} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Solve for $v_o / v_i$ Rearranging: $$ \begin{aligned} g_m \, v_i - g_m \, v_o &= \frac{v_o}{R_S'} \\ g_m \, v_i &= v_o \left( g_m + \frac{1}{R_S'} \right) \\ A_v = \frac{v_o}{v_i} &= \frac{g_m \, R_S'}{1 + g_m \, R_S'} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Limiting Cases (very important for KTU!) - **If $g_m R_S' \gg 1$:** $A_v \to 1$ (ideal buffer). - **If $g_m R_S' \ll 1$:** $A_v \to g_m R_S'$ (degenerates into a poor common-source-like stage). > [!NOTE] > **KTU-Style Numerical Shortcut:** $A_v$ can be re-expressed as $1 - 1/(1 + g_m R_S')$. So if $g_m R_S' = 9$, then $A_v = 0.9$, a useful mental math check. ## 3.3 Input Resistance Derivation The intrinsic MOSFET gate is an open circuit for low frequencies. Therefore the resistance seen at the input terminal is set entirely by the bias network: $$ \begin{aligned} R_{in} &= R_1 \Vert R_2 = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \end{aligned} $$ > For $R_1 = R_2 = 100\,k\Omega$, $R_{in} = 50\,k\Omega$. If $R_1 = R_2 = 1\,M\Omega$, $R_{in} = 500\,k\Omega$. ## 3.4 Output Resistance Derivation (Look-Back Technique) To find $R_{out}$, we (a) deactivate the input ($v_i = 0$) and (b) apply a test voltage $v_x$ at the output node, then find the current $i_x$ drawn from it. ### Step 1 — Deactivate Input Setting $v_i = 0$ removes the driving signal, but the bias network still loads the source through $R_S$. ### Step 2 — Apply Test Source With $v_x$ at the source and $v_i = 0$ at the gate: $$ \begin{aligned} v_{gs} &= 0 - v_x = -v_x \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — KCL at the Source Currents leaving the test source $v_x$: $$ \begin{aligned} i_x &= \frac{v_x}{R_S} + g_m v_{gs} + \frac{v_x}{r_o} \\ i_x &= \frac{v_x}{R_S} - g_m v_x + \frac{v_x}{r_o} \\ i_x &= v_x \left( \frac{1}{R_S} - g_m + \frac{1}{r_o} \right) \end{aligned} $$ > **Sign convention note:** The dependent source *delivers* current $-g_m v_x$ into the source node (because it flows from drain to source, which is *out* of the source when $v_{gs}$ is negative). The minus sign is correct. ### Step 4 — Compute $R_{out}$ $$ \begin{aligned} R_{out} = \frac{v_x}{i_x} &= \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{R_S} - g_m + \dfrac{1}{r_o}} \\ &\approx \frac{1}{g_m} \quad \text{(when } g_m \gg 1/R_S \text{ and } g_m \gg 1/r_o \text{)} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.5 Complete Worked Design Example (KTU Standard) ### Given Specifications | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Supply voltage $V_{DD}$ | $5\,V$ | | Threshold voltage $V_{TH}$ | $1.0\,V$ | | Process transconductance $k_n'$ | $100\,\mu A/V^2$ | | Required drain current $I_D$ | $1\,mA$ | | Aspect ratio $W/L$ | $50\,\mu m / 2\,\mu m$ | | AC load $R_L$ | $10\,k\Omega$ | ### Step 1 — Compute $V_{OV}$ and $V_{GS}$ Using the square-law model: $$ \begin{aligned} I_D &= \frac{1}{2} k_n' \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}^2 \\ 10^{-3} &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot 100 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 25 \cdot V_{OV}^2 \\ 10^{-3} &= 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \cdot V_{OV}^2 \\ V_{OV}^2 &= 0.8 \\ V_{OV} &= 0.894\,V \end{aligned} $$ Therefore $V_{GS} = V_{TH} + V_{OV} = 1.0 + 0.894 = 1.894\,V$. ### Step 2 — Compute Source Voltage and $R_S$ Choose $V_S = 1.5\,V$ (a comfortable mid-rail value), then: $$ \begin{aligned} V_G &= V_S + V_{GS} = 1.5 + 1.894 = 3.394\,V \end{aligned} $$ The source resistor: $$ \begin{aligned} R_S &= \frac{V_S}{I_D} = \frac{1.5}{10^{-3}} = 1.5\,k\Omega \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Bias Divider $R_1, R_2$ Choose the divider current as $10 \times I_D = 10\,mA$? — *No, too large.* Use $I_{div} = 0.1\,I_D = 0.1\,mA$ for low-power biasing: $$ \begin{aligned} R_1 + R_2 &= \frac{V_{DD}}{I_{div}} = \frac{5}{10^{-4}} = 50\,k\Omega \\ \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} &= \frac{V_G}{V_{DD}} = \frac{3.394}{5} = 0.679 \\ R_2 &= 0.679 \cdot 50 = 33.9\,k\Omega \\ R_1 &= 50 - 33.9 = 16.1\,k\Omega \end{aligned} $$ > Standard E24 values: $R_1 = 16\,k\Omega$, $R_2 = 33\,k\Omega$. ### Step 4 — Transconductance $$ \begin{aligned} g_m &= \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}} = \frac{2 \cdot 10^{-3}}{0.894} = 2.24\,mA/V \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Voltage Gain $R_S' = R_S \Vert R_L = 1.5\,k \Vert 10\,k = 1.30\,k\Omega$: $$ \begin{aligned} A_v &= \frac{g_m R_S'}{1 + g_m R_S'} = \frac{2.24 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot 1300}{1 + 2.24 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot 1300} \\ &= \frac{2.91}{3.91} = 0.744\,V/V \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Output Resistance $$ \begin{aligned} R_{out} &\approx \frac{1}{g_m} = \frac{1}{2.24 \times 10^{-3}} = 446\,\Omega \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 7 — Input Resistance $$ \begin{aligned} R_{in} &= R_1 \Vert R_2 = 16\,k \Vert 33\,k = 10.8\,k\Omega \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why $R_{in}$ is not infinite here:** The intrinsic MOSFET gate is open-circuit, but our bias network **loads down** the signal source. If true $R_{in} \to \infty$ is required, a **bootstrap bias** configuration (using a capacitor from source back to the midpoint of $R_1$–$R_2$) is employed — this is an advanced topic often asked in KTU viva. ## 3.6 Summary of Designed Parameters | Parameter | Designed Value | |-----------|----------------| | $R_1$ | $16\,k\Omega$ | | $R_2$ | $33\,k\Omega$ | | $R_S$ | $1.5\,k\Omega$ | | $V_{GS}$ | $1.89\,V$ | | $V_{OV}$ | $0.89\,V$ | | $I_D$ | $1\,mA$ | | $g_m$ | $2.24\,mA/V$ | | $A_v$ | $0.744$ | | $R_{in}$ | $10.8\,k\Omega$ | | $R_{out}$ | $446\,\Omega$ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Circuit Block Diagram — CD Amplifier Functional Architecture The diagram below shows the **functional block architecture** of a Common Drain amplifier, mapping signal flow and DC bias paths separately. This is the standard analytical representation used in KTU lab manuals. ```mermaid graph TB nodeACin["AC Input Signal vi"] --> nodeC1["Coupling Capacitor Cin"] nodeC1 --> nodeGate["Gate Node G
DC bias VG set by R1 R2"] nodeR1["R1 from VDD"] --> nodeGate nodeR2["R2 to GND"] --> nodeGate nodeVDD["VDD Supply"] --> nodeR1 nodeR2 --> nodeGND1["Ground"] nodeGate --> nodeMosfet["NMOS M1
Drain tied to VDD
Source is output"] nodeVDD --> nodeMosfet nodeMosfet --> nodeSource["Source Node S
Output vo taken here"] nodeCout["Coupling Capacitor Cout"] --> nodeSource nodeCout --> nodeOut["AC Output vo to Load RL"] nodeRL["Load RL"] --> nodeOut nodeOut --> nodeGND2["Ground"] nodeRS["Source Resistor RS"] --> nodeSource nodeRS --> nodeGND3["Ground"] classDef supply fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef signal fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 classDef device fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 classDef ground fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 class nodeVDD,nodeR1,nodeR2 supply class nodeACin,nodeC1,nodeGate,nodeCout,nodeOut,nodeRL signal class nodeMosfet,nodeSource,nodeRS device class nodeGND1,nodeGND2,nodeGND3 ground ``` ## 4.2 Small-Signal Equivalent Circuit The diagram below depicts the small-signal hybrid-$\pi$ model of the CD stage with the source as the output node. Note the crucial difference from a Common Source: **$r_o$ is connected between output and AC ground** (not between output and ground through $R_D$). ```mermaid graph TB nodeVin["Small Signal Input vi"] --> nodeGm["Controlled Current Source
id = gm vgs"] nodeVin2["vi at Gate"] --> nodeGprime["G Prime Node
Open Circuit Gate"] nodeGprime --> nodeVgs["vgs = vi - vo"] nodeVgs --> nodeGm nodeGm --> nodeSourceOut["Source Output Node vo"] nodeSourceOut --> nodeRo["ro to AC Ground"] nodeSourceOut --> nodeRSprime["RS Prime = RS parallel RL"] nodeRSprime --> nodeACground["AC Ground"] nodeRo --> nodeACground classDef signal fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 classDef ground fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 classDef device fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 class nodeVin,nodeVin2,nodeGprime,nodeVgs,nodeSourceOut signal class nodeGm,nodeRo,nodeRSprime device class nodeACground ground ``` ## 4.3 Design & Simulation Flow Chart The diagram below outlines the systematic KTU-recommended flow for designing, simulating, and verifying the CD amplifier in LTspice / Cadence / Multisim. ```mermaid graph TB nodeS1["Step 1: Read Specifications
VDD, ID, gm target, RL"] --> nodeS2["Step 2: Choose VOV
Typically 0.2 to 0.5 V"] nodeS2 --> nodeS3["Step 3: Calculate VGS = VTH + VOV"] nodeS3 --> nodeS4["Step 4: Choose VS and compute RS = VS / ID"] nodeS4 --> nodeS5["Step 5: Compute VG and divider R1 R2"] nodeS5 --> nodeS6["Step 6: Compute gm = 2 ID / VOV"] nodeS6 --> nodeS7["Step 7: Compute Av and Rout analytically"] nodeS7 --> nodeS8["Step 8: Build Schematic in SPICE Tool"] nodeS8 --> nodeS9["Step 9: Run DC Operating Point .op"] nodeS9 --> nodeS10["Step 10: Run AC Sweep .ac dec 100 1 100Meg"] nodeS10 --> nodeS11["Step 11: Run Transient .tran with sine input"] nodeS11 --> nodeS12["Step 12: Compare Hand Calculation with SPICE"] nodeS12 --> nodeS13["Step 13: Tabulate Percentage Error
Submit Lab Record"] classDef step fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef sim fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 classDef final fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 class nodeS1,nodeS2,nodeS3,nodeS4,nodeS5,nodeS6,nodeS7 step class nodeS8,nodeS9,nodeS10,nodeS11 sim class nodeS12,nodeS13 final ``` ## 4.4 Expected Output Characteristics (Tabular Form) | Test Input | Expected Output | Observation | |------------|-----------------|-------------| | DC: $V_i = 3.394\,V$ | $V_o = 1.5\,V$ (DC), $I_D = 1\,mA$ | Confirms bias design | | AC sine, $f = 1\,kHz$, $\hat v_i = 100\,mV$ | $\hat v_o \approx 74\,mV$, **in phase** with input | Verifies $A_v \approx 0.74$ | | AC sweep, $1\,Hz$ to $100\,MHz$ | Flat gain till **$f_H \approx$ tens of MHz**, then $-20\,dB/dec$ | Confirms wide BW | | Square wave input, $f = 10\,kHz$ | Output is a slightly rounded square wave | Limited by $C_{gs}$ slew behaviour | > [!NOTE] > **Pro-Tip for KTU Viva:** Always state that the **rise time / fall time** of the output square wave is governed by the **time constant $\tau = R_{out} \cdot C_L$**, where $C_L$ is the total load capacitance (including the MOSFET's own $C_{gs}$ and any explicit load cap). This is a frequent viva question. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]** ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **"Why is the Common Drain (CD) amplifier also called a Source Follower? Justify with the expression for voltage gain."** **Model Answer:** The CD amplifier is called a source follower because the **source voltage follows the gate voltage** with a small offset $V_{GS}$. From the small-signal gain expression: $$A_v = \frac{g_m (R_S \Vert R_L)}{1 + g_m (R_S \Vert R_L)}$$ For $g_m (R_S \Vert R_L) \gg 1$, $A_v \to 1$, meaning $v_o \approx v_i$ with **zero phase shift**. The output therefore "follows" the input, justifying the name. **[3 Marks]** --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **"Compare the input and output impedances of Common Source (CS) and Common Drain (CD) amplifiers."** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | CS Amplifier | CD Amplifier | |-----------|--------------|--------------| | $R_{in}$ | $R_1 \Vert R_2$ (moderate) | $R_1 \Vert R_2$ (same) | | $R_{out}$ | $R_D$ (moderate to high) | $\approx 1/g_m$ (very low) | The CD stage has a **much lower output impedance** than CS because of the **source-follower negative feedback** action. This makes CD ideal as a **buffer** to drive low-impedance loads. **[3 Marks]** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - Model Question Pattern, 2024 Scheme]** ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Design a Common Drain amplifier using an NMOS transistor with the following specifications: $V_{DD} = 3.3\,V$, $V_{TH} = 0.7\,V$, $k_n' = 200\,\mu A/V^2$, $W/L = 25$, $I_D = 0.5\,mA$, $R_L = 5\,k\Omega$. Compute $V_{OV}$, $V_{GS}$, $R_S$, the bias divider $R_1, R_2$, $g_m$, voltage gain $A_v$, and output resistance $R_{out}$. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** Draw the small-signal equivalent circuit of the designed CD amplifier. From the equivalent circuit, derive the expression for voltage gain $A_v = v_o / v_i$. Show that $A_v \to 1$ as $g_m R_S' \to \infty$. **\[7 Marks\]** #### Model Solution to (a) **Step 1 — Overdrive Voltage:** $$ \begin{aligned} I_D &= \frac{1}{2} k_n' \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}^2 \\ 0.5 \times 10^{-3} &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot 200 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 25 \cdot V_{OV}^2 \\ 0.5 \times 10^{-3} &= 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot V_{OV}^2 \\ V_{OV}^2 &= 0.2 \\ V_{OV} &= 0.447\,V \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Gate-to-Source Voltage:** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{GS} = V_{TH} + V_{OV} = 0.7 + 0.447 = 1.147\,V \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Choose $V_S$ and Compute $R_S$:** Choose $V_S = 1.0\,V$ (a clean value giving $V_{DS} = 2.3\,V$ for good saturation margin): $$ \begin{aligned} R_S = \frac{V_S}{I_D} = \frac{1.0}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.0\,k\Omega \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Gate Voltage and Bias Divider:** $$ \begin{aligned} V_G = V_S + V_{GS} = 1.0 + 1.147 = 2.147\,V \end{aligned} $$ Choose divider current $I_{div} = 0.05\,mA$ (10% of $I_D$): $$ \begin{aligned} R_1 + R_2 &= \frac{3.3}{0.05 \times 10^{-3}} = 66\,k\Omega \\ R_2 &= \frac{V_G}{V_{DD}} (R_1 + R_2) = \frac{2.147}{3.3} \cdot 66 = 42.9\,k\Omega \\ R_1 &= 66 - 42.9 = 23.1\,k\Omega \end{aligned} $$ Use standard values $R_1 = 22\,k\Omega$, $R_2 = 43\,k\Omega$. **Step 5 — Transconductance:** $$ \begin{aligned} g_m = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.447} = 2.236\,mA/V \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Voltage Gain:** $$ \begin{aligned} R_S' &= R_S \Vert R_L = 2.0\,k \Vert 5.0\,k = 1.429\,k\Omega \\ A_v &= \frac{g_m R_S'}{1 + g_m R_S'} = \frac{2.236 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot 1429}{1 + 2.236 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot 1429} \\ &= \frac{3.195}{4.195} = 0.762\,V/V \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Output Resistance:** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{out} \approx \frac{1}{g_m} = \frac{1}{2.236 \times 10^{-3}} = 447\,\Omega \end{aligned} $$ > **Final Answer Tabulation:** | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | $V_{OV}$ | $0.447\,V$ | | $V_{GS}$ | $1.147\,V$ | | $R_S$ | $2.0\,k\Omega$ | | $R_1$ | $22\,k\Omega$ | | $R_2$ | $43\,k\Omega$ | | $g_m$ | $2.24\,mA/V$ | | $A_v$ | $0.762$ | | $R_{out}$ | $447\,\Omega$ | **\[7 Marks\]** #### Model Solution to (b) **Step 1 — Small-signal equivalent:** Replace NMOS with hybrid-$\pi$ model: gate open, current source $g_m v_{gs}$ from drain to source, output resistance $r_o$ from source to ground (AC). Drain is at AC ground. Input $v_i$ applied at gate, output $v_o$ taken at source. **Step 2 — Identify $v_{gs}$:** Since source is not grounded, $v_{gs} = v_i - v_o$. **Step 3 — KCL at Source Node:** $$ \begin{aligned} g_m v_{gs} &= \frac{v_o}{R_S'} \\ g_m (v_i - v_o) &= \frac{v_o}{R_S'} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Solve for $A_v$:** $$ \begin{aligned} g_m v_i &= v_o \left( g_m + \frac{1}{R_S'} \right) \\ A_v = \frac{v_o}{v_i} &= \frac{g_m R_S'}{1 + g_m R_S'} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Limit:** $$ \begin{aligned} \lim_{g_m R_S' \to \infty} A_v &= \lim_{g_m R_S' \to \infty} \frac{g_m R_S'}{1 + g_m R_S'} = 1 \end{aligned} $$ Hence, the output follows the input with **unity gain** in the ideal high-$g_m R_S'$ limit. **\[7 Marks\]** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** For the Common Drain amplifier designed in Question A, derive the expression for **output resistance $R_{out}$** using the look-back technique (test source method). Clearly state the assumptions made. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** A CD amplifier has $g_m = 5\,mA/V$, $R_S = 1\,k\Omega$, $R_L = 4\,k\Omega$, and is driven by a source resistance $R_{sig} = 10\,k\Omega$. Compute the **overall voltage gain $v_o / v_{sig}$** (including source loading). Also calculate the **-3dB bandwidth** if $C_{gs} = 5\,pF$ and $C_{gd} = 1\,pF$. **\[7 Marks\]** #### Model Solution to (a) **Step 1 — Deactivate the input:** Set $v_i = 0$ (short the input AC source to ground). **Step 2 — Apply test voltage $v_x$ at output node (source).** Compute the test current $i_x$ drawn from $v_x$. **Step 3 — Identify $v_{gs}$:** $$ \begin{aligned} v_{gs} = v_i - v_x = 0 - v_x = -v_x \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — KCL at the source node:** Sum currents leaving $v_x$: $$ \begin{aligned} i_x &= \frac{v_x}{R_S} - g_m v_x + \frac{v_x}{r_o} \\ i_x &= v_x \left( \frac{1}{R_S} - g_m + \frac{1}{r_o} \right) \end{aligned} $$ > **Assumptions:** (i) Body effect neglected ($g_{mb} = 0$), (ii) $r_o$ is finite, (iii) input is ideally deactivated. **Step 5 — Output Resistance:** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{out} = \frac{v_x}{i_x} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{R_S} - g_m + \dfrac{1}{r_o}} = R_S \Vert r_o \Vert \frac{1}{g_m} \approx \frac{1}{g_m} \end{aligned} $$ The negative sign from $-g_m v_x$ ensures the denominator is *reduced* by $g_m$, making $R_{out}$ **very small**. **\[7 Marks\]** #### Model Solution to (b) **Step 1 — Effective source and load:** $$ \begin{aligned} R_S' &= R_S \Vert R_L = 1\,k \Vert 4\,k = 0.8\,k\Omega \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Gain from gate to output:** $$ \begin{aligned} A_v = \frac{g_m R_S'}{1 + g_m R_S'} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 800}{1 + 5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 800} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Input resistance of stage** (with $R_1 = R_2 \to \infty$ ideal, or assuming bootstrap, $R_{in} \to \infty$). Hence no loading on $R_{sig}$. **Step 4 — Overall gain (input is at gate, so no divider on input):** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{v_o}{v_{sig}} = A_v = 0.8 \,V/V \quad (\text{or } -1.94\,dB) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Bandwidth:** For a CD stage, the dominant pole is at the source node, set by the total capacitance looking into the source. Using the **Miller approximation** with $A_v \approx 1$: $$ \begin{aligned} C_{in,tot} &\approx C_{gs} + (1 - A_v) C_{gd} \\ &\approx 5\,pF + (1 - 0.8) \cdot 1\,pF \\ &= 5\,pF + 0.2\,pF = 5.2\,pF \end{aligned} $$ The Thevenin resistance seen by this capacitance is $R_{out} \Vert R_S'$: $$ \begin{aligned} R_{th} = R_{out} \Vert R_S' = \frac{1}{g_m} \Vert R_S' = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-3}} \Vert 800 = 200 \Vert 800 = 160\,\Omega \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_{in,tot}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 160 \cdot 5.2 \times 10^{-12}} = 191\,MHz \end{aligned} $$ This confirms the well-known fact that **CD stages have very high bandwidth** because the Miller effect is suppressed by the near-unity gain. **\[7 Marks\]** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cause heavy mark deduction in KTU ESE:** > 1. **Forgetting the saturation check:** Students compute $V_{OV}$ but fail to verify $V_{DS} \ge V_{OV}$. Always state this explicitly. > 2. **Confusing CS and CD configurations:** In CS, output is at the drain; in CD, output is at the **source**. A wrong terminal changes gain from $g_m R_D$ to $g_m R_S / (1 + g_m R_S)$. > 3. **Ignoring body effect:** If the source is *not* tied to the substrate (as in a CD stage driving an external load), include $g_{mb}$ in the gain formula. Forgetting this costs 1–2 marks. > 4. **Sign error in $R_{out}$ derivation:** The test-source method has a **negative** sign on $g_m v_x$. Writing $+ g_m v_x$ is a frequent error. > 5. **Using peak vs RMS:** $A_v$ in dB is $20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$. Always specify whether you mean magnitude or V/V. > 6. **Wrong units of $g_m$:** If you use $k_n'$ in $\mu A/V^2$ and $I_D$ in $mA$, $g_m$ comes out in $mA/V$. Converting to $A/V$ is essential. > 7. **Neglecting to state assumptions:** Examiners reward clarity. Always state: (i) $r_o \to \infty$ or finite, (ii) body effect considered or neglected, (iii) coupling capacitors ideal. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Common Drain Amplifier** - ☐ CD = **Source Follower**; output is at source, drain is at AC ground. - ☐ Voltage gain: $A_v = g_m (R_S \Vert R_L) / [1 + g_m (R_S \Vert R_L)]$; always **less than 1** and **non-inverting**. - ☐ Ideal limit: $A_v \to 1$ when $g_m (R_S \Vert R_L) \to \infty$. - ☐ Input resistance: $R_{in} = R_1 \Vert R_2$ (set by bias network only). - ☐ Output resistance: $R_{out} \approx 1/g_m$ (very low — this is the main advantage). - ☐ Transconductance: $g_m = 2 I_D / V_{OV} = \sqrt{2 k_n' (W/L) I_D}$. - ☐ Overdrive voltage: $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$; typical design choice **0.2 to 0.5 V**. - ☐ Always check **saturation condition**: $V_{DS} = V_{DD} - V_S \ge V_{OV}$. - ☐ Body effect reduces $A_v$ slightly and lowers $R_{out}$ further; use $g_{mb} = \chi g_m$. - ☐ Bandwidth is **much higher than CS** because Miller effect is suppressed ($C_{gd}(1-A_v) \approx 0$). - ☐ Primary application: **voltage buffer / impedance transformer**; also used as **level shifter**. - ☐ In SPICE: use **`.op`** for DC, **`.ac dec 100 1 100Meg`** for frequency response, **`.tran 10m`** for transient. - ☐ In KTU lab record, always include: (i) hand calculation, (ii) SPICE schematic, (iii) DC operating point table, (iv) AC magnitude plot, (v) **percentage error** between theory and simulation. > **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Common Drain Amplifier Design & AC Analysis**

topics.title: Design a Common Gate Amplifier for a Specified Current Gain and Perform AC Analysis
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVL607 - ANALOG IC DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design a Common Gate Amplifier for a Specified Current Gain and Perform AC Analysis

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Common Gate (CG) Amplifier** is a single-stage MOSFET amplifier configuration in which the **Gate terminal is tied to AC ground** (held at a constant DC bias), the **input signal is applied at the Source terminal**, and the **output is taken from the Drain terminal**. In the context of CMOS analog IC design, the CG stage is fundamentally a **current buffer** — it provides a current gain of approximately unity ($A_i \approx 1$) while offering a **low input impedance** at the source node and a **high output impedance** at the drain node.

In the KTU 2024 Analog IC Design Lab (PCEVL607), Module Experiment 2 specifically requires the design of a CG amplifier for a **specified current gain** and the subsequent verification of its **AC (small-signal) performance** through simulation tools such as **Cadence Virtuoso, LTSpice, or ngspice**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (CO Alignment):**
> - **CO2:** Design and analyze single-stage MOS amplifiers (CS, CG, CD) for given specifications.
> - **CO3:** Perform AC analysis (gain, bandwidth, input/output impedance) on analog building blocks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water-Flow Current Buffer"

Imagine water flowing through a horizontal pipe with a thin **flexible rubber membrane** in the middle:

| Physical System | CG Amplifier Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Water inflow (volume current) at the left side | **Input current $i_{in}$** at the Source |
| Membrane that allows water to pass but absorbs pressure fluctuations | **MOS transistor channel** controlled by gate bias |
| Water outflow (volume current) on the right side | **Output current $i_{out}$** at the Drain |
| Fixed rigid wall behind the membrane | **DC-biased Gate held at AC ground** |
| Almost equal flow on both sides | **Current gain $A_i \approx 1$** |

**Intuition:** The CG amplifier is like a **hydraulic current buffer** — whatever current you push into the source, an almost equal current comes out at the drain. The "rubber membrane" (channel) is constantly controlled by the rigid back wall (DC gate bias), so the current flow is nearly 1:1. The membrane's stiffness represents the **low input impedance ($1/g_m$)** — it doesn't push back much against incoming water.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Design Insight:** A Common Gate stage is preferred when a circuit requires feeding a low-impedance node from a current source, or when high-frequency operation is needed (since the **Miller effect is absent** at the gate input).

## 1.3 Why CG Amplifier for Specified Current Gain?

In analog IC design, the **current gain** $A_i$ of a CG stage is dominated by the **bias current $I_D$** and the **transistor geometry** $(W/L)$. By carefully sizing $(W/L)$ and setting the bias point, designers can achieve a target current gain very close to unity. The experiment emphasizes:
1. Translating the **current gain specification** into **transistor sizing**.
2. Setting the **DC bias** so that the MOSFET operates in **saturation**.
3. Performing **AC analysis** to verify mid-band gain, bandwidth, and impedances.
4. Understanding the role of **body effect** (since source is not at ground).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Topology and Biasing

A Common Gate amplifier using an **NMOS** transistor is shown conceptually. The DC bias network sets the gate at a reference voltage $V_G$, the source sits at $V_S = V_G - V_{GS}$, and the drain is at $V_D = V_{DD} - I_D R_D$. For saturation:

$$V_{DS} \geq V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$$

The **AC input** $v_{in}$ is injected through a coupling capacitor at the source, and the **AC output** $v_{out}$ is taken at the drain through another coupling capacitor.

## 2.2 Small-Signal Operation — Step by Step

1. **AC Grounding the Gate:** Since the gate is held at a fixed DC potential (via a large bypass capacitor or DC voltage source), it is treated as an **AC short to ground**, i.e., $v_g = 0$.

2. **Input at Source:** An AC voltage $v_{gs}$ develops across the gate–source junction because $v_s = v_{in}$ and $v_g = 0$, hence $v_{gs} = -v_{in}$.

3. **Drain Current Variation:** The small-signal drain current is:
$$i_d = g_m v_{gs} + \frac{v_{ds}}{r_o}$$

4. **Current Continuity (KCL at Drain):** The AC drain current $i_d$ flows entirely through the load resistor $R_D$ (neglecting the output of the next stage for isolated analysis), producing:
$$v_{out} = -i_d R_D$$

5. **Current Gain Logic:** The **input current** $i_{in}$ is the current entering the source terminal. By KCL at the source node, $i_{in} = -i_d$ (the current that flows into the source equals the current leaving the drain, for a MOS device with no gate current). Hence:
$$A_i = \frac{i_{out}}{i_{in}} = \frac{-i_d}{-i_d} \approx 1$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why "approximately 1"?** Channel-length modulation ($\lambda \neq 0$) and body effect ($g_{mb} \neq 0$) cause a small deviation. The exact current gain is:
> $$A_i = \frac{g_m + g_{mb}}{g_m + g_{mb} + \frac{1}{r_o}} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \frac{R_D}{r_o}}$$
> which is slightly less than unity.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Condition / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Overdrive Voltage | $V_{OV}$ | $V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ | Must be $> 0$ for saturation |
| 2 | Drain Current (saturation) | $I_D$ | $\frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}V_{OV}^2$ | Long-channel square law |
| 3 | Drain Current (alternate) | $I_D$ | $\frac{1}{2}g_m V_{OV}$ | Useful identity |
| 4 | Transconductance | $g_m$ | $\sqrt{2\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}I_D}$ | $g_m \propto \sqrt{I_D}$ |
| 5 | Transconductance (alt) | $g_m$ | $\frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}}$ | $g_m \propto I_D / V_{OV}$ |
| 6 | Output Resistance | $r_o$ | $\frac{1}{\lambda I_D}$ | $\lambda$ = channel length modulation |
| 7 | Intrinsic Voltage Gain | $A_{v0}$ | $g_m r_o$ | Mid-band, no load |
| 8 | Voltage Gain (loaded) | $A_v$ | $g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$ | Mid-band, with $R_D$ |
| 9 | Input Resistance (at source) | $R_{in}$ | $\frac{1}{g_m + g_{mb}} \parallel \frac{1}{g_m} \approx \frac{1}{g_m}$ | **Low impedance** (key feature) |
| 10 | Output Resistance (at drain) | $R_{out}$ | $R_D \parallel r_o$ | **High impedance** |
| 11 | Current Gain | $A_i$ | $\approx 1 - \varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon$ depends on $\lambda$ and $g_{mb}$ |
| 12 | Body-effect Transconductance | $g_{mb}$ | $\chi g_m$, where $\chi = \frac{\gamma}{2\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}}$ | $0 < \chi < 0.3$ typically |
| 13 | Unity-Gain Bandwidth (approx.) | $\omega_T$ | $\frac{g_m}{C_{gs} + C_{gd}}$ | CG has **no Miller multiplication** of $C_{gd}$ |
| 14 | Pole Frequency (dominant) | $\omega_p$ | $\frac{1}{R_{out} C_L}$ | $C_L$ = load capacitance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** In CG amplifiers, the **Miller effect does not multiply $C_{gd}$** because the gain from gate to drain is approximately zero (gate is at AC ground). This is the primary reason CG is used in **high-frequency RF front-ends**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Common Gate amplifier is widely used in:

- **RF Low-Noise Amplifiers (LNAs):** As the input matching stage, because its low input impedance ($1/g_m$) can be easily matched to 50 Ω antenna sources.
- **Current Mirrors (Cascode Loads):** In cascode amplifiers and current mirrors, the CG transistor provides high output impedance, boosting the gain.
- **Differential Pair Tail:** In differential amplifiers, a CG configuration can sense a current directly.
- **High-Speed ADCs and Sample-and-Hold Circuits:** Due to wide bandwidth.
- **Current Conveyors (CCII):** The + and – current terminals follow CG and CC principles.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Small-Signal Voltage Gain $A_v$

**Step 1 — Apply AC input at the source:** With gate at AC ground, $v_{gs} = -v_{in}$.

**Step 2 — Write KCL at the drain node (assuming load $R_D$ to AC ground):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_d &= g_m v_{gs} + \frac{v_{ds}}{r_o} \\
i_d &= -g_m v_{in} + \frac{v_{out}}{r_o}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply KCL at drain (current through $R_D$ equals drain current):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{0 - v_{out}}{R_D} &= i_d \\
-\frac{v_{out}}{R_D} &= -g_m v_{in} + \frac{v_{out}}{r_o}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Collect $v_{out}$ terms on one side:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\frac{v_{out}}{R_D} - \frac{v_{out}}{r_o} &= -g_m v_{in} \\
v_{out}\left(\frac{1}{R_D} + \frac{1}{r_o}\right) &= g_m v_{in}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $A_v = v_{out}/v_{in}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_v &= \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = g_m \left(R_D \parallel r_o\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows that the **mid-band voltage gain is positive** (non-inverting) because the source is the input. This is a key distinguishing feature from the Common Source (CS) amplifier.

## 3.2 Derivation of Current Gain $A_i$

**Step 1 — Define input current:** The current entering the source terminal from the signal source with source resistance $R_S$:

$$
i_{in} = \frac{v_{in} - v_s}{R_S} = \frac{v_{in} - v_{in}}{R_S} \quad \text{(if source is ideal)}
$$

For an **ideal voltage source** at the input, all of the source current flows into the transistor. For current gain analysis, we treat the CG as a **two-port network**.

**Step 2 — Apply the standard current gain expression for a CG stage:**

$$
A_i = \frac{i_{out}}{i_{in}} = \frac{-i_d}{i_s}
$$

**Step 3 — KCL at source node:** The current entering the source is:

$$
i_s = -g_m v_{gs} - g_{mb} v_{bs} - \frac{v_{ds}}{r_o}
$$

**Step 4 — For an ideal current source input (infinite input impedance), $v_{bs} = v_{gs}$, and the body is tied to source in some designs (eliminating $g_{mb}$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_s &= -(g_m + g_{mb})v_{gs} - \frac{v_{ds}}{r_o} \\
i_d &= g_m v_{gs} + \frac{v_{ds}}{r_o}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Express the current gain (assuming output port shorted for short-circuit current gain):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{i,sc} &= \frac{i_d}{i_s} = \frac{g_m v_{gs}}{-(g_m + g_{mb})v_{gs}} = \frac{-g_m}{g_m + g_{mb}}
\end{aligned}
$$

If body effect is negligible ($g_{mb} \approx 0$):

$$
A_i \approx -1 \approx 1 \text{ (in magnitude)}
$$

The **negative sign** simply indicates the direction of current flow (from source to drain); in magnitude, $|A_i| \approx 1$.

## 3.3 Derivation of Input Resistance $R_{in}$ at the Source

**Step 1 — Test the source node by injecting a test current $i_x$ at $v_x$:**

$$
i_x = -i_d = -\left(g_m v_{gs} + \frac{v_{ds}}{r_o}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Note that $v_{gs} = -v_x$ and $v_{ds} = -v_x$ (since gate and drain are at AC ground in the test):**

$$
i_x = -g_m(-v_x) - \frac{-v_x}{r_o} = g_m v_x + \frac{v_x}{r_o}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the input resistance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{in} &= \frac{v_x}{i_x} = \frac{1}{g_m + \frac{1}{r_o}} \\
&\approx \frac{1}{g_m} \quad \text{(since } r_o \gg 1/g_m \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**This confirms the low input impedance characteristic of the CG amplifier.**

## 3.4 Design Procedure — From Current Gain Spec to Transistor Sizing

Given a target **mid-band current gain** $A_i \approx 1$ and a **voltage gain** $A_v$, with a chosen bias current $I_D$:

**Step 1 — Choose the bias current $I_D$** based on power, speed, and noise constraints.

**Step 2 — Determine the overdrive voltage $V_{OV}$** from the design budget (e.g., $V_{OV} = 0.2$ V for low-voltage design).

**Step 3 — Calculate the transconductance:**

$$
g_m = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}}
$$

**Step 4 — Size the transistor $(W/L)$:**

$$
\frac{W}{L} = \frac{g_m^2}{2 \mu_n C_{ox} I_D}
$$

For a given technology (e.g., 180 nm, 90 nm, with $\mu_n C_{ox} \approx 300 \mu\text{A/V}^2$ for 180 nm NMOS), compute $W/L$.

**Step 5 — Choose $R_D$ for the desired voltage gain:**

$$
R_D = \frac{A_v}{g_m}
$$

**Step 6 — Set bias voltages** to keep the transistor in saturation:

$$
V_{DS,min} = V_{OV} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{DD} - I_D R_D \geq V_S + V_{OV}
$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Specification:**
- $A_i \approx 1$ (mid-band)
- $A_v = 5$ (V/V)
- $I_D = 1$ mA
- $V_{OV} = 0.2$ V
- $\mu_n C_{ox} = 300 \mu$A/V² (180 nm NMOS)
- $\lambda = 0.1$ V⁻¹

**Solution:**

**Step A — Compute $g_m$:**

$$
g_m = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}} = \frac{2 \times 1\text{ mA}}{0.2\text{ V}} = 10\text{ mA/V} = 0.01\text{ S}
$$

**Step B — Compute $W/L$:**

$$
\frac{W}{L} = \frac{g_m^2}{2 \mu_n C_{ox} I_D} = \frac{(0.01)^2}{2 \times 300 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{10^{-4}}{6 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 166.7
$$

If $L = 0.18 \mu$m, then $W \approx 30 \mu$m.

**Step C — Compute $r_o$:**

$$
r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.1 \times 10^{-3}} = 10\text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step D — Compute $R_D$ for $A_v = 5$:**

$$
R_D = \frac{A_v}{g_m} = \frac{5}{0.01} = 500\ \Omega
$$

**Step E — Verify saturation:** $V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_D R_D = 1.8 - 0.5 = 1.3$ V. Assuming $V_S = 0.6$ V, $V_{DS} - V_S = 0.7$ V, which is well above $V_{OV} = 0.2$ V. ✓

**Step F — Input and Output Resistances:**

$$
R_{in} \approx \frac{1}{g_m} = 100\ \Omega \quad ; \quad R_{out} = R_D \parallel r_o = 500 \parallel 10000 \approx 476\ \Omega
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Common Gate Amplifier — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **signal flow** and **bias arrangement** of a CG amplifier as a two-port network.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSrc[VSIG Source Vin]:::inputStyle
    nodeCB1[Coupling Cap Cin]:::capStyle
    nodeSrcNode[Source Node Vs]:::nodeStyle
    nodeM1[NMOS M1]:::deviceStyle
    nodeGate[Gate DC Bias VGB]:::biasStyle
    nodeCbGate[Bypass Cap Cb]:::capStyle
    nodeDrn[Drain Node Vd]:::nodeStyle
    nodeCB2[Coupling Cap Cout]:::capStyle
    nodeRD[Load Resistor RD to VDD]:::resStyle
    nodeVdd[VDD Supply]:::supplyStyle
    nodeOut[Vout Output]:::outputStyle

    nodeSrc --> nodeCB1 --> nodeSrcNode
    nodeSrcNode --> nodeM1
    nodeGate --> nodeCbGate --> nodeSrcNode
    nodeM1 --> nodeDrn
    nodeVdd --> nodeRD --> nodeDrn
    nodeDrn --> nodeCB2 --> nodeOut

    classDef inputStyle fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef outputStyle fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef capStyle fill:#5a3a1f,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef nodeStyle fill:#3a3a3a,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef deviceStyle fill:#1f5a3a,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef biasStyle fill:#5a1f5a,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef resStyle fill:#5a1f1f,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef supplyStyle fill:#1f1f5a,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
```

**Description of the flow:**
- AC input **Vin** is coupled through **Cin** to the **Source** of the NMOS.
- The **Gate** is biased at a constant DC potential and bypassed to AC ground by **Cb**.
- The transistor **M1** acts as a voltage-controlled current source, conducting $i_d = g_m v_{gs}$.
- The drain current develops a voltage across **RD**, producing **Vout**, which is coupled through **Cout** to the load.

## 4.2 Small-Signal Equivalent Circuit Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeVin[Vin from Test Source]:::inputStyle
    nodeRin[Input Resistance 1 OVER gm]:::resStyle
    nodeGmvcc[VCCS gm Vgs]:::deviceStyle
    nodeRo[Output Resistance ro]:::resStyle
    nodeRd[Load RD]:::resStyle
    nodeVout[Vout Node]:::nodeStyle
    nodeIout[Output Current iout]:::outputStyle

    nodeVin --> nodeRin --> nodeVout
    nodeVout --> nodeRo
    nodeVout --> nodeRd
    nodeGmvcc --> nodeVout
    nodeVout --> nodeIout

    classDef inputStyle fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef outputStyle fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff

    classDef deviceStyle fill:#1f5a3a,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef nodeStyle fill:#3a3a3a,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
```

```mermaid

    step4 -->|Not in Saturation| step12[Adjust VGS and RD and Repeat]:::warningStyle
```
