## 4.4 Frequency Response Visualization (Conceptual)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Conceptual CG Amplifier Frequency Response"
    x-axis "Frequency log f Hz" [1 10 100 1000 10000 100000 1000000 10000000 100000000 1000000000]
    y-axis "Gain Magnitude dB" 0 5 10 15 20 25 30
    line [20 20 20 20 20 19 18 15 10 5]
```

The plot shows a **flat mid-band region** at $20\log_{10}(A_v) \approx 20$ dB, with the gain rolling off beyond the dominant pole due to parasitic and load capacitances. The CG stage typically exhibits a **wider bandwidth** than its CS counterpart because the Miller effect is absent at the gate.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** 
*Why is the input resistance of a Common Gate amplifier low, and what is its typical expression?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The input resistance is low because the AC input is applied at the **Source** terminal while the Gate is at AC ground. A test voltage $v_x$ applied at the source produces a current $i_x = (g_m + 1/r_o)v_x$ flowing into the transistor, making the input resistance:

$$
R_{in} = \frac{1}{g_m + \frac{1}{r_o}} \approx \frac{1}{g_m}
$$

This low input impedance (typically 50–500 Ω) is the **defining feature** of the CG stage and makes it suitable as a current buffer and RF input matching stage. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Mention any two advantages of a Common Gate amplifier over a Common Source amplifier in high-frequency applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Absence of Miller Effect:** In the CG stage, the gate is at AC ground, so the gate–drain capacitance $C_{gd}$ does not undergo Miller multiplication. This gives a **much higher bandwidth** than the CS stage. **(1.5 marks)**

2. **Better Input Matching:** The low input impedance ($1/g_m$) can be directly matched to 50 Ω sources, eliminating the need for matching networks in RF LNAs. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, with internal choice between Question A and Question B. The expected structure is two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **small-signal voltage gain** of a Common Gate amplifier, clearly stating assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a Common Gate amplifier using an NMOS transistor for the following specifications:
- Mid-band current gain $A_i \approx 1$
- Voltage gain $A_v = 4$ V/V
- Bias current $I_D = 0.5$ mA
- $\mu_n C_{ox} = 200 \mu$A/V²
- $V_{OV} = 0.25$ V
- $\lambda = 0.05$ V⁻¹

Compute the transistor sizing $(W/L)$, the load resistance $R_D$, the input resistance, and the output resistance. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

Assume: (i) Gate is at AC ground, (ii) Body is tied to source (no body effect initially), (iii) Output is taken at drain with load $R_D$.

Apply KCL at the drain node. With $v_{gs} = -v_{in}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_d &= g_m v_{gs} + \frac{v_{ds}}{r_o} \\
\frac{0 - v_{out}}{R_D} &= i_d \\
-\frac{v_{out}}{R_D} &= -g_m v_{in} + \frac{v_{out}}{r_o}
\end{aligned}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
v_{out}\left(\frac{1}{R_D} + \frac{1}{r_o}\right) = g_m v_{in}
$$

$$
\boxed{A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)} \quad \text{(7 marks — derivation + boxed result)}
$$

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Compute $g_m$ (1 mark):**

$$
g_m = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}} = \frac{2 \times 0.5\text{ mA}}{0.25\text{ V}} = 4\text{ mA/V}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $W/L$ (2 marks):**

$$
\frac{W}{L} = \frac{g_m^2}{2 \mu_n C_{ox} I_D} = \frac{(4 \times 10^{-3})^2}{2 \times 200 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{16 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-7}} = 80
$$

**Step 3: Compute $R_D$ (1 mark):**

$$
R_D = \frac{A_v}{g_m} = \frac{4}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = 1\text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step 4: Compute $r_o$ (1 mark):**

$$
r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.05 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 40\text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step 5: Compute $R_{in}$ and $R_{out}$ (2 marks):**

$$
R_{in} \approx \frac{1}{g_m} = 250\ \Omega \quad ; \quad R_{out} = R_D \parallel r_o = 1\text{ k} \parallel 40\text{ k} \approx 976\ \Omega
$$

**Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| $g_m$ | 4 mA/V |
| $W/L$ | 80 |
| $R_D$ | 1 kΩ |
| $r_o$ | 40 kΩ |
| $R_{in}$ | 250 Ω |
| $R_{out}$ | 976 Ω |
| $A_i$ | $\approx 1$ |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the small-signal model of a Common Gate amplifier including the body-effect transconductance $g_{mb}$. Derive the **short-circuit current gain** $A_{i,sc}$ and show that it deviates from unity due to $g_{mb}$ and $\lambda$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a CG amplifier with $g_m = 5$ mA/V, $g_{mb} = 1$ mA/V, $r_o = 20$ kΩ, $R_D = 2$ kΩ, compute:
(i) The voltage gain $A_v$
(ii) The input resistance $R_{in}$
(iii) The output resistance $R_{out}$  
(iv) The current gain $A_i$ (magnitude). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

The small-signal model of the CG stage (with body effect) includes the dependent current source $g_m v_{gs}$, an additional body-effect source $g_{mb} v_{bs}$ (since source is not at ground), and $r_o$ in parallel. The KCL at the drain:

$$
i_d = g_m v_{gs} + g_{mb} v_{bs} + \frac{v_{ds}}{r_o}
$$

With the output port shorted ($v_{ds} = 0$) and $v_{bs} = v_{gs}$ (since bulk is grounded, source = $-v_{gs}$):

$$
A_{i,sc} = \frac{i_d}{i_s} = \frac{(g_m + g_{mb}) v_{gs}}{-(g_m + g_{mb}) v_{gs}} = -1
$$

When $r_o$ is finite and output is not shorted, the current gain becomes:

$$
|A_i| = \frac{g_m}{g_m + g_{mb} + \frac{1}{r_o} + \frac{g_m R_D}{r_o}} \cdot \frac{r_o + R_D}{r_o}
$$

This expression is **less than 1**, with the deviation growing as $R_D$ increases, $g_{mb}$ increases, or $r_o$ decreases. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Voltage Gain (2 marks):**

$$
A_v = g_m (R_D \parallel r_o) = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times (2\text{ k} \parallel 20\text{ k}) = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1818 \approx 9.09\ \text{V/V}
$$

**(ii) Input Resistance (2 marks):**

$$
R_{in} = \frac{1}{g_m + g_{mb} + \frac{1}{r_o}} = \frac{1}{0.005 + 0.001 + \frac{1}{20000}} = \frac{1}{0.00605} \approx 165.3\ \Omega
$$

**(iii) Output Resistance (1 mark):**

$$
R_{out} = R_D \parallel r_o = 2\text{ k} \parallel 20\text{ k} = 1818\ \Omega
$$

**(iv) Current Gain (2 marks):**

$$
|A_i| = \frac{g_m}{g_m + g_{mb} + \frac{1}{r_o}} = \frac{0.005}{0.00605} \approx 0.826
$$

**Summary:** $A_v \approx 9.09$, $R_{in} \approx 165\ \Omega$, $R_{out} \approx 1.82$ kΩ, $|A_i| \approx 0.83$ (showing the deviation from unity due to body effect).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the sign of $v_{gs}$:** In the CG amplifier, $v_{gs} = -v_{in}$, not $+v_{in}$. Students often miss the negative sign, leading to a wrong sign on the voltage gain. **(Lose 2 marks)**
> 
> 2. **Ignoring body effect ($g_{mb}$):** When source is not at ground, $g_{mb}$ must be included in the small-signal model. Omitting it gives incorrect $R_{in}$ and current gain. **(Lose 2–3 marks)**
> 
> 3. **Confusing CG with CD (Source Follower):** The Common Drain has high input resistance; the Common Gate has low input resistance. Misnaming the topology is a **fundamental error** that costs heavily. **(Lose 3–4 marks)**
> 
> 4. **Wrong units in $W/L$ calculation:** $C_{ox}$ is in F/m² (or $\mu$F/cm²), currents in A, lengths in m. Mixing units is a common mistake. **(Lose 1–2 marks)**
> 
> 5. **Not verifying saturation:** A CG stage that leaves the saturation region is invalid. Always state $V_{DS,sat} = V_{OV}$ and verify. **(Lose 1 mark)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **CG Amplifier** = Input at Source, Output at Drain, Gate at AC ground.
- ✅ **Current gain** $A_i \approx 1$ — it is fundamentally a **current buffer**.
- ✅ **Input resistance** $R_{in} \approx 1/g_m$ — **low**, ideal for current-mode inputs.
- ✅ **Output resistance** $R_{out} = R_D \parallel r_o$ — **high**.
- ✅ **Voltage gain** $A_v = g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$ — **non-inverting** (positive sign).
- ✅ **Body effect** is **important** in CG because source is not at bulk potential; always include $g_{mb}$ for accurate $R_{in}$.
- ✅ **No Miller multiplication** of $C_{gd}$ → **wide bandwidth** in high-frequency designs.
- ✅ **Design flow:** $I_D \to V_{OV} \to g_m \to (W/L) \to R_D \to$ verify saturation.
- ✅ **AC analysis** in Cadence/LTSpice: apply small-signal AC source, sweep 1 Hz – 10 GHz, plot $|V_{out}/V_{in}|$ in dB, extract mid-band gain and 3 dB bandwidth.
- ✅ **Used in:** RF LNAs, cascode gain stages, current mirrors, high-speed analog front-ends, sample-and-hold circuits.
- ✅ **KTU Exam Rule of Thumb:** A CG design problem will usually specify $A_i$ (close to 1) and $A_v$ (a small integer like 2, 4, 5). The student must derive $W/L$, $R_D$, and impedances in a clean, step-by-step format.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design a RC Coupled Amplifier Using MOSFET and Perform AC Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVL607 - ANALOG IC DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design a RC Coupled Amplifier Using MOSFET and Perform AC Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **RC Coupled Amplifier Using MOSFET** is a multistage (or single-stage) voltage amplifier in which a **Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor (MOSFET)** is biased in the saturation region and coupled to the source/load through **resistor-capacitor (RC) networks**. The coupling capacitors (C_C) block the DC bias from propagating between stages, while the resistors (R_G, R_D, R_S) establish the DC operating point (Q-point). The AC analysis determines the small-signal voltage gain, input/output impedance, and bandwidth of the amplifier.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** This experiment tests the student's ability to (i) design the DC biasing network, (ii) compute small-signal parameters, (iii) derive the mid-band AC gain, and (iv) predict the lower and upper cutoff frequencies using **short-circuit time-constant (SCTC)** or **open-circuit time-constant (OCTC)** techniques.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Megaphone" Analogy

Imagine a **small voice speaking into a megaphone**:

- The **MOSFET** acts as the megaphone's amplifying element — a small electrical whisper (input signal v_gs) at its gate controls a much larger flow of current from drain to source.
- The **DC biasing resistors (R_G1, R_G2, R_S, R_D)** are like the "idle setting" of the megaphone — they set up a steady current through the device so that even a tiny input signal has room to swing both ways without distortion.
- The **coupling capacitors (C_C1, C_C2)** are like **one-way glass windows**: they let the AC voice (signal) pass through to the next stage, but block the DC "battery setting" of one stage from interfering with the next.
- The **bypass capacitor (C_S)** is like a **shortcut wire around a speed bump** (R_S): for AC signals it short-circuits the source resistor, preventing AC negative feedback and preserving high gain.
- The **AC analysis** is simply asking: *"How much louder does the voice get, and what is the frequency range over which it works well?"*

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Symbol | Parameter | Typical Range / Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **V_DD** | Supply Voltage | **5 V – 15 V** |
| **I_D** | Drain Current | **0.5 mA – 5 mA** |
| **V_GS** | Gate-to-Source Voltage | **0.7 V – 3 V** (for enhancement NMOS) |
| **g_m** | Transconductance | **1 mA/V – 30 mA/V** |
| **r_o** | Output Resistance of MOSFET | **10 kΩ – 100 kΩ** |
| **A_v** | Mid-band Voltage Gain | **−5 to −50 V/V** (negative = phase inversion) |
| **f_L** | Lower Cutoff Frequency | **20 Hz – 100 Hz** |
| **f_H** | Upper Cutoff Frequency | **100 kHz – 1 MHz** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** The negative sign in A_v indicates a **180° phase inversion** between input and output — a hallmark of the common-source (CS) configuration. Students must retain this sign explicitly in derivations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Topology and Operating Principle

The RC-coupled MOSFET amplifier is implemented in the **Common Source (CS) configuration**. The general structure comprises:

1. **Input coupling capacitor (C_C1):** Connects the source v_in to the gate; blocks DC from source.
2. **Biasing network (R_G1, R_G2):** Voltage divider that sets the DC gate voltage V_G.
3. **MOSFET (M1):** The active amplifying device biased in **saturation region**.
4. **Drain resistor (R_D):** Converts drain current variations into output voltage.
5. **Source resistor (R_S) with bypass capacitor (C_S):** Provides DC stability and AC gain boost.
6. **Output coupling capacitor (C_C2):** Blocks DC from reaching the load R_L.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

- **Step 1 — DC Biasing:** R_G1 and R_G2 form a voltage divider. Since the MOSFET gate draws no DC current, the gate voltage is:
$$V_G = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{R_{G2}}{R_{G1} + R_{G2}}$$

- **Step 2 — Source Voltage:** The source voltage V_S = I_D · R_S, and V_GS = V_G − V_S is automatically established to produce the desired I_D.

- **Step 3 — Saturation Condition:** For NMOS in saturation, V_DS ≥ V_GS − V_TH, ensuring constant-current behavior.

- **Step 4 — AC Coupling:** When v_in is applied, C_C1 passes the AC component, superimposing it on the DC bias at the gate. The gate-source voltage becomes v_gs(t) = v_in(t) (assuming no attenuation).

- **Step 5 — Small-Signal Amplification:** The small change v_gs modulates the drain current, which flows through R_D, producing a voltage v_d = −g_m · v_gs · R_D (inverted and amplified).

- **Step 6 — Output Delivery:** C_C2 blocks the DC drain voltage, passing only the amplified AC signal to the load R_L.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Design Choices

- **Why R_G1 || R_G2 in parallel for AC?** The gate draws no current, but the AC equivalent circuit sees both resistors in parallel to ground. The **input resistance** is the parallel combination: R_in = R_G1 || R_G2.
- **Why a bypass capacitor C_S?** Without C_S, the source resistor would introduce AC negative feedback (source degeneration), reducing the gain by a factor of (1 + g_m · R_S). C_S short-circuits R_S at mid-band frequencies, restoring maximum gain.
- **Why CS configuration is preferred?** It provides **high voltage gain**, **moderate input impedance**, and natural **phase inversion** — making it ideal as a basic amplifier building block.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Boundary Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Drain Current (Sat.) | $I_D = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2$ | A | $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS}-V_{TH}$ |
| 2 | Drain Current (using k_n') | $I_D = \dfrac{k_n'}{2} \cdot \dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2$ | A | where $k_n' = \mu_n C_{ox}$ |
| 3 | Transconductance | $g_m = \dfrac{2 I_D}{V_{GS}-V_{TH}} = \sqrt{2 k_n' \dfrac{W}{L} I_D}$ | A/V or S | Saturation region |
| 4 | Body-effect transconductance | $g_{mb} = \chi \cdot g_m$ | A/V | $\chi \approx 0.1 - 0.3$ |
| 5 | Output Resistance | $r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \dfrac{V_A}{I_D}$ | Ω | $\lambda$ = channel-length modulation |
| 6 | Mid-band Voltage Gain (no R_L) | $A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$ | V/V | Ideal CS, with C_S shorted |
| 7 | Mid-band Voltage Gain (with R_L) | $A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel R_L \parallel r_o)$ | V/V | When load R_L connected |
| 8 | Source-degenerated Gain | $A_v = -\dfrac{g_m R_D'}{1 + g_m R_S}$ | V/V | Without bypass C_S |
| 9 | Input Resistance | $R_{in} = R_{G1} \parallel R_{G2}$ | Ω | MOSFET gate = open circuit |
| 10 | Output Resistance | $R_{out} = R_D \parallel r_o$ | Ω | Looking into drain |
| 11 | f_L from C_C1 | $f_{L1} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_{C1}}$ | Hz | R_S = source resistance |
| 12 | f_L from C_C2 | $f_{L2} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_{out} + R_L) C_{C2}}$ | Hz | C_C2 cutoff |
| 13 | f_L from C_S | $f_{LS} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_S \parallel \frac{1}{g_m}) C_S}$ | Hz | Bypass capacitor pole |
| 14 | Overall Lower Cutoff | $f_L \approx \sqrt{f_{L1}^2 + f_{L2}^2 + f_{LS}^2}$ | Hz | Approximate summation |
| 15 | Upper Cutoff (Miller) | $f_H \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_M}$ | Hz | Miller approximation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** RC-coupled MOSFET amplifiers form the **front-end gain stage** in virtually every analog integrated circuit — op-amps, audio codecs, RF receivers, sensor readout ASICs, and biomedical instrumentation amplifiers. The exact same topology appears in 90% of CMOS op-amp input stages (e.g., the classic 741-style op-amp evolved into CMOS equivalents using this very CS configuration).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Design Procedure (Worked Numerical Example)

**Design Specification (Typical KTU Lab Problem):**

> Design a single-stage RC-coupled CS amplifier using an **NMOS transistor** with the following requirements: V_DD = **12 V**, I_D = **1 mA**, V_GS = **2 V**, V_TH = **0.8 V**, k_n'(W/L) = **1 mA/V²**, λ = **0.01 V⁻¹**, C_in = **5 pF**, desired f_L = **100 Hz**, R_L = **10 kΩ**.

### Step 1 — Verify Saturation Current

$$I_D = \frac{k_n'}{2} \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$$

$$I_D = \frac{1 \text{ mA/V}^2}{2} \cdot (2 - 0.8)^2 = 0.5 \cdot 1.44 = 0.72 \text{ mA}$$

**Interpretation:** The given k_n'(W/L) gives only 0.72 mA. To achieve I_D = 1 mA, we adjust (W/L).

### Step 2 — Recalculate Required (W/L) for I_D = 1 mA

$$\frac{W}{L} = \frac{2 I_D}{k_n'(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2} = \frac{2 \times 1 \text{ mA}}{1 \text{ mA/V}^2 \times (1.2)^2} = \frac{2}{1.44} = 1.389$$

$$\boxed{\frac{W}{L} \approx 1.4}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Small-Signal Transconductance g_m

$$g_m = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{GS} - V_{TH}} = \frac{2 \times 1 \text{ mA}}{1.2 \text{ V}} = 1.667 \text{ mA/V}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Output Resistance r_o

$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.01 \times 1 \text{ mA}} = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$$

### Step 5 — Design R_S for Stability (Choose V_S = 2 V)

$$R_S = \frac{V_S}{I_D} = \frac{2 \text{ V}}{1 \text{ mA}} = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$$

### Step 6 — Compute Gate Voltage V_G

$$V_G = V_{GS} + V_S = 2 + 2 = 4 \text{ V}$$

### Step 7 — Design Voltage Divider R_G1, R_G2

Choose total gate resistance R_G1 + R_G2 = 1 MΩ (high input impedance). Then:

$$V_G = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{R_{G2}}{R_{G1} + R_{G2}}$$

$$4 = 12 \cdot \frac{R_{G2}}{10^6}$$

$$R_{G2} = \frac{4 \times 10^6}{12} = 333.33 \text{ k}\Omega \approx 330 \text{ k}\Omega \text{ (standard)}$$

$$R_{G1} = 1 \text{ M}\Omega - 333 \text{ k}\Omega = 667 \text{ k}\Omega \approx 680 \text{ k}\Omega \text{ (standard)}$$

### Step 8 — Design R_D for Proper V_DS and Gain

Choose V_DS = 6 V (mid-rail for maximum swing). Then V_D = V_S + V_DS = 2 + 6 = 8 V.

$$R_D = \frac{V_{DD} - V_D}{I_D} = \frac{12 - 8}{1 \text{ mA}} = 4 \text{ k}\Omega$$

### Step 9 — Compute Mid-band Voltage Gain A_v

$$A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel R_L \parallel r_o)$$

$$R_D \parallel R_L \parallel r_o = 4\text{k} \parallel 10\text{k} \parallel 100\text{k}$$

$$= \frac{1}{\frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{10} + \frac{1}{100}} \text{ k}\Omega = \frac{1}{0.25 + 0.1 + 0.01} = \frac{1}{0.36} = 2.778 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$A_v = -1.667 \text{ mA/V} \times 2.778 \text{ k}\Omega = -4.63 \text{ V/V}$$

$$\boxed{A_v \approx -4.63 \text{ V/V} \;\; (\text{or } 13.32 \text{ dB})}$$

### Step 10 — Compute Input and Output Resistance

$$R_{in} = R_{G1} \parallel R_{G2} = 680\text{k} \parallel 330\text{k} = \frac{680 \times 330}{680 + 330} = 222 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_{out} = R_D \parallel r_o = 4\text{k} \parallel 100\text{k} = \frac{400}{104} = 3.85 \text{ k}\Omega$$

### Step 11 — Design Coupling Capacitor C_C1 for f_L1 = 100 Hz

Assume source resistance R_sig = 600 Ω. Then:

$$f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_{sig} + R_{in}) C_{C1}}$$

$$C_{C1} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times (600 + 222000) \times 100} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 222600 \times 100}$$

$$C_{C1} = \frac{1}{1.3986 \times 10^{8}} = 7.15 \text{ nF} \approx 10 \text{ nF (standard)}$$

### Step 12 — Design C_C2 for f_L2 = 100 Hz

$$C_{C2} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_{out} + R_L) f_{L2}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times (3850 + 10000) \times 100}$$

$$C_{C2} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 13850 \times 100} = 1.149 \times 10^{-7} \text{ F} \approx 0.12 \text{ μF} \rightarrow 0.1 \text{ μF (standard)}$$

### Step 13 — Design Bypass Capacitor C_S for f_LS = 100 Hz

$$C_S = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S \parallel \frac{1}{g_m}) f_{LS}}$$

$$\frac{1}{g_m} = \frac{1}{1.667 \text{ mA/V}} = 600 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$R_S \parallel \frac{1}{g_m} = 2000 \parallel 600 = \frac{2000 \times 600}{2600} = 461.5 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$C_S = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 461.5 \times 100} = 3.45 \text{ μF} \rightarrow 4.7 \text{ μF (standard)}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Frequency Response Summary

| Frequency Band | Capacitor State | Gain Expression | Dominant Pole |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **f < f_L (Low)** | C_C1, C_C2, C_S open | $\vert A_v \vert$ drops at −20 dB/dec | C_S pole (typically highest f_L) |
| **f_L < f < f_H (Mid-band)** | All capacitors → short | $A_v = -g_m(R_D \parallel R_L \parallel r_o)$ | Constant gain |
| **f > f_H (High)** | Internal C_GS, C_GD, C_DB matter | $\vert A_v \vert$ drops at −20 dB/dec | Miller effect dominates |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick:** Students often forget that **f_LS (from C_S) is usually the highest of the three lower-cutoff poles** because R_S || (1/g_m) is small. This makes C_S's pole the dominant low-frequency pole.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete Circuit Schematic (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the full RC-coupled MOSFET amplifier, including signal flow and the role of each component:

    rg2["BIAS RESISTOR R_G2\n330 kohm to GND"]:::bias

    rd["DRAIN RESISTOR R_D\n4 kohm to V_DD"]:::load
    rs["SOURCE RESISTOR R_S\n2 kohm to GND"]:::bias

    out["OUTPUT NODE v_out\nAmplified AC signal"]:::node

    cc1 -->|AC coupled| gate

    rg2 --- gate

    rs --- cs
    rd --> cc2
    cc2 --> load
    load --> out
    mosfet:::mos

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define the role of the bypass capacitor C_S in a CS MOSFET amplifier. What happens to the gain if C_S is removed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The bypass capacitor **C_S** is connected in parallel with the source resistor **R_S**. Its role is to **short-circuit R_S at AC (signal) frequencies**, eliminating AC negative feedback (source degeneration). With C_S in place, the full small-signal transconductance g_m is utilized and the voltage gain becomes $A_v = -g_m(R_D \parallel R_L \parallel r_o)$ — the **maximum achievable gain**. If C_S is removed, R_S introduces source degeneration and the gain reduces to $A_v = -\frac{g_m(R_D \parallel R_L \parallel r_o)}{1 + g_m R_S}$, significantly attenuating the output signal. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Why is a coupling capacitor used between stages in an RC coupled amplifier?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A coupling capacitor is used to **block the DC bias voltage of one stage from affecting the DC operating point of the next stage**, while allowing the **AC signal to pass through unimpeded**. This is essential because each amplifier stage has its own Q-point, and direct (DC) connection would shift the bias of subsequent stages, causing distortion or saturation. The capacitor must be chosen large enough so that its reactance $X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}$ is negligible at the lowest signal frequency, ensuring minimum signal attenuation. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Design a single-stage RC-coupled CS MOSFET amplifier with the following specifications: V_DD = 15 V, I_D = 2 mA, V_GS = 2.5 V, V_TH = 1 V, k_n'(W/L) = 2 mA/V², λ = 0.02 V⁻¹, R_L = 5 kΩ, and lower cutoff frequency f_L = 50 Hz. Perform complete DC design, AC analysis, and compute the values of all coupling and bypass capacitors. Assume source resistance R_sig = 1 kΩ.**

#### **Part (a) — DC Design & Small-Signal Parameters [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Verify given I_D and compute transconductance g_m**
$$g_m = k_n' \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_{TH}) = 2 \text{ mA/V}^2 \times (2.5 - 1) = 3 \text{ mA/V}$$

$$I_D = \frac{g_m}{2}(V_{GS}-V_{TH}) = \frac{3}{2} \times 1.5 = 2.25 \text{ mA}$$

Since the computed I_D ≈ 2.25 mA is close to 2 mA, we adjust (W/L) slightly:
$$\frac{W}{L}\bigg|_{adj} = \frac{2 I_D}{k_n'(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2} = \frac{2 \times 2}{2 \times 2.25} = 0.889$$

For simplicity, use W/L = 0.89 and corresponding g_m = 2.67 mA/V.

**Step 2: Output resistance r_o**
$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.02 \times 2 \text{ mA}} = 25 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 3: Choose V_S = 2.5 V (50% of V_GS for stability)**
$$R_S = \frac{V_S}{I_D} = \frac{2.5}{2 \text{ mA}} = 1.25 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4: Compute V_G**
$$V_G = V_{GS} + V_S = 2.5 + 2.5 = 5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5: Design R_G1 and R_G2 (choose R_G1 + R_G2 = 2 MΩ for high R_in)**
$$R_{G2} = \frac{V_G}{V_{DD}} \times (R_{G1}+R_{G2}) = \frac{5}{15} \times 2\text{M} = 666.7 \text{ k}\Omega \approx 680 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_{G1} = 2 \text{ M}\Omega - 666.7 \text{ k}\Omega = 1.33 \text{ M}\Omega \approx 1.3 \text{ M}\Omega$$

**Step 6: Choose V_DS = 5 V (good swing), compute R_D**
$$V_D = V_S + V_{DS} = 2.5 + 5 = 7.5 \text{ V}$$

$$R_D = \frac{V_{DD} - V_D}{I_D} = \frac{15 - 7.5}{2 \text{ mA}} = 3.75 \text{ k}\Omega \approx 3.9 \text{ k}\Omega$$

#### **Part (b) — AC Analysis & Capacitor Design [7 marks]**

**Step 7: Mid-band voltage gain**
$$A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel R_L \parallel r_o)$$

$$R_D \parallel R_L \parallel r_o = 3.75\text{k} \parallel 5\text{k} \parallel 25\text{k} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{3.75}+\frac{1}{5}+\frac{1}{25}} = \frac{1}{0.5067} = 1.974 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$A_v = -2.67 \text{ mA/V} \times 1.974 \text{ k}\Omega = -5.27 \text{ V/V}$$

**Step 8: Input and Output Resistance**
$$R_{in} = R_{G1} \parallel R_{G2} = 1.3\text{M} \parallel 680\text{k} = \frac{1.3 \times 0.68}{1.98} \text{ M}\Omega = 446.5 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_{out} = R_D \parallel r_o = 3.75\text{k} \parallel 25\text{k} = 3.26 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 9: Coupling Capacitor C_C1**
$$C_{C1} = \frac{1}{2\pi f_L (R_{sig} + R_{in})} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times (1000 + 446500)}$$

$$C_{C1} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times 447500} = 7.11 \text{ nF} \rightarrow 10 \text{ nF (standard)}$$

**Step 10: Coupling Capacitor C_C2**
$$C_{C2} = \frac{1}{2\pi f_L (R_{out} + R_L)} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times (3260 + 5000)} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times 8260}$$

$$C_{C2} = 385.4 \text{ nF} \rightarrow 0.47 \text{ μF (standard)}$$

**Step 11: Bypass Capacitor C_S**
$$R_S \parallel \frac{1}{g_m} = 1250 \parallel \frac{1}{2.67 \text{ mA/V}} = 1250 \parallel 374.5 = \frac{1250 \times 374.5}{1624.5} = 288.2 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$C_S = \frac{1}{2\pi f_L \times 288.2} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times 288.2} = 5.52 \text{ μF} \rightarrow 10 \text{ μF (standard)}$$

**Final Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| W/L | 0.89 |
| R_G1 | 1.3 MΩ |
| R_G2 | 680 kΩ |
| R_S | 1.25 kΩ |
| R_D | 3.9 kΩ |
| g_m | 2.67 mA/V |
| A_v | −5.27 V/V |
| C_C1 | 10 nF |
| C_C2 | 0.47 μF |
| C_S | 10 μF |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**For a CS MOSFET amplifier with I_D = 1.5 mA, V_GS = 2 V, V_TH = 0.8 V, μ_n C_ox = 50 μA/V², W = 10 μm, L = 2 μm, λ = 0.015 V⁻¹, R_D = 5 kΩ, R_L = 10 kΩ, and R_S = 1 kΩ bypassed by C_S:**

#### **Part (a) — Compute g_m, r_o, A_v, R_in, R_out [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Compute k_n'(W/L)**
$$k_n' \cdot \frac{W}{L} = 50 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2 \times \frac{10}{2} = 250 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2 = 0.25 \text{ mA/V}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute g_m**
$$g_m = k_n' \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_{TH}) = 0.25 \times (2 - 0.8) = 0.3 \text{ mA/V}$$

**Step 3: Compute r_o**
$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.015 \times 1.5 \text{ mA}} = 44.4 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4: Compute A_v (with C_S bypassing R_S)**
$$A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel R_L \parallel r_o)$$

$$R_D \parallel R_L \parallel r_o = 5\text{k} \parallel 10\text{k} \parallel 44.4\text{k} = \frac{1}{0.2 + 0.1 + 0.0225} = 3.10 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$A_v = -0.3 \text{ mA/V} \times 3.10 \text{ k}\Omega = -0.93 \text{ V/V}$$

**Step 5: R_in and R_out** (Assuming R_G1 || R_G2 = 1 MΩ)
$$R_{in} = 1 \text{ M}\Omega, \quad R_{out} = R_D \parallel r_o = 5\text{k} \parallel 44.4\text{k} = 4.5 \text{ k}\Omega$$

#### **Part (b) — Compare Gain With and Without C_S Bypass [7 marks]**

**With C_S (bypassed):**
$$A_{v,\text{byp}} = -0.93 \text{ V/V}$$

**Without C_S (R_S = 1 kΩ active):**
$$A_{v,\text{unbyp}} = -\frac{g_m (R_D \parallel R_L \parallel r_o)}{1 + g_m R_S} = -\frac{0.3 \times 3.10}{1 + 0.3 \times 1} = -\frac{0.93}{1.3} = -0.715 \text{ V/V}$$

**Reduction in gain:**
$$\Delta A_v = \frac{0.93 - 0.715}{0.93} \times 100\% = 23.1\%$$

**Conclusion:** Removing C_S reduces the gain by ~23%, demonstrating the critical role of the bypass capacitor in achieving maximum amplification. However, removing C_S improves linearity and bandwidth, which is a common design trade-off.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in A_v** — KTU examiners explicitly test for the 180° phase inversion. Lose **1 mark** if omitted.
> 2. **Using V_GS − V_TH as V_DS** — A common confusion. The condition is V_DS ≥ V_GS − V_TH, not equality. Always verify with the saturation inequality.
> 3. **Not including r_o in parallel with R_D** — Many students compute A_v = −g_m · R_D only, ignoring r_o. KTU 2024 marking scheme deducts **1–2 marks** for this.
> 4. **Wrong coupling capacitor formula** — Students often use C = 1/(2πfL·R_in) only, forgetting to add R_sig or R_L. The correct formulas include the **Thevenin source/load resistance**.
> 5. **Body effect omission** — In real IC design, g_mb (body transconductance) reduces the gain via back-gate effect. While not always required, mentioning it shows depth.
> 6. **C_S pole identification** — Students often ignore that C_S's effective resistance is **R_S || (1/g_m)**, not just R_S. This is a **favourite KTU question** worth 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — RC Coupled MOSFET Amplifier:**

- ✅ **Configuration:** Common Source (CS) is the standard; provides voltage gain with phase inversion.
- ✅ **DC Biasing:** Voltage divider R_G1, R_G2 sets V_G; R_S provides stability; MOSFET gate draws no current.
- ✅ **Saturation Condition:** $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ must hold; otherwise transistor enters triode region.
- ✅ **Transconductance:** $g_m = k_n'(W/L)(V_{GS}-V_{TH}) = \sqrt{2 k_n'(W/L) I_D}$ — **two equivalent forms**.
- ✅ **Mid-band Gain:** $A_v = -g_m(R_D \parallel R_L \parallel r_o)$ — **always include r_o** for accuracy.
- ✅ **Bypass Capacitor C_S:** Short-circuits R_S at AC; without it, gain drops by factor $(1 + g_m R_S)$.
- ✅ **Coupling Capacitors:** C_C1, C_C2 block DC and pass AC; size them for desired f_L.
- ✅ **Dominant Lower Pole:** Usually the **C_S pole** because R_S || (1/g_m) is small.
- ✅ **Miller Effect:** C_GD appears as $(1 - A_v)C_{GD}$ at input, limiting f_H.
- ✅ **Input Impedance:** $R_{in} = R_{G1} \parallel R_{G2}$ — typically hundreds of kΩ to MΩ.
- ✅ **Output Impedance:** $R_{out} = R_D \parallel r_o$ — typically a few kΩ.
- ✅ **Phase Inversion:** Output is 180° out of phase with input — **essential CS property**.
- ✅ **Design Iterations:** If W/L is impractical, adjust V_GS or I_D and re-iterate until all specs are met.
- ✅ **SPICE Validation:** Always simulate in **LTspice / Cadence** with .AC analysis to validate hand calculations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** The most common exam question asks you to **design the bias network AND perform the AC analysis** in one 14-mark problem. Practice the integration of these two halves — they are **not independent**, and the AC analysis depends on the DC biasing choices. Always show the saturation verification before proceeding to AC calculations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design a Current Mirror with Passive and Active Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVL607 - ANALOG IC DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design a Current Mirror with Passive and Active Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Current Mirror** is a fundamental analog integrated circuit building block that replicates a reference DC current (I_REF) flowing through one branch of a circuit into one or more output branches with a precise, geometry-defined scaling factor. In the KTU 2024 Analog IC Design Lab context, it is implemented using matched Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistors (MOSFETs) operating in the **saturation region**, exploiting the square-law dependence of drain current on gate-source voltage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** A current mirror with **passive load** uses a *resistor* as the drain load of the mirror transistor, while a current mirror with **active load** uses a *transistor* (typically a current-source biased MOSFET, e.g., a diode-connected or PMOS current source) as the load. Active loads provide *significantly higher* small-signal output impedance and better Power Supply Rejection Ratio (PSRR).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Twin Faucet" Intuition

Imagine two identical water tanks connected at the bottom by a perfectly sealed U-tube. If you pour exactly **1 litre per second** into Tank A, an identical amount (1 L/s) will naturally flow out of Tank B because the hydraulic pressure (analogue of V_GS) is identical. The mirror transistor M2 is the "second tap" — its current is locked to M1's current because their **gate-source voltages are physically tied together** and their threshold voltages match (they are fabricated on the same die, side by side).

To get a *different* flow rate (scaling), you simply change the **pipe diameter** — that is, the **W/L ratio** of the mirror transistor relative to the reference transistor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used:**
> - **Mirror Ratio (M):** $M = I_O / I_{REF} = (W/L)_2 / (W/L)_1$
> - **Process Parameter (μC_ox):** typically **100 μA/V²** for a generic 180 nm / 250 nm CMOS process.
> - **Threshold Voltage (V_TH):** typically **0.5 V to 0.7 V** for NMOS in modern nodes.

### 1.3 Why Two Different Loads?

- **Passive Load (Resistor R):** Cheap, simple, requires no extra biasing. Drawback: the output node impedance is dominated by r_o2 of the mirror transistor, which is *relatively low* (kΩ range), making the current depend strongly on output voltage (poor current sourcing behaviour).
- **Active Load (Transistor):** A MOSFET biased in saturation acts as a near-ideal current source. The small-signal resistance looking into its drain is *very high* (r_O of a MOSFET, often tens to hundreds of kΩ). The output impedance of the mirror becomes the *parallel combination* of two such high resistances, yielding a stiffer, more ideal current source.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

#### **A. The Reference Branch (Diode-Connected Transistor M1)**

- M1 has its gate tied to its drain, forcing it to operate in **saturation** because V_DS = V_GS > V_GS − V_TH is automatically satisfied.
- Applying KVL from V_DD through R into M1:

$$I_{REF} = \frac{V_{DD} - V_{GS1}}{R}$$

- The transistor's square-law equation *simultaneously* dictates the same current:

$$I_{REF} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 (V_{GS1} - V_{TH})^2$$

#### **B. The Mirror Branch (M2)**

- M2 shares the same V_GS as M1 (gates are shorted, sources are common).
- Therefore M2 also operates in saturation, and its drain current is:

$$I_O = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_2 (V_{GS2} - V_{TH})^2$$

- Since V_GS2 = V_GS1 and V_TH2 = V_TH1 (matched devices), the ratio simplifies to:

$$I_O = I_{REF} \cdot \frac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1} = M \cdot I_{REF}$$

#### **C. Passive Load Topology — Output Resistance**

- The output node sees r_o2 of M2 in parallel with the load resistor R_L (if external) or simply r_o2 if the load is the mirror transistor itself.
- Output impedance: r_out = r_o2 = 1/(λ · I_O), where λ is the channel-length modulation coefficient.

#### **D. Active Load Topology — Cascode Effect**

- Replacing R with a diode-connected PMOS (M3) on top of M2 means the small-signal resistance looking into M2's drain is now r_o2 || r_o3.
- If a *cascode* PMOS is used (gate biased separately), r_out ≈ (g_m3 · r_o3) · r_o2, an order of magnitude larger.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Boundary Condition |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | $I_{REF} = \frac{V_{DD} - V_{GS1}}{R}$ | Reference current from KVL | A ; requires $V_{GS1} < V_{DD}$ |
| 2 | $I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$ | MOSFET square-law (saturation) | A ; valid for $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ |
| 3 | $M = \frac{I_O}{I_{REF}} = \frac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1}$ | Mirror ratio | dimensionless |
| 4 | $g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} I_D}$ | Transconductance | A/V |
| 5 | $g_m = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{GS} - V_{TH}} = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}}$ | Alternate g_m form | A/V |
| 6 | $r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{V_A}{I_D}$ | Output resistance | Ω ; V_A = Early voltage |
| 7 | $r_{out,\,passive} = r_{o2} \parallel R_L$ | Output impedance, passive load | Ω |
| 8 | $r_{out,\,active} = r_{o2} \parallel r_{o3}$ | Output impedance, active load (basic) | Ω |
| 9 | $r_{out,\,cascode} \approx (g_{m3} r_{o3}) r_{o2}$ | Cascode output impedance | Ω ; very large |
| 10 | $V_{DS,\,min} = V_{OV}$ | Headroom / compliance voltage | V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Current mirrors are the workhorse of bias networks, differential pair tail currents, active loads in op-amps, and current-steering DACs. Every modern analog IC — op-amps, comparators, voltage references, PLLs — uses dozens to thousands of them.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Design Walk-Through — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU ESE Style):**

> Design an NMOS current mirror with a **passive resistive load** to deliver **I_O = 200 μA** from an **I_REF = 100 μA** source, with V_DD = 3.3 V, V_TH = 0.7 V, μ_n C_ox = 100 μA/V², λ = 0.01 V⁻¹, and process minimum L = 1 μm. Then re-design the **same mirror with an active PMOS current-source load** biased by the same I_REF.

#### **Part A — Passive Load Design**

**Step 1 — Establish the Mirror Ratio (M)**

From the requirement, the ratio of output to reference current is:

$$M = \frac{I_O}{I_{REF}} = \frac{200 \,\mu A}{100 \,\mu A} = 2$$

**Step 2 — Choose an Overdrive Voltage (V_OV = V_GS − V_TH)**

A standard design choice leaves enough headroom. Choose V_OV1 = 0.3 V (i.e., 300 mV overdrive), which gives:

$$V_{GS1} = V_{TH} + V_{OV1} = 0.7 + 0.3 = 1.0 \,V$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Reference Resistor R**

Apply KVL on the reference branch (V_DD → R → M1 → GND):

$$I_{REF} = \frac{V_{DD} - V_{GS1}}{R} \;\Rightarrow\; R = \frac{V_{DD} - V_{GS1}}{I_{REF}}$$

Substitute values:

$$R = \frac{3.3 - 1.0}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{2.3}{10^{-4}} = 23 \,k\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Size M1 (Determine (W/L)_1)**

Using the square-law equation solved for W/L:

$$\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 = \frac{2 I_{REF}}{\mu_n C_{ox} (V_{GS1} - V_{TH})^2} = \frac{2 I_{REF}}{\mu_n C_{ox} V_{OV1}^2}$$

Substitute:

$$\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 = \frac{2 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}{100 \times 10^{-6} \times (0.3)^2} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{9 \times 10^{-6}} = 22.22$$

Choose L = 1 μm, then W = 22.22 × 1 μm ≈ **22.2 μm**. In practice, round to W = 22 μm or 24 μm for layout convenience.

**Step 5 — Size M2 (Determine (W/L)_2)**

For a 2:1 mirror ratio:

$$\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_2 = M \times \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 = 2 \times 22.22 = 44.44$$

Choose L = 1 μm, then W = **44.4 μm** (round to 44 μm).

**Step 6 — Compute Small-Signal Parameters**

Transconductance of M1:

$$g_{m1} = \frac{2 I_{REF}}{V_{OV1}} = \frac{2 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}{0.3} = 666.7 \,\mu A/V = 0.667 \,mA/V$$

Transconductance of M2 (proportional to sqrt of W/L, so sqrt(2)× larger):

$$g_{m2} = g_{m1} \sqrt{M} = 0.667 \times \sqrt{2} = 0.943 \,mA/V$$

Output resistance of M2 (channel-length modulation):

$$r_{o2} = \frac{1}{\lambda I_O} = \frac{1}{0.01 \times 200 \times 10^{-6}} = 500 \,k\Omega$$

**Step 7 — Compliance Voltage (Headroom)**

The minimum V_DS2 for M2 to remain in saturation:

$$V_{DS2,\,min} = V_{GS2} - V_{TH} = V_{OV1} = 0.3 \,V$$

So the output node can swing between 0.3 V and V_DD before mirroring accuracy degrades.

#### **Part B — Active Load Design (PMOS Current Source on Top)**

**Step 8 — Design the PMOS Active Load (M3)**

The PMOS load must source the same I_O = 200 μA (one per branch, or scaled to drive multiple mirrors). We mirror the PMOS reference branch (M3 with M4, M5, ...).

Choose V_SG3 (PMOS overdrive) = 0.4 V. With |V_THP| = 0.7 V:

$$V_{SG3} = \vert V_{THP} \vert + V_{OVP} = 0.7 + 0.4 = 1.1 \,V$$

For a symmetric design, use μ_p C_ox = 50 μA/V² (typical ratio ~ 1:2 to NMOS in standard CMOS).

$$\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_3 = \frac{2 \times 200 \,\mu A}{50 \,\mu A/V^2 \times (0.4)^2} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-4}}{8 \times 10^{-6}} = 50$$

Choose L = 1 μm, W_P = 50 μm.

**Step 9 — Bias Resistor for PMOS Reference**

The PMOS reference branch sits between V_DD and the output node. If we tie its gate to its drain (diode-connected), its V_SG is fixed at 1.1 V, and the current is set intrinsically by the W/L — no resistor needed for current setting, but a **start-up circuit** is required to avoid the degenerate zero-current state.

**Step 10 — Compute the Active-Load Output Impedance**

For the simple diode-connected PMOS load, the small-signal resistance looking into M3's drain is r_o3 (since the diode connection only provides a low-impedance at the gate, not the drain). So:

$$r_{out,\,active} = r_{o2} \parallel r_{o3}$$

Assuming matched λ for both:

$$r_{o3} = \frac{1}{\lambda I_O} = \frac{1}{0.01 \times 200 \times 10^{-6}} = 500 \,k\Omega$$

$$r_{out} = 500 \,k\Omega \parallel 500 \,k\Omega = 250 \,k\Omega$$

Compare this to a **cascode** active load, where the output impedance can approach:

$$r_{out,\,cascode} \approx g_{m3} r_{o3}^2 = 0.943\,m \times (500\,k)^2 \approx 235.75 \,G\Omega \quad (\text{idealised, before paralleling})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Insight:** The factor of improvement from passive to active is roughly **2× for simple active load** and **100× to 1000× for cascode active load** in r_out. Examiners love to ask you to *quantify* this difference.

### 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix — Passive vs Active Load

| Performance Metric | Passive Load (R) | Active Load (MOSFET) | Cascode Active Load |
|--------------------|------------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Output Impedance r_out | $r_{o2}$ (≈ 500 kΩ) | $r_{o2} \parallel r_{o3}$ (≈ 250 kΩ) | $\approx g_{m3} r_{o3} r_{o2}$ (tens of MΩ) |
| Power Dissipation | Higher (I²R loss in R) | Lower | Lowest |
| Current Accuracy vs V_DS | Poor (V_DS dependent) | Good | Excellent |
| Headroom (V_min) | $V_{OV}$ | $2 V_{OV}$ (stacked) | $2 V_{OV}$ |
| Layout Area | Large (R in poly/high-res) | Small (MOS only) | Moderate |
| Design Complexity | Low | Medium | High |
| PSRR | Poor | Better | Best |
| Suitable for | Biasing, simple loads | Op-amp diff-pair tail, gain stages | High-gain op-amp output stages |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Passive Load Current Mirror

The schematic below maps the functional flow of the reference current as it is "copied" into the mirror branch using a passive resistor load.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[VDD Supply] --> B[Reference Resistor R]
    B --> C[Drain and Gate of M1 NMOS Diode Connected]
    C --> D[Source of M1 to Ground]
    C --> E[Gate of M2 NMOS Mirror]
    E --> F[Drain of M2 Output Node]
    F --> G[Load Resistor RL Passive]
    G --> H[VDD]
    F --> I[IO Mirrored Current to Load]
    D --> J[Ground]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Active Load Current Mirror

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[VDD Supply] --> B[PMOS M3 Diode Connected Reference]
    B --> C[Drain of M3 to Node X]
    C --> D[Gate of M4 PMOS Mirror]
    D --> E[Drain of M4 Output Node Y]
    E --> F[Gate of M2 NMOS Driver]
    F --> G[Source of M2 to Ground]
    E --> H[IO Mirrored Current to External Load]
    C --> I[IREF Reference In]
    G --> J[Ground]
    A --> B
    B --> D
```

### 4.3 Signal-Flow Block Architecture — Mirror Operation Phases

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Phase 1: Establish IREF through R] --> P2[Phase 2: Diode connected M1 sets VGS1]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3: Common VGS applied to M2 gate]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4: M2 sources IO equals M times IREF]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5: Output node biased by active load M4]
    P5 --> P6[Phase 6: Current delivered to external load with high output impedance]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why block diagrams and not transistor schematics?** Mermaid is optimised for flowcharts, not electronic schematics. The block-level architecture above conveys the *signal and current flow paths* clearly, which is what KTU lab viva questions typically test. For the actual SPICE schematic, students should use **Cadence Virtuoso** or **LTspice** to draw the canonical 5-transistor topology during the lab session.

### 4.4 Pin Connections Reference (For Lab Wiring)

- **M1 (Reference NMOS):** Gate = Drain = Node REF_TOP; Source = GND; Bulk = GND
- **M2 (Mirror NMOS):** Gate = Node REF_TOP; Drain = Node OUT; Source = GND; Bulk = GND
- **M3 (PMOS Reference):** Gate = Drain = Node PBIAS; Source = VDD; Bulk = VDD
- **M4 (PMOS Active Load):** Gate = Node PBIAS; Drain = Node OUT; Source = VDD; Bulk = VDD

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define a current mirror. Why is it called a "mirror" of current and not of voltage?**

**Model Answer:**
A current mirror is a circuit that copies a reference DC current from one branch to another with a precise scaling factor determined by the geometric ratio of matched transistors. It is called a *mirror* because the output current is a scaled *image* of the input reference current, analogous to an optical mirror producing a faithful replica.

It mirrors current (not voltage) because the **gate-source voltage V_GS is the controlling variable** that is identical in both transistors, but the resulting *drain current* — which is the desired output quantity — is what gets replicated. Voltage mirroring would be impossible because V_DS of the output device is set by the load, not by the mirror.

(3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State two advantages and one disadvantage of using an active load over a passive resistive load in a current mirror.**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. **Higher output impedance:** An active load (MOSFET in saturation) provides output impedance of order r_o, far higher than a resistor at the same bias point. This yields a stiffer, more ideal current source.
2. **Lower voltage headroom and better PSRR:** The active load consumes less DC drop than a large resistor, allowing more signal swing at the output, and rejects power supply noise better.

**Disadvantage:**
1. **Reduced output voltage swing / compliance:** The stacked MOSFET requires an additional V_DS,sat of headroom, raising the minimum output voltage from V_OV to 2·V_OV and limiting low-voltage operation. Active loads also need a start-up circuit to avoid the zero-current dead state.

(3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, With Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the output current I_O of a basic MOSFET current mirror with a **passive resistive load** in terms of I_REF, (W/L)_1, and (W/L)_2. Clearly state the assumption of channel-length modulation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a current mirror designed with V_DD = 5 V, V_TH = 1 V, μ_n C_ox = 50 μA/V², λ = 0.02 V⁻¹, I_REF = 50 μA, and a target I_O = 150 μA, calculate:
  - (i) The required reference resistor R
  - (ii) The aspect ratios (W/L)_1 and (W/L)_2 (assume V_OV = 0.5 V)
  - (iii) The small-signal output resistance r_out seen at the mirror output node. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

For the diode-connected reference transistor M1 in saturation:

$$I_{REF} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 \quad \text{...(1)}$$

For the mirror transistor M2, sharing the same V_GS:

$$I_O = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_2 (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 \quad \text{...(2)}$$

Dividing (2) by (1), and using **channel-length modulation = 0 (λ = 0)** as the simplifying assumption:

$$\frac{I_O}{I_{REF}} = \frac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1} = M$$

$$\therefore \quad I_O = M \cdot I_{REF} = \frac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1} \cdot I_{REF}$$

**Assumption:** Channel-length modulation is neglected (λ = 0), i.e., I_D is independent of V_DS. In practice, λ ≠ 0 introduces an error term:

$$I_O = I_{REF} \cdot \frac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1} \cdot \left(1 + \lambda (V_{DS2} - V_{DS1})\right)$$

This is the systematic mirroring error. **(7 marks — Full marks breakdown: 2 for setup, 2 for division, 2 for assumption statement, 1 for the error-term discussion)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Design (7 marks)**

**(i) Reference Resistor R:**

The overdrive is given as V_OV = V_GS − V_TH = 0.5 V, so V_GS = 1.0 + 0.5 = **1.5 V**.

$$R = \frac{V_{DD} - V_{GS1}}{I_{REF}} = \frac{5 - 1.5}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{3.5}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 70 \,k\Omega$$

**(ii) Aspect Ratios:**

Mirror ratio M = 150/50 = **3**.

$$\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 = \frac{2 I_{REF}}{\mu_n C_{ox} V_{OV}^2} = \frac{2 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{50 \times 10^{-6} \times (0.5)^2} = \frac{10^{-4}}{1.25 \times 10^{-5}} = 8$$

$$\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_2 = 3 \times 8 = 24$$

**(iii) Output Resistance:**

For a passive load, the output node sees only r_o2 of M2:

$$r_{out} = r_{o2} = \frac{1}{\lambda I_O} = \frac{1}{0.02 \times 150 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{3 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 333.3 \,k\Omega$$

**(Total: 7 marks — 2 for (i), 3 for (ii), 2 for (iii))**

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Alternative]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **current mirror using an active PMOS current-source load**. Derive the small-signal output resistance of this configuration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **output impedance, voltage headroom, and PSRR** of:
  - (i) Passive load current mirror
  - (ii) Simple active load current mirror (diode-connected PMOS)
  - (iii) Cascode active load current mirror

Provide a tabular summary and indicate which configuration is most suitable for the **input stage of a low-noise operational amplifier**. Justify your choice. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — Active PMOS Load Current Mirror (7 marks)**

The active-load current mirror replaces the drain resistor of the mirror transistor M2 with a PMOS current source M4. M3 (diode-connected PMOS) is biased by the same I_REF (or a scaled version), and its V_SG is mirrored to M4. Both M1 (NMOS reference) and M2 (NMOS mirror) sit below the PMOS pair.

**Operation:** M1 sets V_GS1 via diode connection; this V_GS is applied to M2, forcing I_O = M·I_REF. M3 sets V_SG3 for the PMOS reference; this V_SG is applied to M4, forcing M4 to source the same current into the output node. The output node is thus a high-impedance point where two current sources (M2 sinking and M4 sourcing) meet, with the difference current flowing to the external load.

**Output Resistance Derivation:**

The small-signal resistance looking up into M4's drain is r_o4, and looking down into M2's drain is r_o2. These appear in parallel at the output node:

$$r_{out,\,active} = r_{o2} \parallel r_{o4} = \frac{r_{o2} \cdot r_{o4}}{r_{o2} + r_{o4}}$$

If r_o2 = r_o4 = r_o (matched), then:

$$r_{out} = \frac{r_o}{2}$$

**For a cascode version (M5 stacked on M4):** the resistance looking into M5's drain is approximately g_m5 · r_o5 · r_o4, so:

$$r_{out,\,cascode} \approx (g_{m5} r_{o5}) r_{o4} \parallel r_{o2} \approx g_m r_o^2$$

**(7 marks — 2 for diagram description, 3 for operation, 2 for derivation)**

---

**Part (b) — Comparative Analysis (7 marks)**

| Configuration | r_out | Headroom V_min | PSRR | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Passive load (R) | $r_{o2}$ (≈ hundreds of kΩ) | $V_{OV}$ (lowest) | Poor | Lowest |
| Simple active load | $r_{o2} \parallel r_{o4}$ (lower) | $2 V_{OV}$ | Good | Medium |
| Cascode active load | $\approx g_m r_o^2$ (tens of MΩ) | $2 V_{OV}$ | Excellent | Highest |

**Suitability for a low-noise op-amp input stage:**

The **cascode active load** is most suitable because:
1. It provides the **highest small-signal gain** (gain ∝ g_m · r_out), essential for low-noise amplification.
2. Its high PSRR rejects power-supply noise, critical for low-noise performance.
3. The high output impedance makes the stage act as a near-ideal current source, which translates to high Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) in differential configurations.

(7 marks — 4 for table + 3 for justification)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Current Mirror Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to compute V_GS first.** Many students jump straight to R = V_DD / I_REF, ignoring the V_GS drop. KTU examiners *will* deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Mixing up M = (W/L)_2 / (W/L)_1 vs its inverse.** The mirror transistor's W/L is on the **numerator**. Use a sanity check: if I_O > I_REF, then M > 1 and (W/L)_2 > (W/L)_1.
> 3. **Neglecting channel-length modulation (λ).** When the question asks for "exact" output current, you MUST include the (1 + λ·ΔV_DS) correction. Without it, you lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the headroom/compliance voltage.** Any current mirror design must specify the minimum V_OUT for which it operates correctly. Examiners frequently add a sub-question: "What is the minimum output voltage for proper mirroring?"
> 5. **Confusing PMOS V_SG with V_GS.** For PMOS, V_SG = V_DD − V_G; the overdrive is V_SG − |V_TH|, not V_GS − V_TH.
> 6. **Sign error in cascode r_out.** The standard approximation r_out ≈ g_m · r_o² applies to a *cascode*, not a *cascode pair*; the parallel combination with the other side must still be taken at the end.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Current mirror = copy of I_REF with scaling factor M = (W/L)_2 / (W/L)_1.**
- **Reference branch has diode-connected transistor** to set V_GS.
- **Mirror branch shares the gate voltage**, forcing identical V_GS.
- **Square-law equation** governs the current in saturation: $I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$.
- **Reference resistor formula:** $R = (V_{DD} - V_{GS}) / I_{REF}$.
- **Passive load r_out = r_o2** (modest).
- **Active load r_out = r_o2 ∥ r_o3** (better).
- **Cascode active load r_out ≈ g_m · r_o²** (best, used in op-amps).
- **Compliance voltage (headroom)** = V_OV for simple mirror, 2·V_OV for active/cascode.
- **Mirror ratio error** due to channel-length modulation: error ≈ λ · (V_DS2 − V_DS1).
- **Start-up circuit is mandatory** for any self-biased current mirror using active loads, to avoid the zero-current degenerate state.
- **For PMOS loads**, all formulas use V_SG and |V_TH|, with source tied to V_DD.
- **Lab Tip:** When simulating in SPICE / LTspice / Cadence, always perform **DC sweep of V_OUT** to extract the *compliance range* and confirm the r_out by computing ΔV_OUT / ΔI_O at the bias point.
- **Lab Tip:** For an *accurate* mirror, use **common-centroid layout** and place dummies to mitigate process gradient and edge effects — this is what makes on-chip mirrors match within 0.1% rather than 5–10%.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design a Cascode Current Mirror with Active and Passive Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVL607 - ANALOG IC DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design a Cascode Current Mirror with Active and Passive Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Cascode Current Mirror** is an advanced current steering circuit built by cascading a common-source (driver) transistor with a common-gate (cascode) transistor. This stacked topology dramatically increases the output impedance of the mirror by a factor of approximately $(g_m \cdot r_o)$ compared to a simple current mirror, while preserving accurate current replication.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (Course Outcome **CO2: Design and analyze analog sub-circuits using CMOS technology**), the cascode mirror is evaluated in **two canonical variants** based on the nature of the load connected to the drain:

| Mirror Type | Load Element | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Passive Load Mirror** | Poly-resistor (R) | Bias generation, simple references |
| **Active Load Mirror** | Current-source transistor (PMOS/NMOS) | Op-amp gain stages, OTAs, ADC/DAC |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A "load" in a current mirror context is the impedance tied to the output node that the mirror must drive. The classification *active vs passive* refers strictly to whether the load is a passive two-terminal device (resistor) or an active controlled source (transistor configured as a current source).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **water distribution system** in a city:
- A **simple current mirror** is like a single valve that copies the input water flow. Any pressure change downstream (analogous to varying $V_{DS}$) alters the output flow — the regulation is poor.
- A **cascode current mirror** adds a *second valve on top* of the first, controlled by a *fixed pressure reference*. The second valve (cascode transistor) absorbs the downstream pressure fluctuations before they can reach the first valve. Result: a very stable flow regardless of downstream pressure.
- A **passive load (resistor)** behaves like a **narrow pipe** — it limits current simply by friction, but wastes energy as heat (voltage drop).
- An **active load (transistor current source)** behaves like a **smart, self-regulating pump** — it only provides the current needed, with minimal energy waste, and offers very high "resistance" to AC signals.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Standard)

The following metrics are central to KTU valuation of this experiment:

- **Output Impedance ($R_{out}$)** — *bold emphasis on output impedance because it is the single most-marked parameter for cascode mirrors in university exams.*
- **Compliance Voltage ($V_{out,min}$)** — minimum $V_{DS}$ required at the output to keep all transistors in saturation.
- **Current Transfer Ratio ($M = I_{out}/I_{ref}$)** — accuracy of replication.
- **Voltage Headroom** — $V_{DD} - V_{out,min}$, defining usable signal swing.
- **Matching Sensitivity** — dependence on $(W/L)$ ratio and $\Delta V_{DS}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** A transistor is in **saturation** when $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{TN} = V_{DS,sat}$. A current mirror transistor must remain in saturation to function as a true current source. Falling into the triode region collapses the high output impedance and invalidates the mirror behaviour.

## 1.4 Why a Cascode? — The Motivation

A simple current mirror's accuracy degrades due to the **channel length modulation** effect:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TN})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$

The factor $(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$ causes $I_{out}$ to drift when $V_{DS}$ of the output transistor differs from that of the reference transistor. The cascode transistor stabilizes the $V_{DS}$ of the inner transistor, effectively *de-sensitizing* the mirror to output voltage variations.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: Reference Branch Generation
A reference current $I_{ref}$ is forced through a diode-connected NMOS (M1). The gate-source voltage developed is:

$$V_{GS1} = V_{TN} + \sqrt{\frac{2 I_{ref}}{K_n' (W/L)_1}}$$

where $K_n' = \mu_n C_{ox}$.

### Step 2: Cascode Transistor Stacking
A cascode transistor M3 is placed *above* M1. Its gate is tied to a fixed bias $V_{BIAS}$ chosen so that M1 operates in saturation:

$$V_{BIAS} = V_{GS1} + V_{GS3} - V_{DS,sat3} \quad \text{(typical design choice)}$$

### Step 3: Output Branch Mirroring
The output branch uses identical transistors (M2, M4). For a 1:1 mirror, $(W/L)_2 = (W/L)_1$ and $(W/L)_4 = (W/L)_3$. For arbitrary ratios, the $W/L$ ratios scale accordingly.

### Step 4: DC Current Transfer
Neglecting channel length modulation:

$$I_{out} = I_{ref} \times \frac{(W/L)_2 (W/L)_4}{(W/L)_1 (W/L)_3}$$

### Step 5: AC (Small-Signal) Output Impedance
The cascode transistor bootstraps the drain of M2, raising the small-signal output impedance to:

$$R_{out} \approx r_{o2} + r_{o4} + g_{m4} r_{o2} r_{o4} \approx g_{m4} r_{o2} r_{o4}$$

This is the celebrated **cascode gain-boost factor**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" in Plain Words:** When output voltage $V_{out}$ tries to change, M4 senses the change at its source, amplifies it by $g_{m4}$, and the high drain resistance of M2 ($r_{o2}$) prevents the resulting current from flowing. Effectively, the cascode transistor converts a voltage disturbance into a current disturbance that gets absorbed by $r_{o2}$, leaving $I_{out}$ nearly unchanged.

## 2.2 Load Variants — Detailed Behaviour

### A. Passive Load (Resistor)
- **Structure:** Output node of mirror drives a resistor $R_L$ tied to $V_{DD}$ (for NMOS mirror) or to ground (for PMOS mirror).
- **Output Voltage:** $V_{out} = V_{DD} - I_{out} R_L$
- **Output Impedance seen by next stage:** $R_{out,total} = R_{out,mirror} \parallel R_L$
- **Drawbacks:** Wastes $I_{out} R_L$ as heat; $V_{out}$ depends on the value of $R_L$.

### B. Active Load (Current Source Transistor)
- **Structure:** A PMOS transistor (M5) is diode-connected or biased with $V_{BIAS,P}$ such that it acts as a current source of value $I_{out}$.
- **Output Impedance seen by next stage:** $R_{out,total} = R_{out,mirror} \parallel r_{o5}$
- **Advantages:** 
  - No DC voltage drop waste (only $V_{DS,sat}$).
  - Can equalize $V_{DS,mirror} = V_{DS,ref}$ for better matching (when biased appropriately).
  - Higher effective gain in amplifier stages.
- **Drawback:** Requires an additional bias voltage, complicating layout.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Memorization for KTU ESE & Lab Viva:** The following equations appear in 80%+ of cascode mirror problems. Use the `$ ... $` for inline references below; absolute values use `\vert` to avoid breaking the table syntax.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reference current (with $R$ load) | $I_{ref} = \dfrac{V_{DD} - V_{GS1}}{R}$ | A; $R$ in $\Omega$ |
| 2 | Reference current (with active load) | $I_{ref} = \dfrac{1}{2} K_n' (W/L)_1 (V_{GS1} - V_{TN})^2$ | A; $K_n' = \mu_n C_{ox}$ |
| 3 | Output current (ideal mirror) | $I_{out} = I_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{(W/L)_{out}}{(W/L)_{ref}}$ | A |
| 4 | Output current (with $\lambda$ effect) | $I_{out} = I_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{(W/L)_{out}}{(W/L)_{ref}} \cdot \dfrac{(1 + \lambda V_{DS,out})}{(1 + \lambda V_{DS,ref})}$ | A |
| 5 | Simple mirror output impedance | $R_{out} = r_o$ | $\Omega$ |
| 6 | Cascode mirror output impedance | $R_{out} \approx g_{m} \cdot r_{o,driver} \cdot r_{o,cascode}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 7 | Compliance voltage (cascode) | $V_{out,min} = 2 V_{DS,sat}$ | V |
| 8 | Compliance voltage (active load stage) | $V_{out,min} = V_{DS,sat,driver} + V_{DS,sat,load}$ | V |
| 9 | Transistor output resistance | $r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 10 | Transconductance | $g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) I_D}$ | A/V |
| 11 | Voltage gain (active load stage) | $A_v = g_{m,driver} \cdot (r_{o,driver} \parallel r_{o,load})$ | V/V |
| 12 | PSRR enhancement (cascode vs simple) | $\text{PSRR}_{cascode} / \text{PSRR}_{simple} \approx g_m r_o$ | ratio |
| 13 | Power dissipation (mirror branch) | $P = V_{DD} \cdot I_{ref}$ | W |
| 14 | Channel length modulation factor | $\lambda \propto 1/L$ | V$^{-1}$ |
| 15 | $V_{DS,sat}$ (saturation voltage) | $V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{TN}$ | V |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Operational Transconductance Amplifiers (OTAs):** The cascode active-load mirror is the core of high-gain differential pair loads in op-amps.
- **Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADCs):** Current mirrors with active loads bias successive-approximation and pipelined ADC comparators.
- **Voltage-Controlled Oscillators (VCOs):** Used as biasing references to set transconductance precisely.
- **Bias Networks in Mixed-Signal ICs:** Generate multiple current ratios from a single $I_{ref}$ (bandgap references, LDO regulators).
- **Biomedical Implants:** Low-voltage cascode mirrors (with sub-1V supply) are vital in pacemakers and hearing aids.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Design Specification (Canonical KTU Problem)

> **Given:**
> - $V_{DD} = 3.3 \text{ V}$, $V_{SS} = 0 \text{ V}$
> - Technology: $0.18\,\mu\text{m}$ CMOS, $K_n' = 200 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $K_p' = 90 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$
> - $V_{TN} = 0.5 \text{ V}$, $\vert V_{TP} \vert = 0.55 \text{ V}$
> - Channel length modulation $\lambda_n = 0.1 \text{ V}^{-1}$, $\lambda_p = 0.15 \text{ V}^{-1}$
> - Desired $I_{ref} = 100 \,\mu\text{A}$, desired $I_{out} = 100 \,\mu\text{A}$ (1:1 mirror)

> **Find:**
> (a) Design the cascode current mirror with a **passive resistor load** (compute $R$, $(W/L)$ for M1 & M2, $V_{BIAS}$).
> (b) Design the cascode current mirror with an **active PMOS load** (compute $(W/L)_{p}$, biasing).

## 3.2 Part (a) — Cascode Mirror with Passive Load

### Step 1 — Choose $V_{DS,sat}$ for M1
A good rule of thumb: $V_{DS,sat} = 0.2$ V to $0.3$ V for low-voltage operation. Choose $V_{DS,sat} = 0.25$ V.

Then $V_{GS1} = V_{TN} + V_{DS,sat} = 0.5 + 0.25 = 0.75$ V.

### Step 2 — Compute $(W/L)$ for M1
Using the saturation current equation:

$$I_{ref} = \frac{1}{2} K_n' \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 (V_{GS1} - V_{TN})^2$$

$$100 \times 10^{-6} = \frac{1}{2} (200 \times 10^{-6}) \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 (0.25)^2$$

$$\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 = \frac{2 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}{200 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.0625}$$

$$\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 = \frac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{12.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 16$$

So $(W/L)_1 = 16$. For 1:1 mirror, $(W/L)_2 = 16$ as well.

### Step 3 — Compute $(W/L)$ for M3, M4
Choose $V_{DS,sat3} = V_{DS,sat4} = 0.25$ V (same operating condition). Then $(W/L)_3 = (W/L)_4 = 16$.

### Step 4 — Compute Reference Resistor $R$

The DC path from $V_{DD}$ to $V_{SS}$ passes through: $V_{DD} \rightarrow R \rightarrow \text{drain of M1} \rightarrow \text{source of M3} \rightarrow \text{source of M1} \rightarrow V_{SS}$.

Voltage equation:

$$V_{DD} = I_{ref} \cdot R + V_{DS1} + V_{DS3}$$

where $V_{DS1} = V_{DS,sat1} = 0.25$ V and $V_{DS3} = V_{DS,sat3} = 0.25$ V (each transistor in saturation at its edge).

$$3.3 = 100 \times 10^{-6} \cdot R + 0.25 + 0.25$$

$$I_{ref} \cdot R = 3.3 - 0.5 = 2.8 \text{ V}$$

$$R = \frac{2.8}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = 28\,000 \,\Omega = 28 \text{ k}\Omega$$

### Step 5 — Design $V_{BIAS}$ for Cascode Gate
M3's gate is tied to $V_{BIAS}$. The drain of M1 (which is M3's source) is at $V_{DS1} = 0.25$ V above $V_{SS}$.

M3's gate voltage must equal $V_{S,M3} + V_{GS3} = 0.25 + 0.75 = 1.0$ V.

So $V_{BIAS} = 1.0$ V.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Insight:** $V_{BIAS}$ is derived from a *cascode bias generator* — typically a scaled copy of the mirror itself (the "cascode bias reference branch") to ensure PVT tracking. In real IC design, this is implemented as a 4th branch identical to M1-M3 with its drain connected to a smaller current.

### Step 6 — Compute Output Impedance $R_{out}$

First, $r_o$ of each transistor:

$$r_{o1} = r_{o2} = r_{o3} = r_{o4} = \frac{1}{\lambda_n I_D} = \frac{1}{0.1 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}} = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Then:

$$R_{out,cascode} = r_{o2} + r_{o4} + g_{m4} r_{o2} r_{o4}$$

Compute $g_{m4}$:

$$g_{m4} = \sqrt{2 K_n' (W/L)_4 I_{out}} = \sqrt{2 \times 200 \times 10^{-6} \times 16 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$g_{m4} = \sqrt{6.4 \times 10^{-7}} = 8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ A/V} = 0.8 \text{ mA/V}$$

Now:

$$R_{out,cascode} = 100\text{k} + 100\text{k} + 0.8 \times 10^{-3} \times 100\text{k} \times 100\text{k}$$

$$R_{out,cascode} = 200 \times 10^3 + 0.8 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{10}$$

$$R_{out,cascode} = 0.2 \times 10^6 + 8 \times 10^6 = 8.2 \text{ M}\Omega$$

Compare to simple mirror: $R_{out,simple} = 100$ k$\Omega$. **Cascode improves $R_{out}$ by $\approx 82\times$** (i.e., $g_m r_o$).

### Step 7 — Compute Compliance Voltage
For cascode mirror:

$$V_{out,min} = V_{DS,sat2} + V_{DS,sat4} = 0.25 + 0.25 = 0.5 \text{ V}$$

The mirror maintains accurate $I_{out}$ only when $V_{out} \geq 0.5$ V. Usable swing: $V_{DD} - V_{out,min} = 3.3 - 0.5 = 2.8$ V.

## 3.3 Part (b) — Cascode Mirror with Active PMOS Load

### Step 1 — Topology
Replace $R$ with a PMOS current source M5 (diode-connected, or with $V_{BIAS,P}$). $I_{out}$ flows from $V_{DD}$ through M5 down through the cascode mirror branch to $V_{SS}$.

### Step 2 — Choose $V_{SG5}$ for PMOS Load
Pick $V_{DS,sat,p} = 0.3$ V. Then $V_{SG5} = \vert V_{TP} \vert + V_{DS,sat,p} = 0.55 + 0.3 = 0.85$ V.

### Step 3 — Compute $(W/L)_5$

$$I_{out} = \frac{1}{2} K_p' (W/L)_5 (V_{SG5} - \vert V_{TP} \vert)^2$$

$$100 \times 10^{-6} = \frac{1}{2} (90 \times 10^{-6}) (W/L)_5 (0.3)^2$$

$$(W/L)_5 = \frac{2 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}{90 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.09} = \frac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{8.1 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 24.7$$

Choose $(W/L)_5 = 25$ (rounded to standard integer).

### Step 4 — Verify Headroom
Total voltage drop from $V_{DD}$ to $V_{SS}$:

$$V_{DD} = V_{SG5} + V_{GS1} + V_{GS3} = 0.85 + 0.75 + 0.75 = 2.35 \text{ V}$$

Slack: $3.3 - 2.35 = 0.95$ V — well within safe operation.

### Step 5 — Output Impedance of Full Stage
The PMOS load adds its own $r_{o5}$ in parallel:

$$r_{o5} = \frac{1}{\lambda_p I_D} = \frac{1}{0.15 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}} = 66.67 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Total output impedance:

$$R_{out,total} = R_{out,cascode} \parallel r_{o5} = 8.2 \text{ M}\Omega \parallel 66.67 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$\frac{1}{R_{out,total}} = \frac{1}{8.2 \times 10^6} + \frac{1}{66.67 \times 10^3}$$

$$\frac{1}{R_{out,total}} \approx 0.122 \times 10^{-6} + 15 \times 10^{-6} \approx 15.12 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$R_{out,total} \approx 66.13 \text{ k}\Omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Insight:** The PMOS load's $r_{o5}$ *limits* the achievable output impedance! In high-gain amplifier design, this is mitigated by using a **cascode PMOS** as well (forming a *telescopic* or *folded-cascode* topology).

### Step 6 — Voltage Gain of Active-Load Stage
If this stage drives a load, the small-signal gain is:

$$A_v = g_{m4} \cdot (r_{o4} \parallel r_{o5}) = 0.8 \text{ mA/V} \times 66.13 \text{ k}\Omega \approx 52.9 \text{ V/V}$$

In dB: $20 \log_{10}(52.9) \approx 34.5$ dB.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Passive Load (R) | Active Load (PMOS) |
|---|---|---|
| $R_{out}$ | $8.2 \text{ M}\Omega$ (limited only by cascode) | $\approx 66 \text{ k}\Omega$ (limited by $r_{o5}$) |
| Voltage headroom | Worse (drops $I_{out}R$) | Better (only $V_{DS,sat}$ drop) |
| Power consumption | Higher ($I^2R$ heat) | Lower (no $I^2R$) |
| DC operating point stability | Sensitive to $R$ mismatch | Insensitive to load value |
| Use case | Reference generation | Amplifier gain stage |
| Gain when driving load | Low (limited by $R \parallel R_{load}$) | High ($A_v = g_m \cdot r_{o,total}$) |
| Area | Moderate (large $R$ for small current) | Moderate (transistor $W \cdot L$) |
| Linearity | Better (resistor is linear) | Worse (transistor is nonlinear) |
| Noise contribution | Thermal noise $\overline{v_n^2} = 4kTR$ | Flicker + thermal (transistor) |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is a cascode current mirror? State two advantages over a simple current mirror.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A cascode current mirror is a current mirror where each branch contains two stacked transistors — a common-source driver and a common-gate cascode — instead of a single transistor. (1 mark)

**Advantages:**
1. **Higher output impedance:** $R_{out} \approx g_m r_{o1} r_{o2}$ compared to $r_o$ for a simple mirror, by a factor of $\sim g_m r_o$. (1 mark)
2. **Better current accuracy / reduced channel length modulation effect:** The cascode transistor stabilizes the $V_{DS}$ of the driver transistor, making $I_{out}$ nearly independent of output voltage. (1 mark)

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Compare passive and active loads in a cascode current mirror with respect to output impedance and voltage headroom.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Passive Load (R) | Active Load (PMOS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Output Impedance** | Limited by $R \parallel R_{out,mirror}$; for large $R$ the mirror dominates. (1 mark) | Limited by $r_{o,PMOS} \parallel R_{out,mirror}$, both contribute. (1 mark) |
| **Voltage Headroom** | Wastes $I_{out} R$ as IR drop; reduces swing. (0.5 mark) | Only $V_{DS,sat}$ drop; better headroom. (0.5 mark) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]*

> **(a)** Design a cascode current mirror for the following specifications: $I_{ref} = 50\,\mu\text{A}$, $V_{DD} = 2.5$ V, $K_n' = 100\,\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $V_{TN} = 0.45$ V, $\lambda_n = 0.08$ V$^{-1}$. Assume all transistors have $L = 1\,\mu\text{m}$ and choose $V_{DS,sat} = 0.25$ V. Compute the $(W/L)$ ratios for M1–M4, the output impedance, and the compliance voltage. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Repeat the design replacing the reference resistor with a PMOS active load. Specify the required PMOS $(W/L)$ and PMOS bias voltage given $K_p' = 50\,\mu\text{A/V}^2$ and $\vert V_{TP} \vert = 0.5$ V. (7 marks)

#### **Solution to A(a) — Passive Load Design (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute $V_{GS1}$.

$$V_{GS1} = V_{TN} + V_{DS,sat} = 0.45 + 0.25 = 0.7 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute $(W/L)_1$ and $(W/L)_2$.

$$I_{ref} = \frac{1}{2} K_n' (W/L)_1 (V_{GS1} - V_{TN})^2$$

$$50 \times 10^{-6} = \frac{1}{2} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times (W/L)_1 \times (0.25)^2$$

$$(W/L)_1 = \frac{2 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{100 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.0625} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{6.25 \times 10^{-6}} = 16$$

For 1:1 mirror, $(W/L)_1 = (W/L)_2 = 16$ and $(W/L)_3 = (W/L)_4 = 16$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute $r_o$.

$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda_n I_D} = \frac{1}{0.08 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}} = 250 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute $g_m$.

$$g_m = \sqrt{2 K_n' (W/L) I_D} = \sqrt{2 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 16 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$g_m = \sqrt{1.6 \times 10^{-7}} = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ A/V} = 0.4 \text{ mA/V}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute $R_{out}$.

$$R_{out} = r_{o2} + r_{o4} + g_{m4} r_{o2} r_{o4}$$

$$R_{out} = 250\text{k} + 250\text{k} + 0.4 \times 10^{-3} \times 250\text{k} \times 250\text{k}$$

$$R_{out} = 500 \times 10^3 + 25 \times 10^6 = 25.5 \text{ M}\Omega$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Compliance voltage.

$$V_{out,min} = 2 V_{DS,sat} = 0.5 \text{ V}$$

#### **Solution to A(b) — Active Load Design (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Choose $V_{DS,sat,PMOS} = 0.3$ V. Then $V_{SG5} = 0.5 + 0.3 = 0.8$ V.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute $(W/L)_5$.

$$I_{ref} = \frac{1}{2} K_p' (W/L)_5 (V_{SG5} - \vert V_{TP} \vert)^2$$

$$50 \times 10^{-6} = \frac{1}{2} \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times (W/L)_5 \times (0.3)^2$$

$$(W/L)_5 = \frac{2 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{50 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.09} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{4.5 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 22.2 \approx 23$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Compute $r_{o5}$ (taking $\lambda_p = 0.12$ V$^{-1}$):

$$r_{o5} = \frac{1}{0.12 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}} = 166.7 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Total output impedance:

$$R_{out,total} = R_{out,cascode} \parallel r_{o5} = 25.5 \text{ M}\Omega \parallel 166.7 \text{ k}\Omega \approx 165.6 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** PMOS gate bias: $V_{BIAS,P} = V_{DD} - V_{SG5} = 2.5 - 0.8 = 1.7$ V (for diode-connected M5).

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper Cycle 1]*

> **(a)** Derive the small-signal output impedance of a cascode current mirror. Show clearly how the bootstrap effect of the cascode transistor increases $R_{out}$ by a factor of $(g_m r_o)$. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** For an NMOS cascode mirror with reference current $I_{ref} = 200\,\mu\text{A}$ and $V_{DD} = 1.8$ V, design a PMOS active load such that the output voltage $V_{out} = 0.9$ V at DC. Use $K_p' = 80\,\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $\vert V_{TP} \vert = 0.45$ V, $\lambda_p = 0.1$ V$^{-1}$. Find $(W/L)$ of PMOS and its gate bias. (7 marks)

#### **Solution to B(a) — Output Impedance Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Apply a test voltage $v_{test}$ at the output node, measure resulting current $i_{test}$, then $R_{out} = v_{test}/i_{test}$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** The cascode transistor M4 is a common-gate stage. Its source is the drain of M2 (driver), drain is the output. The small-signal current through M4's drain equals the sum of (a) current through $r_{o4}$ and (b) the controlled source $g_{m4} v_{gs4}$. Since $v_{gs4} = -v_{ds2}$ (source of M4 moves with drain of M2), and $v_{ds2}$ is set by the current flowing through $r_{o2}$:

$$i_{test} = \frac{v_{test} - v_{ds2}}{r_{o4}} + g_{m4} v_{ds2}$$

But $v_{ds2} = i_{test} \cdot r_{o2}$ (KCL at drain of M2), so:

$$i_{test} = \frac{v_{test} - i_{test} r_{o2}}{r_{o4}} + g_{m4} (i_{test} r_{o2})$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Rearranging:

$$i_{test} r_{o4} = v_{test} - i_{test} r_{o2} + g_{m4} i_{test} r_{o2} r_{o4}$$
